title: TSA: Temporal Slot Activation for Persistent Object-Centric Video Representation
summary: Unsupervised video object-centric learning aims to decompose dynamic scenes into temporally persistent entity representations. Existing recurrent video slot-attention methods propagate a fixed set of slots across frames, but typically assume unconditional slot propagation: every slot is updated and decoded at every frame, regardless of whether its corresponding object is visible. We show that this design violates a basic lifecycle requirement for persistent slots: when an object is absent or fully occluded, its slot should preserve its previous state and avoid explaining unrelated visible content. Instead, unconditional propagation creates two failure pathways: update-induced state drift, where current-frame evidence overwrites the absent object's representation, and decoder-induced reconstruction interference, where the inactive slot remains coupled to reconstruction through decoder attention. We propose Temporal Slot Activation (TSA), a mechanism that learns a per-slot, per-frame activation score $α_{k,t} \in (0, 1)$ without visibility supervision. TSA uses this activation as a shared latent control variable for slot lifecycle modeling. When a slot is inactive, TSA anchors its state to the previous slot via activation-gated updating and suppresses its decoder participation through an activation-dependent additive bias on attention logits before softmax normalization. This jointly reduces state drift and reconstruction-driven interference. To improve decisions under partial occlusion and gradual reappearance, TSA further conditions activation prediction on a per-slot temporal memory produced by a Temporal Context Encoder. We evaluate TSA on MOVi-C/E, YT-VIS, and OVIS benchmarks using both standard and tracking-based metrics (FG-ARI, mBO, IDF1, HOTA). TSA consistently improves object decomposition and temporal identity preservation, with large gains on long, heavily occluded videos.

AI.tldr: Temporal Slot Activation (TSA) for unsupervised video object-centric learning that gates per-slot per-frame updates and decoder participation to enforce a consistent slot lifecycle under occlusion.
AI.motivation: Unconditional propagation in recurrent video slot-attention causes state drift when objects are absent or occluded and leads to reconstruction interference, harming object persistence and decomposition.
AI.method: Introduce TSA: learn per-slot per-frame activation α_{k,t} ∈ (0,1) without supervision. Activation gates slot updates (activation-gated updating) and applies an activation-dependent bias to decoder attention logits, suppressing inactive slots. Inactive slots anchor to their previous state. Enhance decision-making with a Temporal Context Encoder that provides per-slot temporal memory. This yields a shared latent control variable for lifecycle modeling.
AI.result: Empirically, TSA improves object decomposition and temporal identity preservation on MOVi-C/E, YT-VIS, and OVIS, across standard and tracking metrics (FG-ARI, mBO, IDF1, HOTA), with especially large gains for long, heavily occluded videos.
AI.conclusion: TSA provides an effective, supervision-free mechanism to enforce a persistent slot lifecycle, reducing state drift and reconstruction interference, and yielding robust performance gains in challenging occlusion scenarios.

title: Morphology-Aware Sample Assignment: Overcoming IoU Insensitivity for Surface Defect Detection
summary: Intersection-over-Union (IoU), as a pivotal metric for evaluating the spatial alignment between candidate proposals and ground-truth annotations, directly determines the quality of positive sample sets and the training efficacy of visual detection models. Through theoretical modeling and analysis, we uncover a non-sensitive region on the IoU response curve, within which samples yield nearly identical IoU scores despite distinct geometric overlaps. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a set of morphological similarity metrics covering area, shape, and aspect ratio, to refine the positive sample assignment process, thereby ensuring more discriminative and reliable matching. A supplementary matching score is derived via mean-based aggregation of these multidimensional similarities, compensating for the intrinsic limitation of IoU in representing structural correspondence. Theoretically, incorporating morphological similarity reshapes the response distribution of the matching function, yielding both effective directional gradients and polygon-like iso-response contours, which tightly confine high-response regions around each ground-truth instance and substantially enhance the precision of positive sample selection. Experiments based on the YOLOv9 framework demonstrate consistent performance gains on both NEUDET and GC10- DET datasets. Notably, the proposed approach is fully plug-and-play and incurs zero additional inference overhead, thereby ensuring deployment efficiency for industrial visual inspection.

AI.tldr: Introduces morphological similarity metrics (area, shape, aspect ratio) to complement IoU for positive sample assignment in visual detection, plus a mean-aggregated matching score; reshapes the matching function's response landscape to yield tighter iso-response regions and better ground-truth localization.
AI.motivation: IoU often has non-sensitive regions where distinct overlaps yield similar IoU, causing ambiguity in positive sample selection and limiting training efficacy. A more discriminative, geometry-aware matching metric is needed to improve sample quality without increasing inference cost.
AI.method: Define multidimensional morphological similarity metrics (area, shape, aspect ratio) relative to ground-truth instances. Combine them via mean-based aggregation into a supplementary matching score to pair with IoU, producing a discriminative matching function with polygon-like iso-response contours that tightly bound high responses around ground-truth boxes. Integrate as a plug-in in the YOLOv9 training pipeline without extra inference cost.
AI.result: Empirical validation on YOLOv9 shows consistent gains on NEUDET and GC10-DET datasets, with zero additional inference overhead, indicating practical plug-and-play deployment and improved positive sample precision.
AI.conclusion: Morphological similarity enriches IoU-based matching, shaping the response landscape to yield more reliable positive sampling and enhanced detection performance without sacrificing deployment efficiency.

title: Connections Between Pairs of Filters Improve the Accuracy of Convolutional Neural Networks
summary: While researchers continue to find new and improved network structures for CNNs, most of the newly invented architectures still rely on the traditional pattern of stacking convolutional blocks and separating them with pointwise activation functions. However, there are drawbacks to a network purely building on pointwise nonlinearities. One alternative is to introduce a pairwise connection between two filters of a network. Typical connection functions use multiplications or the minimum operation to realize logical AND connections. In this paper, we go one step further by demonstrating that CNNs can benefit from more general connections, which include parameters that are learned. With such parameters, the network is able to implement different connections in different network layers and better adapt the connection function to the task at hand.

AI.tldr: Proposes learnable generalized pairwise filter connections in CNNs as an alternative to pointwise activations, enabling layer-specific connection functions to improve task adaptability.
AI.motivation: Pointwise nonlinearities in standard CNNs may limit expressivity. Pairwise interactions between filters—previously simple (e.g., multiplication, min) for AND-like behavior—can be extended with learnable parameters to tailor connections across layers for different tasks.
AI.method: Introduce a generalized, parameterized pairwise connection between two filters, with learnable parameters. Allow different connection functions across layers and train end-to-end within CNN architectures to adapt the connection function to the task.
AI.result: The abstract suggests that CNNs can benefit from these generalized, learnable connections, enabling better adaptation to the task compared to pure pointwise nonlinearities.
AI.conclusion: Generalized, learnable pairwise filter connections extend CNN expressivity and adaptability, motivating future work on parameterizations, training dynamics, and computational considerations to leverage this connectivity in practical architectures.

title: CineOrchestra: Unified Entity-Centric Conditioning for Cinematic Video Generation
summary: Cinematic video depicts multiple subjects acting or interacting at specific moments, captured with deliberate camera movement, and stitched together by shot transitions. Together, these elements demand a level of fine-grained control beyond current text-to-video models. Existing work addresses each axis in isolation: multi-subject personalization, temporal control, multi-shot synthesis, or camera control; no prior framework jointly integrates all four. We present CineOrchestra, a unified video diffusion model that controls subjects, events, cameras, and shot transitions simultaneously. Our key insight is that these heterogeneous cinematic elements share a fundamental structure: each is an entity acting over a specific temporal interval, which can therefore all be expressed through one shared structure of entity-centric conditioning primitives, augmented with reference images for visual entities. This formulation reduces the architectural challenge to a single positional encoding problem, which we solve with two parameter-free coordinated rotary embeddings: (a) an interval-sampled temporal RoPE that yields consistent attention behavior across events of dramatically varying duration, and (b) a 2D entity-temporal cross-attention RoPE that disambiguates per-entity conditions and routes each to its corresponding spatiotemporal region. On two new benchmarks, CineOrchestra outperforms six per-axis specialists on dense caption following and shot-transition timing, with consistent gains in a pairwise user study and component ablations.

AI.tldr: A unified diffusion model (CineOrchestra) jointly controls subjects, events, cameras, and shot transitions using entity-centric conditioning and parameter-free coordinated RoPE; it outperforms per-axis baselines on new benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-video work treats cinematic axes (multi-subject personalization, temporal control, multi-shot synthesis, camera control) in isolation. A joint framework is needed for coherent, finely controlled cinematic video generation.
AI.method: Introduce CineOrchestra, a single video diffusion model with entity-centric conditioning primitives augmented by reference images. Use two parameter-free coordinated rotary embeddings: (a) interval-sampled temporal RoPE for consistent attention across varied durations, and (b) a 2D entity-temporal cross-attention RoPE to disambiguate per-entity conditions and route them to their spatiotemporal regions.
AI.result: Evaluated on two new benchmarks; CineOrchestra outperforms six per-axis specialists on dense caption following and shot-transition timing, with gains in a pairwise user study and component ablations.
AI.conclusion: A unified, entity-centric conditioning approach with coordinated RoPE effectively integrates multiple cinematic controls, enabling more coherent and controllable text-to-video generation; the framework establishes a foundation for joint control over subject, action, camera, and editing transitions.

title: Occupancy-Grounded Room Segmentation for Hierarchical 3D Scene Graphs
summary: Hierarchical 3D scene graphs (3DSGs) for indoor robots organize geometric and semantic information across spatial scales, with a room layer that connects object-level perception to room-scale reasoning. Existing systems construct this layer from different spatial substrates (\eg{} place clusters, wall planes, or segmentation outputs), and as a result, room nodes are not evaluated on a common geometric criterion. We present an occupancy-grounded 3DSG pipeline in which room nodes are anchored to tracked free-space regions derived from occupancy decomposition, giving each room an explicit polygonal footprint. We evaluate the pipeline on 12 Matterport3D scenes by matching predicted room polygons to annotated room instances and compare against Hydra, a representative state-of-the-art place-connectivity baseline. The results show that occupancy-grounded anchoring recovers substantially more room instances than place-connectivity construction, at the cost of lower precision, and that wall-accurate room boundaries remain an open problem for both methods. Code is available at https://github.com/crcz25/OccuSG.

AI.tldr: Occupancy-grounded anchoring of room nodes in hierarchical 3D scene graphs yields explicit polygonal footprints by grounding to tracked free-space regions; improves room-instance recovery versus place-connectivity baselines but with reduced precision and persistent wall-boundary challenges.
AI.motivation: Provide a common geometric criterion for room nodes in 3DSGs to enable room-scale reasoning for indoor robots. By anchoring room representations to occupancy-based free-space footprints, rooms gain explicit, comparable footprints across scenes.
AI.method: Derive room polygons by occupancy decomposition to track free-space regions and anchor room nodes to these footprints; evaluate on 12 Matterport3D scenes by matching predicted room polygons to annotated room instances; compare against Hydra (place-connectivity baseline); report that occupancy-grounded anchoring yields more room instances but lower precision; code released.
AI.result: Occupancy-grounded anchoring recovers substantially more room instances than the place-connectivity construction. Precision is trade-off - lower; wall-accurate room boundaries remain challenging for both methods. No per-scene wall boundary perfection yet; methodological comparison shows occupancy approach provides better room coverage.
AI.conclusion: Anchoring room-level nodes to occupancy-derived footprints improves room-instance coverage in 3DSGs and facilitates room-scale reasoning, albeit with precision costs and unresolved wall-boundary accuracy; further work needed to improve boundary localization and possibly combine substrates for robust room delineation. Code available.

title: Can Editing 1 Neuron Fix Repetition Loops in LLMs?
summary:
Yes. Can it cure doom loops? Probably not.
  The Gemma 4 instruction-tuned models share a reproducible failure: on long factual enumeration prompts, such as listing every episode of a TV series, the 88 IAU constellations, or the 151 original Pokemon, they collapse into repetition, either a tight verbatim loop or a list whose entries decay onto a single answer. These loops occur at rates as high as 95% and survive prompt rewording, inference-engine changes, and most sampling adjustments. In this paper we explore whether this behavior is localized enough to remove by weight edits. To localize the cause, we use per-layer ablation and per-neuron attribution, then confirm the strongest candidates with full-generation sweeps. The loops trace to a small set of MLP neurons (or, in the 26B-A4B Mixture-of-Experts model, a few routed experts) which we suppress with static weight edits. These "surgeries" can be as small as a single sign-inverted neuron (in the E2B model). The size of the effective edits grows with model scale, but in all cases, the loop patterns can be addressed at normal generation budgets while preserving general-purpose benchmark scores. However, the edits do not solve everything: we also study longer thinking budgets, where the two larger models most visibly enter doom looping, i.e. a non-convergent regime in which the model self-corrects in circles over a fact it cannot recall, exhausting the budget without committing to a final answer. We show this residual failure is reduced but not eliminated by the same edits, and argue it is fundamentally a knowledge-precision problem rather than a removable circuit; weight surgery can delete a loop, but it cannot supply a missing fact. Our results are both a feasibility demonstration, that is, evidence that a concrete generation pathology can be localized to a few parameters and edited out, and a delineation of where that approach stops.

AI.tldr: Doom loops in Gemma 4 models can be localized and suppressed by small weight edits to a handful of neurons/experts, but longer-thinking regimes reveal a residual knowledge-precision failure that edits cannot fix; demonstrates feasibility with limits.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether a reproducible looping pathology in instruction-tuned LMs can be understood and mitigated via targeted weight edits, and delineate the limits of such intervention.
AI.method: Use per-layer ablation, per-neuron attribution, and full-generation sweeps to identify responsible components; apply static weight edits (sign flips, pruning) to suppress loops; test across models including Mixture-of-Experts; analyze long-budget prompts.
AI.result: Loops map to a small set of MLP neurons or routed experts; edits can eliminate loops while preserving general benchmark scores; loop rate up to 95% in long prompts; larger models require bigger edits; residual non-convergent looping persists under longer budgets.
AI.conclusion: Weight-based edits demonstrate feasibility for localizing and removing a generation loop to a few parameters, but do not solve all looping; the remaining failures point to a knowledge-precision problem—the missing facts cannot be supplied by editing away the loop; defines the boundary of this mitigation approach.

title: A Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-Based Transformer Method for Solving the Open Shop Scheduling Problem
summary: The open shop scheduling problem (OSSP) arises in many industrial and service settings but remains computationally challenging as the number of jobs and machines increases. While exact methods quickly become intractable, classical dispatching rules and metaheuristics may require substantial tuning to maintain solution quality at large scales. This study develops a Transformer-based scheduling policy for OSSP using an encoder-decoder architecture with multi-head attention. The model is trained on Taillard benchmark instances (4x4, 5x5, 7x7, and 10x10) using only the processing-time matrix as input and produces feasible schedules with makespans typically within 15-30% of best-known values. To evaluate scalability, the trained policy is applied without retraining to randomly generated instances from 40x40 to 100x100 and compared against classical dispatching heuristics, including SPT, LPT, MWKR, and EST. Across these large instances, the Transformer achieved average gaps of 12.89-15.12% relative to a standard lower bound. Compared with EST, the Transformer remained competitive, typically within a modest margin, while substantially outperforming SPT and LPT. These results indicate that a Transformer policy trained on small OSSP instances can generalize to substantially larger problems and provide a feature-light, learning-based alternative to classical dispatching rules.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based OSSP policy trained on small Taillard instances generalizes to much larger OSSP problems, achieving competitive performance (12.9–15.1% gap to a lower bound) and outperforming simple heuristics (SPT/LPT); uses only processing-time matrices, offering a light, learning-based alternative to classical rules.
AI.motivation: The open shop scheduling problem is NP-hard and challenging to solve for large instances. Classical dispatching rules and metaheuristics require substantial tuning and may not scale. A data-driven, generalizable policy could provide scalable, high-quality schedules with less problem-specific tuning.
AI.method: Encoder-decoder Transformer with multi-head attention trained on Taillard OSSP instances of sizes 4x4, 5x5, 7x7, and 10x10 using only processing-time matrices as input. The policy is tested on unseen large instances (40x40 to 100x100) without retraining and compared against SPT, LPT, MWKR, and EST. Metrics include makespan relative to best-known values on small instances and gaps relative to a standard lower bound on large instances.
AI.result: On small Taillard instances, produced schedules have makespans typically within 15–30% of the best-known values. On large, unseen instances (40x40–100x100), the Transformer achieved average gaps of about 12.89–15.12% relative to a standard lower bound and was generally competitive with EST, while clearly outperforming SPT and LPT.
AI.conclusion: A Transformer policy trained on small OSSP instances can generalize to much larger problems, offering a feature-light, learning-based alternative to classical scheduling rules with robust performance across scales. Potential limitations include reliance on lower-bound benchmarks for large instances and the need to assess generalization across different instance distributions.

title: Compressing Image Style Training into a Single Model Forward
summary: Diffusion-based style transfer must balance inference efficiency with stylization fidelity. Adapter-based methods are efficient, but they inject style as an external condition and can either weaken reference-specific appearance or copy reference semantics into the generated image. Optimization-based personalization methods such as LoRA internalize style more effectively, but require a separate training process for every new style. We introduce i2L (image-to-LoRA), a framework that amortizes style LoRA training into a single forward pass. Given one or more reference images, i2L predicts LoRA weights for a text-to-image model, enabling immediate style instantiation without per-style optimization. The architecture combines an image encoder, learnable LoRA queries, and compressed decoding heads that generate adapted matrices. Training on semantically diverse style pairs encourages the predictor to preserve appearance cues while suppressing reference-content copying. Experiments on Z-Image, FLUX.2, and Hidream-O1 show that i2L improves style fidelity, prompt alignment, and perceptual quality over existing baselines. Because i2L produces explicit LoRA weights, it also supports asymmetric classifier-free guidance, multi-reference style fusion, and composition with controllable-generation modules.

AI.tldr: i2L is an image-to-LoRA framework that predicts LoRA weights from reference images, enabling instant style transfer without per-style optimization.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-style transfer faces a trade-off between inference efficiency and stylization fidelity. Adapter methods are efficient but may leak reference content; optimization-based personalisation yields better fidelity but requires per-style training. i2L aims to amortize training into a single forward pass.
AI.method: i2L uses an image encoder, learnable LoRA queries, and compressed decoding heads to output adapted LoRA matrices. It predicts LoRA weights from one or more reference images and is trained on semantically diverse style pairs to preserve appearance cues while suppressing reference-content copying. The architecture also enables asymmetric classifier-free guidance, multi-reference style fusion, and modular composition.
AI.result: Compared with existing baselines, i2L achieves improved style fidelity, prompt alignment, and perceptual quality on Z-Image, FLUX.2, and Hidream-O1. Its explicit LoRA weights enable flexible features such as asymmetric guidance, multi-reference fusion, and integration with controllable-generation modules.
AI.conclusion: i2L successfully amortizes LoRA adaptation into a single forward pass, enabling immediate style instantiation from references and flexible composition with diffusion pipelines, scalable to multiple styles without per-style optimisation.

title: Scalable Dynamic Tactile Sensing Enabled by Passive and Flexible Acoustic Waveguides
summary: Artificial dynamic tactile sensing requires sensitivity, robustness, and compliance, yet existing technologies face trade-offs when scaling to large-area arrays, compounded by wiring complexity and cost. Here, we report a passive distributed paradigm using deep sub-wavelength acoustic waveguides that decouples performance from structural flexibility. Elastic-membrane-capped Helmholtz resonators interconnected by spring-reinforced microtubes form an enclosed network with invariant acoustic transmission under macroscopic bending. By sparsely embedding microphones, the system achieves real-time localization (4 mm highest spatial resolution; >99% accuracy in a 4 microphones 64-node sensing array) and waveform reconstruction of low-frequency signals (<100 Hz). Fast Continuous Wavelet Transform and a lightweight neural network enable inference within 5.5 ms. We demonstrate conformable prototypes-fingertip arrays, a tactile glove, and large-area skins-detecting stimuli from single-hair contact to 5-mg particle impacts, arterial pulse waves, feather touches, and finger contact. This establishes a scalable, flexible, low-cost paradigm for next-generation human-machine interfaces.

AI.tldr: A passive distributed tactile sensing system using sub-wavelength acoustic waveguides and resonators achieves large-area flexible sensing with sparse microphones, enabling real-time localization and low-frequency waveform reconstruction with high accuracy and low latency.
AI.motivation: To overcome sensitivity, robustness, and compliance trade-offs in large-area tactile sensors while reducing wiring complexity and cost.
AI.method: Elastic-membrane-capped Helmholtz resonators interconnected by spring-reinforced microtubes form an enclosed, bending-invariant acoustic network. Microphones are sparsely embedded. Localization and waveform reconstruction are performed via Fast Continuous Wavelet Transform and a lightweight neural network.
AI.result: Achieves 4 mm spatial resolution with >99% accuracy in a 4-mic, 64-node array; reconstructs low-frequency signals (<100 Hz); inference in ~5.5 ms. Prototypes include fingertip arrays, a tactile glove, and large-area skins, responding to stimuli from single-hair contact to 5 mg particle impacts, arterial pulse waves, feather touches, and finger contact.
AI.conclusion: Provides a scalable, flexible, and low-cost paradigm for next-generation human–machine interfaces.

title: Efficient On-Device Diffusion LLM Inference with Mobile NPU
summary:
Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) accelerate generation by denoising multiple tokens in parallel, making them attractive for latency-sensitive mobile inference. However, repeated denoising introduces substantial computation on smartphones. Mobile neural processing units (NPUs) offer high-throughput dense matrix computation, but efficiently exploiting them remains challenging: token commitment shrinks per-block effective workloads, token revision complicates KV cache reuse, and limited NPU-visible address space incurs costly remapping and data transfer overheads.
  In this paper, we propose llada.cpp, the first NPU-aware inference framework for accelerating dLLMs on smartphones. llada.cpp aligns block-wise dLLM inference with the execution characteristics of mobile NPUs through three techniques. (1) Multi-Block Speculative Decoding fills the shrinking workload in late-stage current-block decoding with speculative future-block tokens. (2) Dual-Path Progressive Revision keeps committed tokens revisable until stable and refreshes unstable tokens through a CPU-side path without stalling dense NPU execution. (3) Swap-Optimized Memory Runtime compacts NPU-visible address layouts and overlaps data staging with NPU computation to reduce remapping and transfer overheads. We implement llada.cpp as an end-to-end framework and evaluate it across diverse hardware platforms and dLLM workloads. llada.cpp reduces LLaDA-8B generation latency by 17x-42x over the CPU baseline with prefix KV cache reuse, while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: llada.cpp is an NPU-aware end-to-end framework that accelerates diffusion LLMs on smartphones by aligning processing with mobile NPU characteristics, achieving 17x–42x latency reductions on LLaDA-8B while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies of mobile diffusion LLM inference: shrinking per-block workloads, KV cache reuse constraints, and remapping/transfer overheads on mobile NPUs.
AI.method: Introduce three techniques: (1) Multi-Block Speculative Decoding; (2) Dual-Path Progressive Revision; (3) Swap-Optimized Memory Runtime; integrated into an end-to-end framework and evaluated across hardware platforms and dLLM workloads.
AI.result: Latency for LLaDA-8B generation reduced by 17x–42x over CPU baseline with prefix KV cache reuse; generation quality preserved.
AI.conclusion: First NPU-aware framework for accelerating dLLMs on smartphones; demonstrates substantial latency improvements and suggests a viable path for mobile diffusion inference.

title: UP-NRPA: User Portrait based Nested Rollout Policy Adaptation for Planning with Large Language Models in Goal-oriented Dialogue Systems
summary: To address the challenge that current dialogue policy planning methods struggle to dynamically adapt to diverse user characteristics, this paper proposes a User Portrait based Nested Rollout Policy Adaptation (UP-NRPA) online framework with Large Language Models. In contrast to conventional approaches dependent on model training and require offline reinforcement learning policy models for user groups, UP-NRPA enables dynamic customization of dialogue strategies through an adaptive mechanism. This is achieved by leveraging real-time user feedback alongside personality, preferences, and objectives mapped from the current user portrait, thereby adapting to user characteristics without offline reinforcement learning. In collaborative and non-collaborative dialogue benchmarks, UP-NRPA demonstrated considerable benefits, achieving an impressive 100% success rate in multiple dialogue tasks. Particularly in negotiation tasks, the sale-to-list ratio (SL) increased by 56.41%. This demonstrates that UP-NRPA can adapt to diverse user needs without requiring a training mechanism, enabling the dialogue system to adapt to user characteristics.

AI.tldr: A real-time, LLM-driven online framework UP-NRPA uses a User Portrait to adapt dialogue policies without offline RL, achieving 100% success and 56.41% higher sale-to-list ratio in negotiations.
AI.motivation: To overcome static policy planning that fails to adapt to diverse user traits; enable dynamic personalization using real-time feedback and user portraits.
AI.method: Online framework combining User Portrait mapping with Nested Rollout Policy Adaptation, leveraging large language models to tailor strategies; avoids offline RL; uses real-time feedback to adjust policies.
AI.result: In collaborative and non-collaborative dialogue benchmarks, UP-NRPA achieved 100% success across multiple tasks; negotiation tasks saw SL improve by 56.41%.
AI.conclusion: Shows potential for adaptive dialogue systems that personalize strategies to user characteristics without offline RL; further work needed on generalizability, scalability, and evaluation details.

title: Explaining RhythmFormer: A Systematic XAI Analysis of Periodic Sparse Attention for Remote Photoplethysmography
summary: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) transformers achieve low heart-rate error on benchmarks, yet their decisions remain opaque--a growing concern as rPPG moves toward clinical heart rate estimation. Existing rPPG XAI is dominated by qualitative heatmap inspection without quantitative faithfulness metrics or physiology-grounded validation, leaving a gap between visual plausibility and auditable evidence. We address this gap. First, we adapt four attribution methods (raw attention, rollout, flow, Beyond Intuition) to RhythmFormer's bi-level routing attention with top-$k$ selection. Second, we introduce a skin coverage metric quantifying how much attribution mass falls on skin regions. Third, we adapt the SaCo faithfulness coefficient from its original classification setting to rPPG regression by using the MAE between original and perturbed predicted rPPG waveforms as the perturbation impact. Applying these tools, we quantify a multi-hop leakage effect under sparse top-$k$ routing: attention rollout and flow almost completely restores the connections that individual refined-attention layers explicitly set to zero. Beyond Intuition mitigates this via its value-projection-weighted rollout and gradient-supported mask, attaining the highest median refined skin coverage ($0.83$ vs. $0.57$ for vanilla rollout) and faithfulness ($F=0.92$) among the evaluated methods on UBFC-rPPG. Validation across diverse datasets and model variants is needed. A case study on a low-SaCo outlier further shows all four methods recovering consistently once an artefactual region is replaced, suggesting consistent SaCo behavior across attribution families in this illustrative case. Together, these metrics move XAI for rPPG toward auditable numerical evidence about spatial alignment and perturbation faithfulness, i.e. trustworthy rPPG XAI.

AI.tldr: Quantitative XAI evaluation for RhythmFormer rPPG transformer; introduces skin coverage and regression-faithfulness adaptation of SaCo; shows Beyond Intuition excels on UBFC-rPPG; reveals multi-hop leakage under sparse top-k routing; calls for cross-dataset validation.
AI.motivation: XAI in rPPG lacks quantitative, physiology-grounded faithfulness metrics; need auditable evidence linking attribution to physiological relevance.
AI.method: Adapt four attribution methods (raw attention, rollout, flow, Beyond Intuition) to RhythmFormer's bi-level routing with top-k; define skin coverage metric; adapt SaCo faithfulness for regression using MAE between original and perturbed waveforms; evaluate on UBFC-rPPG and related datasets; case study on low-SaCo outlier.
AI.result: Beyond Intuition achieves highest median refined skin coverage (0.83 vs 0.57) and faithfulness (F=0.92) among methods; reveals almost complete restoration of pruned connections by rollout and flow in a multi-hop leakage; evidence of need for more robust methods; results suggest generalization but require validation across datasets and variants.
AI.conclusion: The proposed metrics advance auditable, physiology-aligned XAI for rPPG; findings emphasize spatial alignment and perturbation faithfulness; call for broader validation and standardization of XAI evaluation in rPPG.

title: $μ_0$: A Scalable 3D Interaction-Trace World Model
summary: World models that capture how actions induce physical change enable scalable robot learning without reliance on embodiment-specific action labels. Pixel-space video models provide broad visual priors but expend model capacity on dense appearance reconstruction, while direct action models require embodiment-specific labels that hinder scalability. We present $μ_0$, a scalable world model based on 3D traces. Rather than predicting dense pixels or directly modeling actions, $μ_0$ forecasts smooth 3D trajectories for salient interaction points such as objects, tools, hands, and contact regions, yielding a compact, embodiment-agnostic motion interface. To enable training from diverse video sources, our TraceExtract system automatically extracts 3D supervision by selecting keypoints, constructing globally aligned traces, and associating motion segments with hierarchical language captions. This TraceExtract supervision pretrains $μ_0$ by combining a pretrained vision-language backbone with a modular trace expert, which represents each query via B-spline control points and predicts future traces. Experiments show that $μ_0$ outperforms baselines in both 2D and 3D trace prediction, including trace prediction models and tokenized VLM methods. Because $μ_0$ is frozen and reusable, it can be paired with action experts for downstream robot embodiments. Despite action-free pretraining, the resulting trace-conditioned policies achieve performance competitive with VLA models pretrained with action supervision, such as $π_0$. These results establish 3D traces as a scalable and transferable representation for cross-embodiment manipulation.

AI.tldr: μ0 is a scalable, embodiment-agnostic world model that predicts 3D traces of salient points, with TraceExtract providing 3D supervision from diverse videos; it outperforms baselines and matches action-supervised models, enabling cross-embodiment manipulation.
AI.motivation: Overcome the bottlenecks of pixel-space models (dense reconstruction) and action-label reliance (embodiment-specific supervision) by learning a compact, transferable 3D-trace representation for manipulation that generalizes across embodiments.
AI.method: Predict 3D traces using B-spline control points for salient interaction points; automatically extract globally aligned 3D supervision via TraceExtract, linking motion segments to hierarchical language captions; pretrain μ0 with a vision-language backbone plus a modular trace expert; μ0 is frozen and reusable for downstream tasks, pairing with action experts when available.
AI.result: μ0 achieves superior 2D/3D trace prediction compared to trace-only and tokenized VLM baselines; trace-conditioned policies are competitive with VLA models pretrained with action supervision (e.g., π0); demonstrates scalability and transferability of 3D traces for cross-embodiment manipulation.
AI.conclusion: 3D traces provide a scalable, transferable representation for cross-embodiment manipulation, yielding a reusable foundation model (μ0) that can be paired with action experts to realize embodied control without action-label pretraining.

title: High-Frequency Pricing at Scale for E-Commerce
summary: This paper presents the design, development, and implementation of a specialized forecast-then-optimize algorithmic pricing tool for sales campaigns in fashion e-commerce. Sales events present unique challenges for pricing including volatile demand patterns, rapid pricing decisions, and the need to balance short-term revenue with long-term profitability. We describe our approach combining daily-resolution demand forecasting using gradient-boosted trees with a multi-objective optimization framework that maximizes both long-term profit and net merchandise value for more than 5 million articles. Our solution addresses key limitations of existing weekly-granularity systems by implementing a forecast-then-optimize architecture that reduces pricing decision time from hours to minutes. We validate our approach through 23 A/B tests across 12 markets during 2023-2024 sales campaigns at Zalando, one of Europe's leading online fashion retailers. Experimental results demonstrate that the new pricing system achieves approximately 6% higher profit while maintaining equivalent performance on sales and revenue compared to the previous manual-algorithmic hybrid approach. Based on these results, the algorithm was successfully deployed to production and now handles the majority of algorithmic pricing decisions for sales campaigns at the company.

AI.tldr: Forecast-then-optimize pricing tool for fashion e-commerce; combines daily demand forecasting with multi-objective optimization to improve profitability at scale.
AI.motivation: Sales events require rapid, profit-aware pricing under volatile demand; existing weekly systems limit responsiveness and long-term value extraction; aim to automate pricing for millions of SKUs across campaigns.
AI.method: Daily-resolution demand forecasting using gradient-boosted trees paired with a multi-objective optimization that maximizes long-term profit and net merchandise value; forecast-then-optimize architecture; scales to >5 million articles; reduces decision time from hours to minutes; validated with 23 A/B tests across 12 markets (2023–2024) at Zalando; production deployment.
AI.result: About 6% higher profit with equivalent sales/revenue compared to the prior manual-algorithmic hybrid approach; deployment to production; pricing decisions for campaigns largely automated.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates scalability and effectiveness of forecast-then-optimize pricing in large-scale fashion e-commerce, delivering faster, more profitable pricing decisions without sacrificing throughput.

title: History of the Muddy Children Puzzle
summary: The Muddy Children Puzzle is a puzzle about knowledge and ignorance that has been inspiring for the development of epistemic logic. Who came up with it first? This is unclear. We trace the origin of the Muddy Children Puzzle through logical and literary publications over the past two centuries. The puzzle inspired a numerous variations such as involving numbers or coloured hats. We also present a novel hats puzzle involving self-reference.

AI.tldr: A historical survey tracing the origin of the Muddy Children Puzzle across logic and literature over two centuries, surveying variations (numbers, coloured hats) and introducing a novel self-referential hats puzzle.
AI.motivation: Clarify the origin and dissemination of the Muddy Children Puzzle, map its influence on epistemic logic, and document its variants to illuminate how epistemic reasoning evolved.
AI.method: Historical-literary survey compiling references from logical treatises and literary sources over two centuries; analysis of variants and synthesis of a new self-referential hats puzzle.
AI.result: Identifies unclear origin, traces dissemination through both logical and literary publications, documents numerous variations (including numbers and coloured hats), and presents a novel self-referential hats puzzle.
AI.conclusion: The Muddy Children Puzzle has a long multidisciplinary lineage shaping epistemic reasoning; its core ideas spurred diverse variants, and the work contributes a self-referential hats puzzle as a new variant.

title: Temporal Backtracking Search for Test-time Generative Video Reasoning
summary: While test-time scaling has revolutionized reasoning in large language models, generative video reasoning remains bottlenecked by a single-shot paradigm. We demonstrate that searching over denoising steps cannot rescue logically flawed rollouts because spatial trajectories commit early in the diffusion process. Root-level Best-of-N (BoN) sampling is similarly inefficient: reasoning errors cluster early in the temporal axis, and resampling blindly discards verified upstream progress. To unlock effective test-time scaling for video models, we introduce Temporal Backtracking Search (TBS), which shifts the search space to the temporal axis. TBS transforms video generation into an iterative generate-verify-restart loop via three core mechanisms: (1) variable-K conditioning to resume generation from arbitrary clean prefixes; (2) temporal process verification to localize failures and extract valid restart anchors; and (3) prefix-based search to reallocate compute toward extending correct trajectories rather than root resampling. Across algorithmic, navigation, and robotics domains, TBS Pareto-dominates matched-budget BoN. In a strict out-of-distribution setting where one-shot generation collapses (0.7% for BoN), TBS achieves 22.7%, with every solved episode stemming from a restarted branch. Ultimately, TBS reveals that the local reasoning competence of video models far exceeds what single-shot rollouts indicate, providing a scalable test-time framework to unlock it.

AI.tldr: Temporal Backtracking Search (TBS) shifts video-generation search from spatial rollouts to the temporal axis, enabling an iterative generate-verify-restart loop that improves test-time scalability over single-shot Best-of-N (BoN) methods.
AI.motivation: Test-time scaling for video models remains bottlenecked by a single-shot generation paradigm, where early-committed spatial trajectories cause reasoning errors and resampling strategies fail to recover verified progress. A temporal-axis search aims to localize and recover from failures more efficiently.
AI.method: Introduce Temporal Backtracking Search (TBS) with three core mechanisms: (1) variable-K conditioning to resume generation from arbitrary clean prefixes; (2) temporal process verification to localize failures and extract restart anchors; (3) prefix-based search to reallocate compute toward extending correct temporal trajectories. TBS implements an iterative generate-verify-restart loop.
AI.result: TBS Pareto-dominates matched-budget BoN across algorithmic, navigation, and robotics domains. In a strict out-of-distribution setting where one-shot generation collapses (0.7% BoN success), TBS achieves 22.7% success, with every solved episode stemming from a restarted branch, indicating superior utilization of compute under budget constraints.
AI.conclusion: Overall, TBS reveals that video models possess higher local reasoning competence than single-shot rollouts suggest and provides a scalable test-time framework to unlock this capability.

title: FlowMo-WM: A World Model with Object Momentum and Hidden Ambient Drift
summary: World models in robot learning predict future states from visual observations and actions, enabling agents to reason about the consequences of their controls. However, many action-conditioned models are evaluated in settings where motion is dominated by immediate control, whereas aquatic surface vehicles and other real-world objects continue moving under inertia and are displaced by hidden ambient drift, such as water currents or wind. We propose FlowMo-WM, an end-to-end trainable visual world model that infers object-centric motion state and a predictive long-history context associated with hidden drift from image-action histories without direct supervision of flow fields. FlowMo-WM factorizes image-action history into a short-history latent state, trained to summarize object-centric motion, and a longer-history context, trained to summarize slowly varying exogenous influences. A zero-context residual transition separates action-conditioned base dynamics from context-dependent drift effects during latent rollout. In simulated aquatic surface-vehicle environments with diverse hidden flows, disturbances, and randomized vehicle dynamics, FlowMo-WM improves long-horizon rollout accuracy over representative action-conditioned latent world models. Prediction-time context ablations, in which the inferred context is zeroed or shuffled during rollout, show that the ambient context is important for stable prediction under hidden drift, while frozen linear probes characterize information encoded in the learned factors.

AI.tldr: FlowMo-WM is a visual world model that disentangles short-term object-centric motion from long-term ambient drift to enable accurate long-horizon predictions under hidden flows, using a zero-context residual transition and end-to-end training; it outperforms standard action-conditioned models in simulated aquatic environments.
AI.motivation: Real-world inertia and ambient drift (e.g., water currents, wind) cause motion that is not directly commanded. Conventional action-conditioned models struggle to predict long-horizon dynamics under hidden drift, motivating a model that separates base dynamics from context-driven drift via long-history information.
AI.method: End-to-end trainable visual world model FlowMo-WM. It factorizes image-action history into a short-history latent state (summarizing object-centric motion) and a longer-history context (summarizing slowly varying external influences). A zero-context residual transition separates action-conditioned base dynamics from context-dependent drift during latent rollout. Evaluation uses simulated aquatic surface-vehicle environments with diverse hidden flows and randomized dynamics. Ablations include prediction-time context zeroing/shuffling and frozen linear probes to analyze learned representations.
AI.result: FlowMo-WM improves long-horizon rollout accuracy over representative action-conditioned latent world models in the simulated aquatic environments, demonstrating the importance of inferred ambient context for stable prediction under hidden drift.
AI.conclusion: Ambient context is essential for stable long-horizon prediction under hidden drift; FlowMo-WM’s separation of short-term motion and long-term drift captures slowly varying exogenous influences without explicit flow supervision, with ablations confirming the utility of the inferred context and learned factors.

title: A fully GPU-based workflow for building physics emulators of hypersonic flows
summary: The ability to resolve complex physical phenomena with high fidelity and at low computational cost is central to addressing key challenges in modern engineering. A prime example lies in hypersonic flows, where the precise prediction of the full flowfield topology, in particular with respect to shock wave location and intensity, is critical. Yet supersonic and hypersonic flows continue to be a stumbling block for traditional reduced-order models and neural emulators that struggle to capture steep gradients in flow states with physical consistency in applications of industrial relevance. To that end, we introduce a fully GPU based workflow that integrates accelerated data generation with the training of neural emulators augmented by uncertainty quantification and physics-aware refinement. Our workflow is enabled by a differentiable high-fidelity solver (JAX-Fluids) which we employ for rapid dataset creation and residual-based improvement of the neural emulator to enhance physical consistency. Building on this framework, we first present a suite of model architectures and analyze their scaling behavior to expose their strengths and shortcomings. We then show that residual-based refinement enables training on cases where only mesh and input parameters are available, substantially reducing residuals and improving physical consistency. Together, differentiable simulation and residual-based refinement yield physics emulators that remain reliable beyond their training distribution, a key requirement for deploying surrogates in real-world engineering design loops.

AI.tldr: GPU-based differentiable workflow for hypersonic flow surrogates with residual-based refinement
AI.motivation: Hypersonic flows require accurate full-field topology (shock location and strength). Traditional reduced-order models and neural emulators struggle with steep gradients and maintaining physical consistency in industrially relevant scenarios. A fast, scalable, differentiable data-generation and refinement pipeline is needed to produce reliable surrogates for design loops.
AI.method: A fully GPU-based pipeline leveraging a differentiable high-fidelity solver (JAX-Fluids) for rapid data generation. Neural emulators are trained with uncertainty quantification and augmented by residual-based refinement to enforce physical consistency. The study analyzes multiple model architectures and their scaling, and demonstrates training on cases where only mesh and input parameters are available, using residual-based refinement to reduce residuals.
AI.result: Residual-based refinement substantially reduces residuals and improves physical consistency. Architecture analysis highlights strengths and weaknesses in scaling and performance. The combined differentiable simulation with refinement yields physics-aware emulators that remain reliable beyond their training distribution, enabling deployment in real-world engineering design workflows.
AI.conclusion: Integrating differentiable simulation with residual-based refinement yields robust, physically consistent surrogates for complex hypersonic flows, offering reliable, efficient tools for industrial design and optimization.

title: Orchestra-o1: Omnimodal Agent Orchestration
summary: The recent success of agent swarms has shifted the paradigm of large language model (LLM)-based agents from single-agent workflows to multi-agent systems, highlighting the importance of agent orchestration for task decomposition and collaboration. However, existing orchestration frameworks are limited to a narrow set of modalities and struggle to generalize to more complex settings where heterogeneous modalities coexist and interact. This limitation becomes particularly pronounced in omnimodal scenarios, where tasks require the unified understanding and coordination of diverse inputs such as text, image, audio, and video. In this work, we propose Orchestra-o1, an omnimodal agent orchestration framework designed to support efficient agent collaboration across multiple modalities. Orchestra-o1 introduces a unified orchestration mechanism that enables modality-aware task decomposition, online sub-agent specialization, and parallel sub-task execution. This scalable design allows agent systems to effectively tackle complex real-world tasks involving heterogeneous information sources, surpassing the second-best approach by 10.3% accuracy on the OmniGAIA benchmark. Furthermore, we introduce decision-aligned group relative policy optimization (DA-GRPO), an efficient agentic reinforcement learning approach for training Orchestra-o1-8B, which also achieves state-of-the-art performance against all existing open-source omnimodal agents.

AI.tldr: Orchestra-o1 is an omnimodal agent orchestration framework enabling modality-aware task decomposition, online sub-agent specialization, and parallel execution; it achieves a 10.3% accuracy improvement on OmniGAIA and uses DA-GRPO to train an 8B model, attaining state-of-the-art results among open-source omnimodal agents.
AI.motivation: There is a need to generalize multi-agent orchestration to omnimodal tasks involving diverse inputs (text, image, audio, video). Existing frameworks handle limited modalities and struggle with heterogeneous information, hindering scalable collaboration.
AI.method: Introduce a unified orchestration mechanism with modality-aware task decomposition, online sub-agent specialization, and parallel sub-task execution. Propose DA-GRPO, a reinforcement learning approach for training Orchestra-o1-8B.
AI.result: Empirical gains include surpassing the second-best method by 10.3% accuracy on the OmniGAIA benchmark. DA-GRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance against all existing open-source omnimodal agents.
AI.conclusion: Orchestra-o1 provides scalable, effective omnimodal agent orchestration, enabling efficient collaboration across heterogeneous modalities; DA-GRPO supports efficient training and yields state-of-the-art performance.

title: Mirage Probes: How Vision Models Fake Visual Understanding
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) can answer image-based questions confidently, and often correctly, even when no image is provided. This mirage behavior inflates benchmark scores without reflecting visual grounding. Prior work treats this as a single failure mode. We argue it is two. Using Mirage Probes, a contrastive probing framework that pairs paraphrased question variants with matched mirage and non-mirage labels on the same image, we show that mirage behavior is linearly decodable from internal activations across residual stream, MLP, post-attention, and attention-head sites in two open-source VLMs. We demonstrate that a Naive Bayes text baseline cannot recover this signal, ruling out surface lexical confounds. Cross-benchmark separability patterns, together with a novel Prior Harnessing Index (PHI) measuring how much a model can answer from text alone, expose two distinct regimes: textual biases, where the model answers from language priors without engaging visual representations, and spurious images, where it constructs false visual content in latent space and answers as if grounded. The distinction has direct mitigation consequences: text-distribution cleaning can address the first regime but cannot reach the second, since spurious-image mirages live in the model's visual representations rather than its text. Faithful visual grounding will require interventions at the representational level.

AI.tldr: Two types of mirage failure in vision-language models: textual biases (answers from language priors) and spurious images (false visual content in latent space). Introduces Mirage Probes to linearly decode mirage signals from internal representations, demonstrates limitations of text-only baselines, and proposes Prior Harnessing Index (PHI) to separate regimes. Implications: mitigations require representational interventions, not just text data cleaning.
AI.motivation: Address the phenomenon where vision-language models answer correctly or confidently without using the image, inflating benchmark scores. Propose a framework to dissect this into two distinct failure modes and study where they reside in the model's representations.
AI.method: Mirage Probes: contrastive probing with paraphrased question variants paired with matched mirage and non-mirage labels on the same image. Analyze linearly decodable signals from activations across residual stream, MLP, post-attention, and attention heads in two open-source VLMs. Use Naive Bayes text baseline as a control. Introduce cross-benchmark analyses and Prior Harnessing Index (PHI) to quantify text-only answering.
AI.result: Found two separable regimes: textual biases (text priors without visual engagement) and spurious images (latent-space false visual content leading to grounded-like answers). Mirage signals are linearly decodable from multiple components of the model; NB baselines fail to recover signals, ruling out lexical confounds. Text-distribution cleaning addresses only textual biases; spurious-image regime requires interventions at the representational level.
AI.conclusion: Faithful visual grounding cannot be achieved by text-only adjustments; it requires interventions in the model's representations to align visual content with textual queries.

title: Multi-Agent Embodied Autonomous Driving: From V2X Information Exchange to Shared World Models
summary: Autonomous driving is shifting from isolated vehicle intelligence toward multi-agent embodied systems that share perception, infer intent, and coordinate action under uncertainty. This survey examines this transition through the lens of Shared World Models (SWMs): predictive cross-agent representations maintained across vehicles, infrastructure, and other traffic participants. We review more than 380 publications spanning vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication, collaborative perception, inter-agent cognition, cooperative planning, end-to-end cooperative driving, and simulation and data engines for closed-loop validation. The organizing question is how exchanged observations become aligned state, intent-aware interaction, and coordinated downstream action. Across the surveyed literature, evaluation remains concentrated in simulation, curated benchmarks, and offline protocols. Foundation-model-based coordination also lacks verified real-time safety guarantees in open traffic. These gaps motivate key research priorities for multi-agent embodied autonomous driving (MAEAD): verifiable shared-state maintenance, robust intent and plan alignment, and safe coordinated action under communication, latency, and deployment constraints.

AI.tldr: A survey of multi-agent embodied autonomous driving through Shared World Models (SWMs); reviews 380+ publications across V2X, perception, cognition, planning, end-to-end driving, and simulators; highlights evaluation is mainly in simulation/benchmarks/offline protocols, notes lack of verified real-time safety guarantees for foundation-model-based coordination, and proposes MAEAD priorities: verifiable shared-state maintenance, robust intent/plan alignment, and safe coordination under communication latency and deployment constraints.
AI.motivation: To move autonomous driving from isolated vehicle intelligence to multi-agent embodied systems with shared perception and coordinated action under uncertainty, using predictive cross-agent representations (SWMs).
AI.method: Systematic literature review and synthesis across V2X, collaborative perception, inter-agent cognition, cooperative planning, end-to-end driving, and simulation/data engines for closed-loop validation; organizing question centers on how exchanged observations align into state, intent, and coordinated action.
AI.result: Across the surveyed literature, evaluation remains concentrated in simulation, benchmarks, and offline protocols; foundation-model-based coordination lacks verified real-time safety guarantees in open traffic; identification of gaps and priorities for MAEAD.
AI.conclusion: Need verifiable shared-state maintenance, robust alignment of intent and plans, and safe coordinated action under communication, latency, and deployment constraints; outlines key research priorities for multi-agent embodied autonomous driving (MAEAD).

title: FedSPC: Shared Parameter Correction for Personalized Federated Learning
comment: Accepted for presentation at FL@FM-IJCAI'26, in conjunction with IJCAI 2026. 9 pages
summary: Personalized federated learning (PFL) is one of the important approaches in federated learning for addressing statistical heterogeneity while enabling client-specific adaptation. Many PFL methods split the model into shared and personalized parameters, which are jointly trained on each client. However, this creates an optimization issue: shared parameters are updated by clients optimizing different local objectives, which can lead to inconsistent shared updates and weaken the shared representation. To address this problem, we propose Federated Shared Parameter Correction (FedSPC), a modular correction method for PFL. FedSPC applies control-variate correction only to the shared parameters of a given PFL method, while leaving personalized parameters unchanged. It can be integrated into three common PFL settings: shared feature extractors, shared classifiers, and fully shared models with local regularization. Experiments on CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet with ViT, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 show that FedSPC improves performance across representative PFL methods, including FedPer, FedRep, FedBABU, LG-FedAvg, and Ditto.

AI.tldr: FedSPC is a modular control-variate correction applied to the shared parameters in personalized federated learning, improving performance of diverse PFL methods by stabilizing shared updates without modifying personalized components.
AI.motivation: In PFL, heterogeneous local objectives lead to inconsistent updates of shared parameters, weakening the shared representation. A targeted correction on shared parameters seeks to mitigate this without compromising personalized components.
AI.method: FedSPC introduces a control-variate correction that is applied only to the shared parameter updates within a given PFL method. It is modular and can be integrated into three settings: (1) shared feature extractors, (2) shared classifiers, and (3) fully shared models with local regularization. The correction is compatible with representative PFL methods such as FedPer, FedRep, FedBABU, LG-FedAvg, and Ditto.
AI.result: Empirical validation on CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet using ViT, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 shows performance improvements across the tested PFL methods, indicating robustness across architectures and datasets.
AI.conclusion: FedSPC provides a simple, modular mechanism to correct shared parameter updates, improving overall PFL performance while leaving personalized parameters untouched.

title: Hybrid Open-Ended Tri-Evolution Makes Better Deep Researcher
summary: Deep research and agent evolution serve as de-facto tasks for AI agents in real-world applications toward artificial general intelligence. The former enables autonomous retrieval and integration of information in open-ended environments to tackle open-ended research tasks, yet it is constrained by the static parametric deep research capabilities of agent systems. The latter allows agents to autonomously interact with the environment to gain experiences that evolve model capabilities. However, its effectiveness has been widely validated only on verifiable tasks with standard answers, leaving a gap with open-ended research tasks. To bridge these two critical tasks, we propose the Hybrid Open-Ended Tri-Evolution (HOTE) framework, which leverages hybrid-mode reinforcement learning to facilitate the collaborative evolution of a proposer, solver and judge based on web-scale knowledge, moving toward autonomous evolving agents in open-ended tasks and environments. Extensive experiments on three long-form deep research benchmarks demonstrate that the 8B model trained via HOTE surpasses the strongest static open 8-32B models as well as those trained by state-of-the-art deep research training methods with less time overhead, and further verify that the evolution of all three modules in HOTE is indispensable.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Open-Ended Tri-Evolution (HOTE) introduces a triadic framework that co-evolves a proposer, solver, and judge via hybrid-mode reinforcement learning to tackle open-ended deep research tasks, achieving superior performance with a relatively compact 8B model and lower training time compared to larger static models and prior methods. The tri-module evolution is shown to be indispensable for results.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between deep research (autonomous information retrieval/integration in open-ended environments) and agent evolution (autonomous environmental interaction) for open-ended research tasks, addressing limitations of static parametric capabilities and evaluation on verifiable tasks.
AI.method: Hybrid-mode reinforcement learning enabling collaborative tri-evolution of proposer, solver, and judge using web-scale knowledge; iterative updates across the three modules guided by open-ended tasks and long-form benchmarks.
AI.result: An 8B HOTE-trained model surpasses the strongest static open 8–32B models and state-of-the-art deep research training methods, with reduced time overhead; all three modules' evolution is indispensable for the observed gains.
AI.conclusion: HOTE provides a viable path toward autonomous open-ended agents by jointly evolving the proposer, solver, and judge, with clear empirical benefits on long-form deep research benchmarks; tri-evolution is essential for achieving and sustaining performance gains.

title: Avatar V: Scaling Video-Reference Avatar Video Generation
comment: 31 pages, 15 figures. All contributors are listed in alphabetical order by first name
summary: Generating avatar videos that are not merely visually similar to a target individual but behaviorally recognizable, faithfully reproducing their talking rhythm, gestural tendencies, and expression dynamics, remains an open challenge. Existing methods predominantly condition on single static images, which provide insufficient identity information and cannot capture dynamic motion traits, while standard pixel-level objectives underserve the perceptually critical facial regions that determine avatar fidelity. We present Avatar V, a production-scale framework that addresses these limitations through video-reference-conditioned identity modeling. Rather than compressing identity into fixed-size embeddings, the model conditions directly on the full token sequence of a reference video, learning to reproduce both static identity attributes (facial geometry, skin texture) and dynamic behavioral patterns (talking rhythm, micro-expressions) through attention over the reference context. We introduce Sparse Reference Attention, an asymmetric mechanism achieving linear-complexity conditioning on arbitrarily long references; a motion representation stream enabling closed-loop talking style transfer; and an identity-aware super-resolution refiner inheriting the full reference conditioning. These are supported by a data engine curating 100M+ training clips from 50M raw videos, and a five-stage training pipeline with flow matching pre-training, personality fine-tuning, two-phase distillation (>10x acceleration), and RLHF alignment, deployed across thousands of GPUs. Avatar V generates 1080p videos of unlimited duration, achieving state-of-the-art identity preservation, lip synchronization, and generation quality on our cross-scene benchmark, consistently outperforming leading systems including Seedance 2.0, Kling O3 Pro, Veo 3.1, and OmniHuman 1.5 in both automated metrics and human evaluation.

AI.tldr: Avatar V is a production-scale video-reference-conditioned avatar generation framework that uses full reference video tokens, introducing Sparse Reference Attention and additional streams, enabling unlimited-duration 1080p avatars with state-of-the-art identity, lip-sync, and quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome reliance on single static images and pixel-level objectives which fail to capture dynamic identity and motion traits for high-fidelity avatar videos.
AI.method: Condition on the full token sequence of a reference video instead of fixed embeddings; introduce Sparse Reference Attention with linear complexity; add a motion representation stream for talking style transfer; implement identity-aware super-resolution; build a large data engine (100M+ clips) and a five-stage training pipeline (flow matching pretraining, personality fine-tuning, two-phase distillation, RLHF); deploy across thousands of GPUs.
AI.result: Avatar V generates 1080p videos of unlimited duration, achieving state-of-the-art identity preservation, lip synchronization, and generation quality on a cross-scene benchmark, outperforming Seedance 2.0, Kling O3 Pro, Veo 3.1, and OmniHuman 1.5 in automated metrics and human evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Video-reference-conditioned identity modeling with scalable attention and dedicated pipelines can surpass image-based methods for high-fidelity avatar videos, though practical considerations such as efficiency, generalization, and potential misuse warrant further study.

title: Efficient Domain-Adaptive Policy Learning via Kernel Representation with Application to Quadrotor Control under Non-Stationary Disturbances
summary: We present an algorithm for efficient domain-adaptive policy learning via kernel representations. Learning domain-adaptive policies is challenging since it requires an environment representation that is both sufficiently expressive to model complex sim-to-real gaps during offline training, and computationally efficient enough to support rapid online adaptation during deployment. For instance, a quadrotor may encounter time-varying, non-stationary disturbances, such as sudden gusts of wind, payload shifts, or transitions between distinct flight regimes with and without ground effects. To address these challenges, we model unknown disturbances using a differentiable kernel approximation based on random Fourier features. During the offline training phase, we randomly sample kernel coefficients and bandwidth parameters to generate a rich diversity of disturbance profiles. We then optimize the control policy via differentiable simulation with analytical gradients, a process that takes only 50 seconds of training time on an RTX 4090 GPU. During hardware deployment, the policy adapts to non-stationary environments in real time by updating both the kernel coefficients and bandwidth through online least-squares estimation. We evaluate our method on quadrotor trajectory tracking tasks across high-fidelity numerical simulations and hardware experiments using Crazyflie, subjected to various disturbances, including complex aerodynamic effects, wind, ground effects, and payload fluctuations.

AI.tldr: Kernel-based domain-adaptive policy learning for quadrotors using random Fourier features to model disturbances; offline differentiable training with diverse synthetic disturbances; real-time online adaptation via online least-squares; validated in simulation and hardware (Crazyflie).
AI.motivation: Address sim-to-real gaps and non-stationary disturbances in robotic control by learning a rich yet efficient environment representation that supports rapid online adaptation.
AI.method: Model disturbances with a differentiable kernel using random Fourier features. Offline phase: randomize kernel coefficients and bandwidths to generate diverse disturbance profiles; train policy via differentiable simulation with analytical gradients (≈50s on RTX 4090). Deployment: online update of kernel coefficients and bandwidths through online least-squares estimation. Evaluation on quadrotor trajectory tracking in high-fidelity simulations and hardware (Crazyflie) under wind, ground effects, payload changes, and aerodynamic disturbances.
AI.result: Demonstrates fast offline training and real-time adaptability; robust quadrotor performance under varied disturbances in both simulation and hardware experiments.
AI.conclusion: Kernel-based disturbance modeling with random Fourier features enables efficient offline policy learning and rapid online adaptation, improving domain transfer for quadrotor control under complex, non-stationary disturbances.

title: The Weight Norm Sets the Grokking Timescale: A Causal Delay Law
summary: Grokking is the delayed onset of generalization in neural networks, arising long after they fit the training data. Whether the weight norm causes this delay is disputed: some studies report a critical norm at the transition, others observe grokking with no fixed norm at all. We settle this by intervening on the norm during training rather than only observing it. Under free training with weight decay, networks grok when the weight norm reaches a value Wc that varies little across seeds and learning rates (CV 1 to 2 percent) and grows with the modular base as a power law. When we instead clamp the norm to a fixed multiple rho of Wc and hold it there, the network still groks, but the delay follows T_grok proportional to exp(alpha rho). One exponent, alpha near 7.5, fits this delay across four moduli (R^2 = 0.996). Over the swept ranges the held norm moves the delay by about 19x and the learning rate by only about 2x, and holding the norm above Wc slows grokking rather than preventing it. A final LayerNorm removes the dependence by decoupling weight scale from the network function; without it the exponential law returns. This pinned-norm delay is the exponential counterpart to the logarithmic delay predicted for a freely contracting norm.

AI.tldr: Grokking is governed by weight norm: a critical norm Wc triggers delayed generalization; constraining the norm yields an exponential delay T_grok ~ exp(alpha rho); LayerNorm decouples scale, removing norm dependence; the two views cohere into a norm-driven picture of grokking.
AI.motivation: Resolve whether weight norm causally controls grokking and reconcile conflicting observations by actively manipulating norm during training.
AI.method: Intervene on norm during training: (1) train with free weight decay to identify a robust critical norm Wc; (2) clamp the norm to rho*Wc and observe grokking delay; (3) sweep across modular bases, seeds, and learning rates; (4) test LayerNorm and ablations to decouple scale; (5) fit the relation T_grok ~ exp(alpha rho) and compare across moduli.
AI.result: Under free training, grokking occurs when norm reaches Wc, with Wc robust across seeds/learning rates (CV ~1–2%) and growing with the modular base via a power law. Clamping the norm to rho*Wc preserves grokking but yields delay T_grok ~ exp(alpha rho), with alpha ≈ 7.5 (R^2 ≈ 0.996) across four moduli. Over the swept ranges, norm-hold changes delay by ~19x while learning rate changes by ~2x; holding the norm above Wc slows but does not prevent grokking. LayerNorm decouples weight scale from network function, removing the exponential dependence; without LayerNorm, the exponential law reappears.
AI.conclusion: There are two complementary views of grokking depending on norm dynamics: a freely contracting norm predicts a logarithmic delay, while a pinned-norm perspective yields an exponential delay. The dominant factor is weight norm behavior; LayerNorm can remove this dependence, clarifying the mechanism by which grokking occurs.

title: WorkBench Revisited: Workplace Agents Two Years On
summary: The best agent on WorkBench in March 2024, GPT-4, completed 43% of tasks and took an unintended harmful action, such as emailing the wrong person, on 26% of them. We re-visit the benchmark in June 2026 and find that the best agent to date, Claude Opus 4.8, completes 89% and takes an unintended harmful action on 2.5%. Aside from this considerable progress in frontier agent performance, three things stand out. First, capability and safety go together on WorkBench rather than trade off, so the models that finish the most tasks also do the least unintended damage. Second, while several classes of error have been totally eliminated, frontier models still make some basic mistakes that occasionally result in irreversible harm, such as sending an email to the wrong person. Third, the rise of open-weight models has drastically lowered costs for a performance level that was previously only accessible to proprietary models, while frontier costs have stayed relatively stable. We release an updated version of the benchmark with data and code quality improvements, new model scores, and analysis of agent progress on WorkBench since 2024.

AI.tldr: Frontier agents on WorkBench show strong gains in task completion with safety improving alongside capability. GPT-4 (2024) completed 43% of tasks with 26% unintended harmful actions; Claude Opus 4.8 (2026) reaches 89% completion with 2.5% harm. Open-weight models reduce costs while frontier costs stay stable. Updated benchmark released with improved data/code quality, new scores, and progress analysis.
AI.motivation: Evaluate how leading agents balance task performance and safety, track progress over time, and understand cost dynamics, to guide alignment and safety research.
AI.method: Reevaluate the WorkBench benchmark with updated data (2024 vs 2026), quantify task completion rates and unintended harmful actions, analyze error classes (including irreversible harm), compare proprietary vs open-weight models, and report updated scores and benchmark enhancements.
AI.result: 2026 best agent Claude Opus 4.8 achieves 89% task completion with 2.5% unintended harmful actions, indicating safety and capability improvements correlate. Several error classes have been eliminated, but basic mistakes causing irreversible harm (e.g., emailing the wrong recipient) persist. Open-weight models reduce costs for high performance; frontier costs remain relatively stable. The benchmark has been updated with data/code quality improvements and new model scores.
AI.conclusion: The trajectory shows safety and capability improve together on WorkBench, though irreversibility risks remain. Open-weight models democratize access to frontier performance, but continued focus on error taxonomy and mitigations is needed; the updated benchmark will aid tracking future progress.

title: How do Self-Supervised Remote Sensing Vision Models Transfer to Downstream Tasks?
summary: Self-supervised geospatial foundation models (GeoFMs) learn transferable representations from remote sensing data, but their downstream behavior is difficult to characterize. We study six representative GeoFMs spanning joint-embedding, reconstruction, and multimodal pretraining families, and evaluate transfer across classification, regression, and segmentation benchmarks under different label availability and downstream pipelines.
