AI.method: Reproduction of results, ablation experiments to isolate components (chord window δ, chord transport, proximal alignment), and simplification to interpret their roles; analysis on how each component operates with respect to image noise and frequency content.
AI.result: a) δ acts as a timestep shift; b) chord transport edits high-noise images and primarily affects low-frequency content; c) proximal alignment works on low-noise images and adds high-frequency target details.
AI.conclusion: The editing process is best viewed as a coarse transport stage plus a fine alignment stage; this supports developing adaptive, prompt-conditioned dynamic timesteps; code and results are available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: Self-Improving VLA Policies: Selected Diffusion Noise for Spurious-Robust Action Smoothing
summary: Diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies enable strong generalization in robotic manipulation, but remain sensitive to spurious visual correlations and noisy action generation, leading to brittle behavior under perturbations. We introduce Selected Diffusion Noise (SDN), a simple, training-free test-time method that improves both robustness and success rate by leveraging the diffusion noise space as a controllable degree of freedom. SDN dynamically samples noise vectors that are maximally separated from a reference set to mitigate reliance on spurious cues, while selecting candidates that yield more coherent action trajectories. This dual objective encourages stable behavior even under object-masked observations and reduces action jitter without modifying model parameters. We evaluate SDN on two simulation benchmarks (Google Robot, Widow-X) and two real-world robotic datasets across multiple VLA policies, including pi_0, Groot-N1.5, and Groot-N1.6. SDN consistently improves success rates by +8% in simulation and +10% in real-world settings, while producing smoother and more stable actions. Our results highlight that diffusion noise selection can serve as an effective and general mechanism for enhancing VLA policies at test time.

AI.tldr: SDN is a training-free, test-time method that selects diffusion noise vectors to improve robustness and success in diffusion-based VLA policies, without retraining.
AI.motivation: VLA policies for robotic manipulation suffer from brittleness due to spurious visual cues and noisy action generation; there is a need for a test-time mechanism to stabilize behavior under perturbations and partial observations.
AI.method: At test time, dynamically sample diffusion noise vectors that are maximally separated from a reference set to reduce reliance on spurious cues, and select candidates yielding more coherent action trajectories; no model parameters are updated; evaluated across simulation benchmarks and real-world datasets on multiple VLA policies.
AI.result: SDN yields consistent improvements in success rates (+8% in simulation, +10% in real-world) and produces smoother, more stable actions across several VLA policies and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-noise selection is a general, training-free mechanism to enhance robustness and success of diffusion-based VLA policies, effective under perturbations and object-masked observations.

title: SpikF-GO: Spiking Fourier Graph Operators for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
comment: 23 pages, 2 figures, 11 tables. Accepted for presentation at ECML PKDD 2026. Code: https://github.com/jafarbakhshaliyev/SpikF-GO
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have emerged as an energy-efficient alternative to conventional neural networks, demonstrating strong performance in computer vision and robotics. More recently, SNNs have been applied to time series forecasting (TSF), with methods exploring spiking temporal backbones, spike-compatible positional encodings, Fourier-domain processing, and redesigned neuron dynamics. However, existing SNN forecasting approaches process variables independently, lacking explicit mechanisms for modeling inter-variable dependencies. This is a critical limitation in multivariate settings, where cross-variable correlations carry substantial predictive information. We propose Spiking Fourier Graph Operators (SpikF-GO), which addresses this gap by combining a hypervariate graph formulation in which every scalar observation becomes a graph node with spike-driven spectral processing. SpikF-GO introduces a Hard Concrete frequency gate for learnable sparse frequency selection and a Complex LIF gate that applies independent spiking neurons to real and imaginary Fourier components, preserving binary, event-driven computation throughout the spectral domain. We further present a variant incorporating Central Pattern Generator-based positional encodings for stronger long-range temporal modeling. Evaluated on eight benchmarks under a unified experimental protocol, SpikF-GO achieves the best average rank among all SNN methods and outperforms its ANN counterpart, FourierGNN, at reduced energy cost. SpikF-GO maintains competitive accuracy even at substantially smaller embedding dimensions, thereby achieving significant energy reductions. To our knowledge, this is among the first works to bring graph-based multivariate modeling into the spiking domain for TSF and the first to provide a unified comparison across SNN forecasting architectures under a common experimental protocol.

AI.tldr: SpikF-GO introduces a spike-driven, graph-based, multivariate TSF framework that jointly models inter-variable dependencies with spectral processing, using a Hard Concrete frequency gate and a Complex LIF gate, achieving state-of-the-art SNN performance with reduced energy and competitive accuracy at smaller embeddings.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of existing SNN forecasting methods that treat variables independently and fail to capture cross-variable dependencies in multivariate time series, while maintaining energy-efficient, event-driven computation.
AI.method: Propose Spiking Fourier Graph Operators (SpikF-GO): a hypervariate graph where each scalar observation is a graph node, coupled with spike-driven spectral processing. Introduces a Hard Concrete frequency gate for learnable sparse frequency selection and a Complex LIF gate that applies independent spiking neurons to real and imaginary Fourier components; optional Central Pattern Generator-based positional encodings to enhance long-range temporal modeling.
AI.result: Evaluated on eight benchmarks under a unified protocol; achieves the best average rank among SNN methods and outperforms FourierGNN (an ANN baseline) with lower energy cost. Maintains competitive accuracy at substantially smaller embedding dimensions, enabling significant energy reductions.
AI.conclusion: This work pioneers graph-based multivariate modeling in the spiking domain for time-series forecasting and provides a unified comparison across SNN forecasting architectures under a common experimental protocol.

title: Formalizing Numerical Analysis: An Agent Pipeline and Quality Audit Beyond Kernel Acceptance
summary: Recent work has demonstrated that coding agents can formalize entire advanced mathematics textbooks in Lean 4, yet existing efforts concentrate on branches of mathematics already well-represented in mathlib and measure success solely through kernel acceptance. We address both limitations by applying a coding agent to formalize Numerical Methods for Ordinary Differential Equations, a textbook in numerical analysis that is largely absent from mathlib, stressing the agent's capacity to develop new theory from scratch. We further introduce a systematic, reproducible three-dimensional framework for evaluating the quality of agent-produced formalizations beyond compilation: semantic correctness, Mathlib reuse, and cross-file reuse via LLM-as-judge methods. Applying this framework to our own formalization and to the released outputs of RepoProver and M2F, we uncover recurring unfaithful formalization patterns, including incomplete multi-part statements, added weakening hypotheses, and parameter restrictions, that kernel acceptance entirely obscures. Our results suggest that compilation-based metrics substantially overstate formalization quality, and we provide a reproducible audit methodology to support more rigorous evaluation of future autoformalization systems.

AI.tldr: Agent formalizes a numerical ODE methods textbook in Lean 4; reveals that kernel-based acceptance hides quality gaps; proposes a 3D evaluation framework and audit methodology; finds unfaithful patterns in RepoProver/M2F outputs, arguing compile-based metrics overstate quality.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of prior autoformalization work that prioritize mathlib-heavy topics and rely on kernel acceptance; evaluate agents' ability to create new theory from scratch and assess formalizations beyond compilation.
AI.method: Apply coding agent to formalize Numerical Methods for ODEs; develop a three-dimensional evaluation framework (semantic correctness, Mathlib reuse, cross-file reuse with LLM-as-judge); apply framework to the authors' own formalization and to RepoProver and M2F outputs; audit for unfaithful patterns.
AI.result: Found recurring unfaithful formalization patterns: incomplete multi-part statements, added weakening hypotheses, parameter restrictions; kernel acceptance obscures these issues; compilation-based metrics overstate quality; provide reproducible audit methodology.
AI.conclusion: Compilation-based metrics are insufficient; the proposed framework enables more rigorous evaluation of autoformalization; demonstrates agent's capacity to develop new theory from scratch and offers a reproducible audit for future systems.

title: WAM4D: Fast 4D World Action Model via Spatial Register Tokens
summary: World action models (WAMs) have recently shown promise in jointly modeling future observations and executable robot actions. However, most existing WAMs still operate in 2D video or latent spaces, where visually plausible rollouts miss the 3D spatial constraints and occluded contact geometry required for precise manipulation. While geometric foundation models offer strong priors for recovering dense 3D structure and motion from visual observations, forcing WAMs to predict the dense 4D representation introduces costly geometric decoding and slows down causal action generation. To address the trade-off, we present WAM4D, a fast 4D world action model that uses lightweight spatial register tokens as training-time future-depth readouts to transfer pretrained geometric priors into a causal video-action transformer, then removes the register branch for lightweight action inference. To prevent non-causal shortcuts, we further design causal mixture attention for the Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) WAM backbone, defining modality-specific visibility among video, action, and geometry tokens. Comprehensive experiments on RoboTwin 2.0 and challenging real-world manipulation tasks show that WAM4D improves spatial consistency and achieves competitive action prediction while maintaining efficient inference.

AI.tldr: WAM4D is a fast 4D world action model that injects geometric priors via training-time depth readouts into a causal video-action transformer, then uses lightweight inference without the extra branch, aided by causal mixture attention.
AI.motivation: Existing WAMs largely operate in 2D or latent spaces and lack explicit 3D spatial constraints; incorporating dense 4D representations is costly. Geometric priors help recover 3D structure but can slow inference. A method that transfers geometry priors into a causal WAM could improve manipulation with efficient inference.
AI.method: Introduce WAM4D: use lightweight spatial register tokens as training-time future-depth readouts to transfer pretrained geometric priors into a causal video-action transformer; remove the register branch at inference. Implement causal mixture attention for the Mixture-of-Transformers WAM backbone with modality-specific visibility among video, action, and geometry tokens.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on RoboTwin 2.0 and real-world manipulation tasks shows improved spatial consistency and competitive action prediction while maintaining efficient inference.
AI.conclusion: WAM4D achieves a favorable trade-off by embedding geometric priors into WAMs, enabling more spatially consistent, 3D-aware manipulation with fast, causal inference.

title: A Modular Dual-Arm Apple Harvesting Robot with Enhanced Field Performance
summary: Robotic apple harvesting offers a promising solution to labor shortages in commercial orchards, but low throughput and poor performance in orchard environments hinder its commercial adoption. This paper presents a modular dual-arm apple harvesting robot that uses a vertically stacked arms to enable simultaneous operation in the upper and lower zones of a single tree, simplifying platform positioning from multi-tree lateral repositioning to single-tree stops. Compared to our prior horizontal dual-arm system, the platform integrates 5 advances: (1)a foundation-model-based perception pipeline combining Grounding-DINO and EfficientViT-SAM for robust fruit localization in unstructured outdoor environments; (2)7th-order jerk-bounded trajectory generation paired with a Control Barrier Function safety filter to achieve fast yet safe arm motions; (3)a linear sweep harvesting strategy with a 10cm approach buffer and rotational detachment that improves picking reliability; (4)a temporal-logic-based dual-arm coordination policy with vision-arm async scheduling that maximizes usage of a shared vacuum source; and (5)field validation in 2 commercial orchards covering different apple varieties and tree architectures during the 2025 harvest season. Across the 1738 arm cycles collected in these field trials, the system achieved an 80.0% per-attempt success rate and a mean per-arm cycle time of 7.53s. Fruit damage assessments confirmed that 91.2% of robotically harvested fruit retained the highest USDA grade (Extra Fancy), with bruise rates between 2.4% and 4.9%. With further improvements in the picking cycle time and handling of heavy foliage occlusions, this new modular robot design holds promise for commercial harvesting of apples.

AI.tldr: A modular dual-arm apple harvesting robot with vertically stacked arms and multiple innovations improves throughput and fruit quality; field trials show competitive performance and high-fruit-grade yield, indicating commercial viability with further cycle-time and occlusion handling improvements.
AI.motivation: Address labor shortages and low throughput in commercial orchards; simplify positioning to single-tree stops; improve reliability and safety in unstructured outdoor environments.
AI.method: A vertically stacked dual-arm platform with a perception pipeline combining Grounding-DINO and EfficientViT-SAM for robust localization; 7th-order jerk-bounded trajectory with a Control Barrier Function safety filter; linear-sweep harvesting with a 10 cm buffer and rotational detachment; temporal-logic-based dual-arm coordination with vision-arm asynchronous scheduling; field validation in 2 commercial orchards across varieties and architectures; 1738 arm cycles.
AI.result: 80.0% per-attempt success; mean per-arm cycle time 7.53 s; 91.2% of harvested fruit rated Extra Fancy; bruise rates 2.4%-4.9%; validation across different varieties and trees during 2025 harvest season.
AI.conclusion: The modular vertical dual-arm design with integrated perception, planning, and safety demonstrates strong potential for commercial apple harvesting, with opportunities to further reduce cycle time and improve performance under dense foliage occlusion.

title: Smoothing Dark Areas in Molecular Latent Diffusion
summary: Latent diffusion is a promising framework for scalable 3D molecular generation, but it requires a latent space that remains smooth, valid, and navigable beyond posterior samples. Existing molecular VAEs, however, are typically learned through reconstruction-based objectives, which do not guarantee such a latent space. We show that this leads to dark areas: regions of latent space that are reachable during diffusion sampling but decode to disconnected or chemically invalid molecules. Unlike in image generation, molecular decoding requires strict structural and chemical precision, so even small latent perturbations can produce catastrophic failures. We therefore propose TopVAE, a topology-optimized VAE that reduces dark areas by making the decoder internalize structural and chemical constraints during training, eliminating the need for test-time chemical correction. TopVAE greatly improves off-posterior robustness, and when paired with a standard DiT, achieves $77\%$ lower FCD-3D on QM9, the highest V&C, $52\%$ lower FCD-3D on GEOM-Drugs, and $1.29{\times}$ more stable and connected molecules on zero-shot scaffold inpainting.

AI.tldr: Topology-optimized VAE (TopVAE) learns a latent space for molecular diffusion with reduced dark areas, enforcing chemical constraints during training to yield robust, valid molecules without test-time corrections.
AI.motivation: Latent spaces in molecular VAEs often contain dark regions where diffusion samples decode to invalid or disconnected molecules; unlike image domains, small latent perturbations can cause major chemical failures, so a latent space must be smooth, valid, and navigable beyond posterior samples.
AI.method: TopVAE trains the decoder to internalize structural and chemical constraints via topology-aware optimization, reducing dark areas; paired with a standard DiT diffusion model to enable robust off-posterior sampling without post hoc corrections.
AI.result: With DiT, achieves 77% lower FCD-3D on QM9 (highest validity and connectivity), 52% lower FCD-3D on GEOM-Drugs, and 1.29× more stable and connected molecules on zero-shot scaffold inpainting.
AI.conclusion: TopVAE mitigates latent-space dark areas by embedding chemical and structural constraints into the decoder during training, yielding a more navigable latent space for molecular diffusion and reducing the need for test-time corrections.

title: Applicability Condition Extraction for Therapeutic Drug-Disease Relations
summary: Identifying conditions that a certain drug takes therapeutic effect on a target disease is crucial for clinical decision-making support. However, most existing biomedical information extraction methods have focused on identifying only relations between drugs and diseases, while largely overlooking the context-specific conditions where such relations can apply. To address this problem, we introduce the task of applicability condition extraction for therapeutic drug--disease relations from biomedical research literature. We create the first dataset that has manually annotated triples of drugs, diseases, and applicability conditions on biomedical paper abstracts with 1,119 drug-disease pairs. Using this dataset, we systematically evaluate the performance of a range of existing methods. In addition, we propose a new method that enhances LoRA to consider relations between drugs and diseases. Our method consistently outperforms strong baselines across different evaluation settings. The source code and dataset of this paper can be obtained from: https://github.com/guantingluo98/Drug-ACE

AI.tldr: First dataset and method for extracting applicability conditions of drug–disease relations; introduces 1,119 annotated triples; a LoRA-enhanced model achieves state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To address the gap that existing biomedical information extraction (IE) methods focus on drug–disease relations but ignore context-specific applicability conditions; there is a need for a dataset and modeling approach to capture conditional applicability of therapies.
AI.method: Constructed the first manually annotated dataset of drug, disease, and applicability-condition triples from biomedical paper abstracts (1,119 drug–disease pairs). Systematically evaluated existing methods. Proposed a LoRA-enhanced model that explicitly considers drug–disease relations to predict applicability conditions.
AI.result: The proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines across multiple evaluation settings on the dataset.
AI.conclusion: Provides the first dataset and a superior method for applicability-condition extraction in therapeutic drug–disease relations; source code and dataset are available at the linked GitHub repository.

title: ShearFuse-UNet: Hadamard, DCT, and Shearlet Transform Fusion for Next-Day Wildfire Spread Prediction
summary: We propose ShearFuse-UNet, a lightweight and computationally efficient deep learning model for next-day wildfire spread prediction from multi-modal satellite data. The model integrates three complementary transform-domain branches inside each encoder block of a U-Net backbone: a 2D Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (WHT) branch, a 2D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) branch, and a cone-adapted digital Shearlet residual branch. The WHT and DCT branches establish orthogonal latent spaces with learnable spectral scaling and fixed soft-thresholding, while the Shearlet branch provides anisotropic, multi-directional feature decomposition that explicitly encodes the elongated edge structures characteristic of fire fronts. A learned SpectralFusion gate adaptively combines the WHT and DCT responses, and the Shearlet reconstruction is added as a residual. This three-branch design bears a loose structural analogy to transformer self-attention: the WHT and DCT branches provide complementary spectral representations that are adaptively fused, while the Shearlet branch contributes directional content through a residual pathway. Unlike self-attention, the proposed design relies on fixed mathematical transforms rather than learned projection operators, reducing parameter count and computational cost. Evaluated on the WildfireSpreadTS dataset, ShearFuse-UNet achieves an F1 score of 0.596 with only 267k parameters, outperforming a ResNet18-based U-Net (14M parameters, F1 = 0.589) and demonstrating a highly favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Results on the Google Next-Day Wildfire Spread dataset further validate these findings across a different benchmark.

AI.tldr: A lightweight three-branch UNet for next-day wildfire spread prediction using fixed transform-domain branches (WHT, DCT) plus a cone-adapted Shearlet residual branch. Spectral fusion combines the WHT and DCT; no learned projections in those branches, reducing parameters.
AI.motivation: Address the need for accurate and efficient wildfire spread forecasting from multi-modal satellite data with limited compute budget; replace or supplement costly self-attention with fixed, mathematically principled transforms to capture spectral and directional structure of fire fronts.
AI.method: In each encoder block of a U-Net, integrate three branches: (1) 2D Fast Walsh–Hadamard Transform (WHT) with learnable spectral scaling and fixed soft-thresholding; (2) 2D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) with orthogonal latent spaces and similar spectral handling; (3) a cone-adapted digital Shearlet residual branch capturing anisotropic, multi-directional features of fire fronts. A learned SpectralFusion gate adaptively fuses WHT and DCT responses; the Shearlet reconstruction is added as a residual. This aims to emulate attention-like aggregation using fixed transforms to reduce parameters and computation.
AI.result: On WildfireSpreadTS, achieves F1 = 0.596 with 267k parameters, outperforming a ResNet18-based U-Net (14M parameters, F1 = 0.589). Cross-dataset validation on Google Next-Day Wildfire Spread corroborates the findings, indicating strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: The three-branch transform-domain UNet demonstrates that fixed spectral and directional decompositions can rival attention-based architectures for wildfire spread forecasting while dramatically reducing parameter count and computation, with good cross-dataset generalization.

title: GAIT: Legged Robot Proprioceptive State Estimation with Attention over Inertial-Leg Tokens
summary: In this paper, we propose a method that applies Inertial-Leg (IL) tokenization to an attention-based network for proprioceptive state estimation in legged robots. Unlike existing learning-based state estimators that concatenate all sensor measurements into a single flat vector, the proposed architecture represents inertial measurements and leg-wise measurements as individual tokens and uses an attention mechanism to learn the relative importance of each measurement.This design allows the network to reweight each measurement according to the current contact condition, reflecting the fact that the reliability of forward kinematic measurements depends on whether the corresponding foot is in contact. Unlike conventional contact-aided estimators, however, the proposed method learns this behavior without relying on an explicit contact estimator or on explicit measurement updates based on a stationary contact assumption. To validate the proposed method, we conducted experiments on a Unitree Go1 robot, including debris terrain not modeled in simulation and gait patterns not seen during training. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves better estimation performance than existing learning-based state estimators under unseen gait patterns and also improves performance over contact-aided model-based methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces inertial-leg (IL) tokenization with attention for proprioceptive state estimation in legged robots, treating inertial and leg measurements as tokens to adaptively weigh inputs based on contact, without explicit contact estimation.
AI.motivation: Conventional estimators concatenate sensor data into a flat vector and rely on explicit contact assumptions or estimators. There is a need for robust, contact-aware state estimation that works under unseen terrains and gait patterns without explicit contact updates.
AI.method: Represent inertial and leg-wise sensor readings as individual tokens; use attention to learn the relative importance of each token, enabling reweighting based on current contact conditions. No explicit contact estimator or stationary-contact-based measurement updates. Validation on a Unitree Go1 with debris terrain and unseen gait patterns.
AI.result: Outperforms existing learning-based state estimators under unseen gait patterns and surpasses contact-aided model-based methods in estimation performance on tested scenarios.
AI.conclusion: IL tokenization with attention provides robust, contact-aware proprioceptive state estimation for legged robots without explicit contact modeling, generalizing to unseen terrains and gaits.

title: Can Machine Learning Forecast Rice Yields in Data-Constrained Settings? Satellite Climate Data, National Crop Statistics, and Lessons from Sierra Leone
summary: Sierra Leone's agriculture operates with almost no data-driven decision support, and no published machine learning study has examined the country's crop yields. We ask whether rice yield can be forecast from data Sierra Leone currently has. Using 25 years of FAOSTAT production data (2000-2024) for nine major crops, we train XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and Random Forest under a strict anti-leakage protocol with expanding-window walk-forward evaluation across seven held-out years, benchmarked against naive persistence. No model trained on crop statistics alone outperforms persistence. Augmenting with free satellite climate data (CHIRPS rainfall, NASA POWER temperature) reverses this result: a climate-only XGBoost reduces forecast error by one third (RMSE 284 vs 428 kg/ha), a gain that holds for a linear model and is robust to excluding the anomalous 2018 season. Early-season (May-June) rainfall is the dominant predictor, implying seasonal yield risk is observable months before harvest. No model anticipated the 2018 collapse, whose origins were institutional rather than climatic. We translate the findings into policy recommendations for Sierra Leone's Feed Salone Strategy, with a fully open-source pipeline.

AI.tldr: Climate data enables yield forecasting for Sierra Leone; crop-statistics alone underperform persistence; early-season rainfall key; open-source pipeline supports policy actions.
AI.motivation: Address the data gap in Sierra Leone agriculture by assessing whether available data can forecast crop yields and inform policy decisions.
AI.method: 25-year FAOSTAT yield data (2000–2024) for nine crops; models: XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, Random Forest; expanding-window walk-forward evaluation across seven held-out years; benchmark against naive persistence; experiments with/without CHIRPS rainfall and NASA POWER temperature; evaluate robustness and an anomalous 2018 season; provide an open-source pipeline.
AI.result: Crop-statistics-only models do not beat persistence. Augmenting with climate data yields a 33% RMSE reduction (284 vs 428 kg/ha) using climate-only XGBoost; gains persist with linear models and when excluding 2018. Early-season May–June rainfall emerges as the dominant predictor, enabling seasonal yield risk signals months before harvest. The 2018 collapse is attributed to institutional factors, not climate. Policy implications and an open-source forecasting pipeline are provided.
AI.conclusion: Climate information is critical for forecasting Sierra Leone crop yields; the findings inform the Feed Salone Strategy and demonstrate a fully open-source forecasting pipeline; non-climatic shocks should be considered in policy planning.

title: FactoryLLM: A Safe and Open-Source AI Playground for Evaluating LLMs in Smart Factories
summary: Fault diagnostics and recovery in smart factories is challenging because critical information is dispersed across manuals of multiple machines which are interconnected through the manufacturing process. Large Language Models (LLMs) can provide a promising approach. In this paper, we propose FactoryLLM, a safe and open-source AI playground designed for evaluating different LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models by analysing documents from multiple machines across the manufacturing process. FactoryLLM enables the user to configure the LLM, and assess performance when reasoning over multiple documents, through a dual evaluation setup using both RAGAS and NVIDIA's LLM-as-a-Judge metrics. FactoryLLM is safe because it allows users to run local or open-source LLMs without sharing sensitive industrial data, providing a controlled environment for experimentation. We demonstrate the efficacy of FactoryLLM through a case study which involves an Autonomous Intelligent Vehicle and its Mobile Planner software, evaluating three LLMs across 30 maintenance queries derived from approximately 600 pages of cross-machine documentation. The results suggest that FactoryLLM is effective in cross-machine document reasoning: every model achieved a groundedness score above 0.88. The full code and documentation for community to test FactoryLLM with their manufacturing specific scenarios are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Open-source, safe RAG evaluation playground (FactoryLLM) for cross-machine manufacturing docs; demonstrates high groundedness across three LLMs on 30 queries from ~600 pages; code available.
AI.motivation: Fault diagnostics in smart factories is hampered by dispersed information across interconnected machine manuals; a safe, configurable environment is needed to evaluate LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation on sensitive industrial documents.
AI.method: Develops FactoryLLM to configure LLMs and assess cross-document reasoning using multi-document corpora; employs dual evaluation with RAGAS and NVIDIA LLM-as-a-Judge metrics; includes a case study with an Autonomous Intelligent Vehicle and its Mobile Planner; 30 maintenance queries drawn from ~600 pages of documentation across machines.
AI.result: All tested models achieved groundedness scores above 0.88, indicating effective cross-machine document reasoning; demonstrates practical viability; open-source code and documentation are provided for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: FactoryLLM provides a safe, open, and reusable platform for evaluating RAG-based LLMs on manufacturing documentation, enabling reproducibility, privacy-preserving experimentation, and broader testing across manufacturing scenarios.

title: Diffusion-Refined Segmentation and Vision-Language Interpretation for Pediatric Brain Tumor MRI
summary: Accurate pediatric brain tumor segmentation remains challenging due to limited annotated data, heterogeneous imaging phenotypes, diffuse tumor boundaries, and class imbalance across tumor subregions. Here, we present a two-stage deep learning framework for improving multi-modal pediatric brain MRI segmentation and clinical interpretation. First, we evaluate 3D Res U-Net and Swin-UNETR baselines on BraTS-PEDs MRI scans, using four co-registered modalities to predict tumor core, whole tumor, and enhancing tumor regions. Second, we introduce diffusion-based refinement models conditioned on coarse Swin-UNETR predictions, including a 3D DDPM refiner and MedSegDiff. Conditioning substantially improves diffusion stability and performance, particularly for enhancing tumor boundary segmentation. Conditioned MedSegDiff achieves the strongest boundary agreement with the lowest HD95. Finally, predicted tumor volumes and representative segmentation overlays are integrated with a multimodal language model to generate structured radiology-style reports. Together, our results suggest that coarse-to-refined diffusion segmentation can improve pediatric tumor boundary delineation and support end-to-end interpretable AI-assisted neuro-oncology workflows.

AI.tldr: Two-stage diffusion-based refinement improves pediatric brain tumor segmentation; conditioned diffusion (MedSegDiff) on coarse Swin-UNETR predictions enhances boundary delineation and enables radiology-style reports via a multimodal LM.
AI.motivation: Overcome limited annotated data, heterogeneous imaging, diffuse tumor boundaries, and class imbalance in pediatric brain tumors; aim to produce accurate, interpretable segmentation and clinically useful reports.
AI.method: Stage 1: evaluate 3D Res U-Net and Swin-UNETR baselines on BraTS-PEDs with four modalities to segment tumor core, whole tumor, enhancing tumor. Stage 2: diffusion-based refiners conditioned on coarse Swin-UNETR outputs: 3D DDPM refiner and MedSegDiff; conditioning improves diffusion stability and boundary accuracy; evaluate via HD95; integrate predicted volumes and overlays with a multimodal language model to generate radiology-style reports.
AI.result: Conditioned MedSegDiff achieves strongest boundary agreement with lowest HD95; diffusion refinement notably improves enhancing tumor boundary; coarse-to-refined approach yields better boundary delineation; radiology reports generated.
AI.conclusion: Coarse-to-refined diffusion segmentation can improve pediatric tumor boundary delineation and enable end-to-end interpretable AI-assisted neuro-oncology workflows.

title: Robustness without Wrinkles: Parallel Simulation and Robust MPC for Certified Deformable Manipulation
summary: We present CORD-SLS, a real-time control method for safe deformable object manipulation, with a focus on ropes and cloth. At its core is a GPU-parallel differentiable simulator with contact smoothing which enables efficient gradient-based planning through intermittent contact. To robustly satisfy constraints under model and sensing uncertainty, we develop a real-time, GPU-parallel output-feedback robust model predictive control (MPC) algorithm that plans with this simulator. We further show that the simulator accelerates model-based RL for training neural manipulation policies. To improve real-world robustness, we use conformal prediction to calibrate visual-feedback and perception-error bounds for MPC, producing reachable tubes that enable high-probability safe control. We evaluate CORD-SLS on high-dimensional, contact-rich rope and cloth manipulation tasks in simulation and hardware, including obstacle avoidance, routing, folding, and smoothing. Across settings, CORD-SLS achieves millisecond-speed planning, exceeding baselines in safety, speed, and task success.

AI.tldr: GPU-parallel differentiable simulator-based MPC enables real-time, safe deformable-object manipulation (rope/cloth) with uncertainty-aware planning via conformal-prediction bounds; achieves millisecond planning and outperforms baselines in safety and task success, in simulation and hardware.
AI.motivation: Safe, real-time manipulation of deformable objects under model and sensing uncertainty; overcome challenges of contact-rich, high-dimensional manipulation by leveraging differentiable simulation and robust planning, and improve real-world robustness with probabilistic perceptual bounds.
AI.method: GPU-parallel differentiable simulator with contact smoothing; real-time output-feedback robust MPC that plans using this simulator; conformal prediction to calibrate visual-feedback and perception-error bounds, forming reachable tubes for high-probability safe control; also shows accelerator for model-based RL training; evaluated on rope and cloth tasks with obstacles, routing, folding, smoothing in simulation and hardware.
AI.result: Millisecond-scale planning; improved safety, speed, and task success over baselines across rope and cloth tasks; demonstrated in simulation and hardware, including obstacle avoidance, routing, folding, and smoothing.
AI.conclusion: CORD-SLS provides a scalable, real-time, uncertainty-aware framework for safe deformable-object manipulation and accelerates model-based RL; conformal-prediction calibration strengthens real-world robustness through reachable tubes for MPC.

title: PostDeg: Placement Beats Parameterization in LayerNorm GNNs
comment: Yash Tomar and Aryav Das contributed equally to this work
summary: LayerNorm-based GNNs routinely erase the topology signals (degree, centrality, $k$-core) that node-selection policies should depend on, but the literature has not located where in the residual block the erasure happens. We answer that question: a positive per-node scalar inserted before LayerNorm is divided out up to a stabilizer term, while the same scalar inserted after LayerNorm reaches the score head as representation magnitude. The surviving slot is the post-LayerNorm position. We instantiate it with PostDeg, a parameter-free post-LayerNorm inverse-degree scale, and pre-register four falsifiers (graphwise scalars, extra LayerNorm, expressive same-slot capacity, backbone-agnostic source) that would reject the rule. PostDeg gains $+3.5\%/+2.5\%/+5.6\%$ over the LN backbone on influence maximization, network dismantling, and maximum independent set, with $10/10$ paired-seed wins per task; none of the four falsifiers fires. The takeaway is that placement, not parameterization, carries the gain -- a small invariance check that generalizes to any positive topology scalar in any normalized residual stack.

AI.tldr: Post-LayerNorm placement of a positive topology scalar (PostDeg) in LN-based GNNs improves performance on several graph tasks, with gains suggesting placement, not parameterization, drives benefits; four robustness falsifiers do not refute the claim.
AI.motivation: Identify where topology signals are erased in LN-based GNN residual blocks and determine whether performance gains come from scalar placement or parameterization, with robustness checks to falsify the claim.
AI.method: Introduce a post-LN, parameter-free inverse-degree scalar (PostDeg) placed after LayerNorm in the residual path; compare against LN backbone across influence maximization, network dismantling, and maximum independent set; design four falsifiers (graphwise scalars, extra LayerNorm, expressive same-slot capacity, backbone-agnostic source) to challenge the rule.
AI.result: PostDeg yields consistent gains (+3.5%, +2.5%, +5.6% across tasks) with 10/10 paired-seed wins; none of the four falsifiers trigger, indicating the gain stems from placement, not parameterization.
AI.conclusion: Placement of a topology scalar post-LN in residual blocks is the key driver of gains in LN-based GNNs; the claim generalizes to any positive topology scalar in a normalized residual stack and is robust to proposed falsifiers.

title: VeriGeo: Controllable Geometry Question Generation with Numerical and Analytical Verification
summary: Geometry problem generation is useful for AI-assisted education and multimodal mathematical reasoning, but reliable synthesis remains difficult because the problem statement, diagram, constraints, and solution should be mutually consistent. Existing methods often trade off controllability and reliability: seed-based rewriting is flexible but weakly verifiable, whereas diagram-first construction improves validity but is less suited to arbitrary user-specified constraints. We introduce VeriGeo, a controllable geometry generation framework grounded in executable reasoning traces. Given user constraints such as target concepts and difficulty, an Author agent generates a problem and diagram, and a Solver agent produces a proof-aligned solution. Both agents use a shared action sequence that connects natural language, diagrams, geometric constraints, and proof steps into a verifiable representation. A three-stage pipeline checks numerical consistency, analytical realizability, and global consistency, using verification-guided reflection to repair recoverable failures and reject unrecoverable ones. Across five LLM backbones, raw generations frequently fail these checks, while VeriGeo repairs a substantial fraction of the invalid attempts. Supervised fine-tuning on 8.7k examples generated by VeriGeo achieves the best reported GeoQA performance among end-to-end multimodal LLM-based solvers, and obtains strong results on PGPS9K and MathVista-GPS, demonstrating the effectiveness of verified synthetic data for improving multimodal geometry reasoning.

AI.tldr: VeriGeo is a verification-guided, controllable geometry problem generation framework that uses author and solver agents with a shared action sequence and a three-stage verification pipeline to ensure numerical, analytical, and global consistency; it repairs or rejects invalid generations and, after supervised fine-tuning on 8.7k synthetic examples, achieves top GeoQA performance among end-to-end multimodal LLM solvers and strong results on geometry benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To improve the reliability and controllability of geometry problem generation by enforcing cross-modal consistency among statements, diagrams, constraints, and solutions, addressing the fragility of seed-based rewriting and the validation gaps in diagram-first methods.
AI.method: Two agents (Author and Solver) operate via a common action sequence that links natural language, diagrams, geometric constraints, and proof steps into a verifiable representation. A three-stage pipeline (numerical consistency, analytical realizability, global consistency) with verification-guided reflection repairs recoverable failures and reject unrecoverable ones. The approach is evaluated across five LLM backbones; 8.7k VeriGeo-generated examples are used for supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Raw generations frequently fail the checks across LLM backbones; VeriGeo repairs a substantial fraction of invalid attempts. Supervised fine-tuning yields the best reported GeoQA performance among end-to-end multimodal LLM-based solvers and achieves strong results on PGPS9K and MathVista-GPS.
AI.conclusion: Verified synthetic data coupled with a verification-guided generation pipeline effectively improves multimodal geometry reasoning and reliability, demonstrating the viability of VeriGeo for scalable, controllable geometry problem generation.

title: Clay-CNN Hybrids: Leveraging Geo-Foundational Models as Auxiliary Context for Landslide Detection
summary: Rapid post-event landslide mapping is essential for disaster response but remains difficult to automate due to extreme class imbalance. This study evaluates whether Clay v1.5, a Geo-Foundational Model (GFM), can improve pixel-level landslide segmentation on the Landslide4Sense (L4S) benchmark, which contains 3,799 training chips with 14 Sentinel-2 and terrain bands and approximately 2% positive pixels. We compare three strategies: Clay as the primary encoder with multi-scale residual terrain fusion, a U-Net backbone augmented with Clay semantic context at the bottleneck, and a standard U-Net baseline. The hybrid U-Net + Clay model with two-stage Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) achieved the best test F1 of 64.5 +/- 1.8% over three seeds, surpassing the Clay-only backbone (55.2 +/- 3.6%) and the U-Net baseline (59.9%). Clay as a standalone encoder underperformed the U-Net due to the absence of multi-scale skip connections, but its pretrained representations consistently improved performance when injected as auxiliary context. These findings suggest that GFMs are most effective for landslide detection when they complement spatially detailed convolutional architectures rather than replace them.

AI.tldr: GFMs (Clay v1.5) boost pixel-level landslide segmentation when used as auxiliary context to a U-Net, with a two-stage LoRA-based hybrid achieving the best F1 ~64.5%, outperforming stand-alone Clay and standard U-Net.
AI.motivation: Address extreme class imbalance and the need for rapid post-event landslide mapping; evaluate Clay v1.5 (GFM) on Landslide4Sense for pixel-level segmentation.
AI.method: Compare three strategies: (1) Clay as primary encoder with multi-scale residual terrain fusion; (2) U-Net backbone augmented with Clay semantic context at the bottleneck; (3) standard U-Net baseline. Include a hybrid U-Net + Clay model using two-stage Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Dataset: Landslide4Sense with 3,799 training chips, 14 Sentinel-2 and terrain bands, ~2% positive pixels. Evaluation across three seeds; best test F1 reported.
AI.result: Hybrid U-Net + Clay with two-stage LoRA achieves test F1 = 64.5% ± 1.8% over three seeds, outperforming Clay-only backbone (55.2% ± 3.6%) and U-Net baseline (59.9%). Clay as a standalone encoder underperforms the U-Net due to the absence of multi-scale skip connections, but pretrained Clay representations improve performance when injected as auxiliary context.
AI.conclusion: GFMs are most effective for landslide detection when they complement spatially detailed convolutional architectures rather than replace them; GFMs provide robust gains when used as auxiliary context in imbalanced segmentation tasks.

authors: Lars A. T. H. van Alen
René M. J. G. van de Molengraft
title: Short-Horizon Position Accuracy of Single-Track Models: Implications for Motion Planning of Autonomous Vehicles
comment: Submitted to The International Journal of Automotive Engineering, Official Journal of the Society of Automotive Engineers of Japan, Inc. (JSAE)
summary: Accurate and computationally efficient vehicle models are essential for motion planning of autonomous vehicles, where positional accuracy directly affects trajectory feasibility and safety. However, the positional accuracy has not been systematically evaluated against real measurements. Therefore, this paper compares the short-horizon positional accuracy of three single-track vehicle models against vehicle measurements across various driving maneuvers. Model parameters are identified through dedicated experiments with the instrumented test vehicle. Rather than identifying a single best model, this work aims to provide insight into the trade-offs between model complexity, parameterization quality, and positional accuracy for informed model selection in Model Predictive Control applications.

AI.tldr: Three single-track vehicle models are evaluated for short-horizon positional accuracy against instrumented-vehicle measurements; parameters are identified experimentally; the study emphasizes trade-offs between model complexity, parameter quality, and accuracy for MPC model selection rather than declaring a single best model.
AI.motivation: Accurate short-horizon position tracking is critical for motion planning and safety in MPC-based autonomous driving. There is a lack of systematic evaluation of how different single-track models perform against real measurements.
AI.method: Identify parameters for three single-track models using dedicated experiments on an instrumented test vehicle. Collect data over diverse driving maneuvers. Compare short-horizon positional accuracy against measurements. Analyze trade-offs between model complexity, parameterization quality, and accuracy to inform model choice for MPC.
AI.result: Quantitative comparison of positional errors across maneuvers for the three models; no single model is deemed universally best. The study clarifies how complexity and parameter accuracy relate to positional accuracy, providing guidance for MPC model selection.
AI.conclusion: Offers insights into the trade-offs between model complexity, parameterization quality, and positional accuracy, aiding informed model selection for Model Predictive Control in autonomous vehicle planning.

title: Utility-Constrained Policy Optimization
summary: Constrained MDPs (CMDPs) are a widely adopted framework for incorporating safety into RL agents; however, the framework does not support risk-sensitive constraints. This can be problematic: For example, CMDPs allow for optimal solutions that, in order to satisfy the risk-neutral constraints, mix infrequent catastrophic behaviors and frequent, overly conservative ones. Moreover, prior empirical results suggest that enforcing stricter, risk-sensitive constraints can improve performance even under risk-neutral evaluation. The natural framework to incorporate risk-sensitive constraints is utility-constrained MDPs (UCMDPs), but no practical solutions for this problem existed. In this work, we introduce a simple yet powerful methodology for UCMDPs and constrained RL. Besides allowing for risk-sensitive constraints, our framework does not require us to fix constraint limits in advance of training the agent, provided that a sensible range is known. This increases policy flexibility and, in practice, allows for adjustments to these limits at no extra training cost. Besides benefiting from the generality of the framework, our agent shows strong performance in practice, consistently matching or outperforming existing baselines in several Safety Gymnasium benchmark tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces utility-constrained MDPs (UCMDPs) for risk-sensitive RL, a practical framework that allows adjustable constraint limits during training, and demonstrates strong results on Safety Gymnasium tasks.
AI.motivation: CMDPs are risk-neutral and can yield policies that mix rare catastrophic behaviors with overly conservative ones; risk-sensitive constraints can improve performance even under risk-neutral evaluation; there is a gap for practical solutions that handle risk-sensitivity in RL.
AI.method: Presents a simple, general methodology for UCMDPs and constrained RL that does not require fixing constraint limits before training, requiring only a known sensible range for limits; allows adjusting limits during training at no extra training cost.
AI.result: Empirically, the agent consistently matches or outperforms baselines on Safety Gymnasium benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: UCMDP provides a flexible and practical framework for risk-aware constrained RL, extending CMDPs to risk-sensitive constraints and enabling limits to be adjusted without retraining.

title: When Should Agent Trust Be Conditional? Characterizing and Attacking Skill-Conditional Reputation in Agent Swarms
summary: Open platforms increasingly route tasks among heterogeneous LLM agents--differing in base model, scaffold, and tool stack--whose competence varies sharply by skill: an agent excellent at one skill may be useless at another. The standard reputation approach summarizes each agent by a single global trust score, but that scalar is the wrong object here, because routing every task to the globally most-trusted agent leaves the value of specialization unclaimed. We study skill-conditional trust R(i | k)--the trust to place in agent i for a task requiring skill k, rather than one score per agent--and pose three falsifiable questions: when is conditioning worth it, how much cross-skill evidence should be borrowed, and whether that borrowing is safe. A controlled phase-diagram analysis answers the first two: conditional trust wins only in a specific regime--high agent heterogeneity, sparse per-skill evidence, and correlated skills--and the coupling strength beta that buys this data efficiency is dual-use, because the same cross-skill borrowing is also a laundering channel. On a public benchmark of 14 genuinely heterogeneous AppWorld agents, real pools land inside the beneficial regime--a small but genuine gain, with the per-skill best agent genuinely changing across skills. We then show that an attacker with cheap evidence in one skill and none in a target skill hijacks the conditional router, driving routing regret from 0 to 0.94 on a pool our zero-cost Conditional Information Value Test (CIVT) rates GREEN--while the ungated trust verdict it contaminates reads -0.06 instead of the honest +0.19. A zero-evidence gate bounds the attack but does not eliminate it; we characterize the residual cost under an explicit budget. We do not claim Sybil-resistance--we quantify the trade-off.

AI.tldr: Skill-conditional trust assigns task routing based on agent performance for each skill, not a single global trust score. A phase-diagram shows when cross-skill evidence helps; results show modest gains but vulnerabilities to cheap, cross-skill attacks. The work balances data efficiency with security risks.
AI.motivation: Open platforms route tasks to heterogeneous LLM agents with varying skill proficiency. A single global trust score wastes specialization. Understanding when per-skill trust is beneficial and how to bound risks is needed.
AI.method: Theoretical phase-diagram analysis to compare unconditional vs skill-conditioned trust under varying agent heterogeneity, per-skill evidence, and skill correlations; introduce cross-skill evidence parameter beta; evaluate on a public AppWorld benchmark (14 agents); analyze attack scenarios where an attacker leverages cheap evidence in one skill to manipulate routing in another; propose a zero-evidence gate via Conditional Information Value Test (CIVT) to gauge risk; quantify trade-offs and residual costs under budgeted evidence.
AI.result: Conditional trust yields gains only in regimes with high heterogeneity, sparse per-skill evidence, and correlated skills; beta enables data efficiency but also facilitates laundering across skills; on AppWorld, gains are small but real with best-agent changing across skills; attackers can drive routing regret up to 0.94 even with a zero-cost CIVT-green gate, while ungated trust misreads as +0.19; a zero-evidence gate bounds attack but residual cost remains under budget constraints.
AI.conclusion: Skill-conditioned trust can outperform global trust in specific regimes, but introduces security vulnerabilities via cross-skill evidence flow. The authors quantify a practical trade-off and stop short of asserting Sybil-resistance, highlighting the need for careful defense against laundering-like attacks.

title: FEMOT: Multi-Object Tracking using Frame and Event Cameras
summary: Conventional RGB cameras have been widely used in multi-object tracking due to their ability to capture rich appearance and semantic information. However, their performance is often degraded under complex real-world challenges, such as motion blur, low illumination, and overexposure. Bio-inspired event cameras offer high temporal resolution and high dynamic range, providing complementary cues under extreme scenarios. Nevertheless, RGB-event multi-object tracking remains underexplored due to the lack of large-scale and well-annotated datasets. To address this issue, we propose FEMOT, a large-scale RGB-event multi-object tracking dataset that covers diverse real-world scenarios and 14 challenging attributes. With both RGB and event data as well as high-quality annotations, FEMOT provides a reliable platform for systematically evaluating RGB-event multi-object tracking methods. Based on FEMOT, we retrain and evaluate over ten strong trackers, thereby establishing a comprehensive benchmark for future research. Furthermore, we propose FEMOTR, a multimodal tracking framework that decouples RGB and event features and fuses them in the frequency domain, thereby effectively exploiting their complementary characteristics for robust object localization and identity association. Extensive experiments on FEMOT and DSEC-MOT datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The source code and benchmark dataset have been released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/FEMOT.

AI.tldr: A large-scale RGB-event MOT dataset (FEMOT) with 14 attributes and a multimodal tracker (FEMOTR) that fuses RGB and event data in the frequency domain, establishing benchmarks for RGB-event MOT and demonstrating improved robustness; code and dataset released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large-scale, well-annotated RGB-event MOT data and exploit complementary cues from event cameras to improve tracking under challenging conditions (motion blur, low light, overexposure).
AI.method: Construct FEMOT: a RGB-event MOT dataset with diverse real-world scenarios and 14 attributes, with high-quality annotations; evaluate over ten existing trackers retrained on FEMOT; propose FEMOTR, a multimodal tracker that decouples RGB and event features and fuses them in the frequency domain to enhance localization and ID association.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on FEMOT and DSEC-MOT demonstrate the effectiveness of FEMOTR and the utility of FEMOT as a benchmark; the authors released source code and dataset.
AI.conclusion: FEMOT provides a reliable platform for RGB-event MOT benchmarking, and FEMOTR offers a strong baseline that leverages complementary RGB-event cues via frequency-domain fusion, accelerating future RGB-event MOT research.

title: Universal Manipulation Exoskeleton: Learning Compliant Whole-body Policies with Real-time Torque Feedback
summary: For robots to work safely in household environments, they need to be compliant and react to torque and force feedback during contact. However, the majority of existing data collection pipelines still lack the ability to capture force and torque data for learning active compliant policies. In this paper, we present Universal Manipulation Exoskeleton (UME), an upper-limb exoskeleton that provides real-time haptic torque feedback while recording whole-arm configurations and joint torque signals for teleoperation. With transparent torque feedback, human operators can even unsheathe kinematically constrained objects while blindfolded. UME is low-cost, lightweight, and portable. Equipped with an embedded IMU, it enables teleoperation for mobile manipulation. With our proposed universal retargeting algorithm, UME can teleoperate a range of robots, including the 7DoF OpenArm, 7DoF Franka, and 6DoF X-ARM. We demonstrate that this combination of capabilities enables learning bimanual, whole-body, and active compliant policies that operate effectively in highly constrained spaces. The learned robust autonomous policies achieve high success rates across a variety of tasks, including long-horizon mobile manipulation, force-mediated box flipping, visually occluded box pushing, and space-constrained tabletop manipulation. Videos, code, and additional information can be found at https://ume-exo.github.io.

AI.tldr: UME: an upper-limb exoskeleton providing real-time haptic torque feedback while recording full-arm configurations and joint torque for teleoperation; enables learning of bimanual, whole-body, active compliant policies; features universal retargeting to multiple robots and high-performance tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of force/torque data capture in data pipelines for learning compliant policies in household/mobility manipulation; enable safe, interactive human-robot collaboration in constrained spaces.
AI.method: Introduce Universal Manipulation Exoskeleton (UME) with transparent torque feedback, embedded IMU, and a universal retargeting algorithm to teleoperate diverse robots (7DoF OpenArm, 7DoF Franka, 6DoF X-ARM). Collects whole-arm configurations and joint torques during teleoperation and supports learning of active compliant policies. Demonstrates unsheathing constrained objects blindfolded and applies to mobile manipulation scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust autonomous policies with high success across tasks: long-horizon mobile manipulation, force-mediated box flipping, visually occluded box pushing, and space-constrained tabletop manipulation. Shows effective learning from UME data and strong cross-robot transfer via retargeting. Resources (videos, code) available at project site.
AI.conclusion: UME enables rich data collection for active compliant policy learning and broad teleoperation across robot platforms; universal retargeting expands applicability to diverse hardware, advancing safe, compliant manipulation in constrained environments.

title: Decompose Sparsely Where You Should, Absorb Densely Where You Should No
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are typically trained to reconstruct the \textbf{entire} residual stream through a sparse dictionary, implicitly assuming that all activation content is amenable to sparse, monosemantic decomposition. We question this assumption and hypothesize that activations contain a low-rank, dense component that is computationally important to the model yet inherently unsuitable for sparse representation, which serves as a major source of the persistent dense latents widely observed in trained SAEs. To test this, we add a small rank-$r$ linear bottleneck in parallel with standard SAEs (BatchTopK and Matryoshka), allowing dense structure to be absorbed before sparse reconstruction. On Gemma-2-2B layer 12, a rank-24 bottleneck reduces dense latent count by up to 84\% while improving sparse probing and targeted probe perturbation on both architectures at matched sparsity. The absorbed component is (i) \textbf{structurally identifiable} as the top principal components and outlier dimensions; (ii) \textbf{causally necessary}, with removing it raising next-token cross-entropy by 7.5$\times$, far exceeding the 2.8$\times$ from removing the geometrically near-identical top-24 PCA directions; and (iii) \textbf{redundantly encoded by sparse dictionaries}, with ablating 787 maximally aligned sparse features raising cross-entropy by only 2.9$\times$ and ablating 2,048 topic-aligned features leaving MMLU topic classification virtually unchanged, whereas removing the scaffold drops it from 98.7\% to chance. Together, our findings identify a compact, semantically informative and causally important component of residual stream activations (which we term a \textbf{computational scaffold}) that standard sparse dictionaries represent inefficiently, suggesting that the scope of sparsity-based interpretability methods warrants careful re-examination.

AI.tldr: A small, dense rank-r bottleneck in parallel with sparse autoencoders reveals a compact, semantically informative “computational scaffold” in residual activations. This scaffold is structurally identifiable, causally necessary for token prediction, and redundantly encoded by sparse dictionaries, challenging the primacy of sparsity-based interpretability.
AI.motivation: Question the common assumption that residual activations in sparse autoencoders are fully amenable to sparse, monosemantic decomposition. Hypothesize a dense, low-rank component that is crucial to computation but not well captured by sparse dictionaries.
AI.method: Introduce a parallel rank-r linear bottleneck (e.g., rank-24) with existing SAEs (BatchTopK and Matryoshka) on Gemma-2-2B model. Evaluate reduction in dense latent count, sparse probing performance, and targeted perturbations. Identify absorbed component via PCA/top components. Perform ablations to test causality (cross-entropy impact when removing the scaffold), and redundancy (feature ablations on sparse dictionaries).
AI.result: Dense latent count reduced by up to 84% with a rank-24 bottleneck. Absorbed component identified as top principal components and outliers; removing it raises next-token cross-entropy by 7.5x, far more than removing near-identical top-24 PCA directions (2.8x). The scaffold is redundantly encoded by sparse dictionaries: ablating 787 aligned sparse features yields only 2.9x CE increase, while removing 2,048 topic-aligned features leaves MMLU topic classification almost unchanged; removing the scaffold drops MMLU accuracy from 98.7% to chance.
AI.conclusion: Existence of a compact, semantically informative, and causally important computational scaffold in residual activations. Standard sparse dictionaries inefficently represent this component, necessitating a re-examination of sparsity-based interpretability methods.

title: Closing the Reflection Gap: A Free Calibration Bonus for Agentic RL
summary: LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents that interact with external environments and observe feedback such as execution results, error messages, and tool outputs. A well-functioning agent should be able to leverage this feedback to accurately assess its own performance. Yet we find a persistent reflection gap: LLM agents tend to mis-assess their own outputs after observing concrete environment feedback -- even for questions they correctly answered -- and standard RL barely helps due to a credit-assignment mismatch. To close this gap, we propose RefGRPO, a simple yet effective fix that augments standard RL algorithms with two key ingredients: a free calibration bonus computed by contrasting the agent's own reflection with the actual outcome (requiring no additional reward model, LLM judge, or external annotation), and a dynamic schedule on its coefficient. Compared to standard RL baselines, our method simultaneously improves reflection calibration (e.g., reduces underconfidence rate $44.4\% \to 7.7\%$) and task accuracy (e.g., $75.1\% \to 76.5\%$) on text-to-SQL across five benchmarks. The resulting calibrated reflection turns the agent into its own verifier grounded in environment feedback, which further enables (i) better self-improvement that uses reflections as pseudo-rewards without outcome supervision, and (ii) more effective test-time selective prediction by committing only to rollouts flagged as correct.

AI.tldr: RefGRPO adds a free calibration bonus and a dynamic coefficient schedule to standard RL for LLM agents, reducing reflection miscalibration and improving text-to-SQL accuracy; results show underconfidence drop from 44.4% to 7.7% and accuracy rise from 75.1% to 76.5% across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the reflection gap: LLM agents misestimate performance after concrete environment feedback; credit-assignment in RL suffers from mismatch; a calibration mechanism leveraging the agent's own reflections—without extra reward models or labels—could improve reliability and performance.
AI.method: Propose RefGRPO: augment standard RL with a free calibration bonus computed by contrasting the agent's own reflection with the actual outcome; introduce a dynamic schedule on the calibration coefficient; requires no reward model, judge, or external annotation; uses reflection as pseudo-rewards; enables test-time selective prediction by only rolling out confidently correct branches.
AI.result: Calibration improves reflection reliability and task accuracy on text-to-SQL across five benchmarks: underconfidence rate decreases from 44.4% to 7.7%; accuracy increases from 75.1% to 76.5%.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated reflection acts as a verifier grounded in environment feedback, enabling self-improvement through pseudo-rewards and more effective test-time selective prediction; shows promise for enhancing trustworthiness and efficiency of LLM-based agents.

title: A New Multi-Domain Benchmark for Micro-Action Recognition and Detection
summary: Micro-actions are short-duration, low-amplitude subtle body movements at the whole-body level that can reveal latent intentions, involuntary reactions, and fine-grained affective changes. Our previous MA-52 benchmark has provided an important foundation for micro-action recognition, but it remains limited in scale, scene diversity, task coverage, and evaluation protocols. To advance micro-action analysis toward more realistic and comprehensive settings, we introduce MMA-82, a large-scale multi-domain extension of MA-52. MMA-82 expands the label space from 52 to 82 fine-grained micro-action categories and covers four distinct domains, including laboratory interviews, street interviews, psychiatric patient interviews, and emotion-rich television videos, resulting in 77,856 annotated instances from 454 subjects. Built upon MMA-82, we establish two core tasks: Micro-Action Recognition and Multi-label Micro-Action Detection. For recognition, we further define in-domain and cross-domain protocols, including few-shot and zero-shot settings, to evaluate model robustness, transferability, and generalization. Extensive experiments show that current methods still struggle with realistic micro-action understanding, especially under domain shift, long-tailed category distributions, and complex temporal localization. Beyond benchmarking, we investigate the relationship between micro-actions and emotion, showing that micro-actions are strongly associated with emotional states and provide complementary cues to facial micro-expressions for improved emotion recognition. These results demonstrate that MMA-82 serves as a comprehensive and challenging benchmark for realistic micro-action analysis and a valuable resource for human-centered AI. MMA-82 is available at https://github.com/LpyNow/MMA-82.

AI.tldr: MMA-82 is a large-scale, multi-domain micro-action benchmark expanding MA-52 to 82 actions across four domains, with two core tasks (recognition and multi-label detection) and robust in-domain/cross-domain protocols including few-shot and zero-shot; current methods struggle with domain shift and long-tail distributions, while micro-actions show strong emotion-related cues.
AI.motivation: Fill scale, diversity, task coverage, and evaluation gaps in MA-52 to enable realistic micro-action understanding and emotion-related analysis.
AI.method: Introduce MMA-82: 82 fine-grained micro-action categories across four domains (lab/street/psychiatric interviews and emotion-rich TV), 77,856 annotated instances from 454 subjects; define Micro-Action Recognition and Multi-label Micro-Action Detection; propose in-domain and cross-domain protocols with few-shot and zero-shot settings; extensive experiments and analysis of domain shift, long-tail effects, and temporal localization; explore relationships between micro-actions and emotion.
AI.result: Current methods still struggle with realistic micro-action understanding, particularly under domain shift, long-tailed category distributions, and precise temporal localization; micro-actions are strongly associated with emotional states and provide complementary cues to facial micro-expressions for improved emotion recognition.
AI.conclusion: MMA-82 provides a comprehensive, challenging benchmark for realistic micro-action analysis and a valuable resource for human-centered AI, enabling more robust recognition and emotion-aware understanding across diverse contexts.

title: Selective Agentic Recovery for UAV Autonomy with a Persistent Mission Runtime
summary: Agentic AI can support unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) autonomy by providing high-level recovery reasoning when local waypoint- or setpoint-based execution encounters blocked passages, repeated no-progress behavior, or mission-level ambiguity. On physical UAVs, however, remote reasoning is most useful when it is invoked selectively, since each call introduces latency, resource cost, backend uncertainty, and a need to validate the returned decision. This paper presents Persistent Mission Runtime (PMR), a UAV recovery framework that keeps the mission loop and safety-critical execution local while using an external agentic reasoner only as an on-demand recovery module. The reasoner selects from predefined recovery skills, and each returned decision is parsed, verified, safety-filtered, and mapped to local executor actions before it can affect flight. PMR introduces learned Cognitive Value of Invocation (learned-CVI), a compact admission gate that estimates when remote agentic reasoning is likely to improve near-term mission progress enough to justify its operational cost. Across a fixed 400-run Gazebo/PX4 benchmark with eight scenarios, learned-CVI raises hard/ambiguous-regime success from 5.0% under local-only autonomy to 95.0%, outperforms one-shot and periodic reasoning baselines by 20.0 and 32.5 percentage points, and reduces remote-agent calls by 16.7% and logged tokens by 29.2% relative to a manually tuned rule-based invocation baseline.

AI.tldr: PMR enables UAV autonomy with on-demand external agentic reasoning, keeping the mission loop local and using a learned admission gate (learned-CVI) to decide when to invoke it; achieves high recovery success while reducing remote reasoning cost.
AI.motivation: Remote reasoning incurs latency, resource usage, and backend uncertainty. Selective invocation is needed to maintain safety and mission progress.
AI.method: PMR keeps the mission loop and safety-critical execution local, integrating an external agentic reasoner as an on-demand recovery module. It offers predefined recovery skills; returned decisions are parsed, safety-filtered, and mapped to local actions. A learned-CVI admission gate estimates when remote reasoning improves near-term progress enough to justify its cost.
AI.result: In 400 Gazebo/PX4 runs across eight scenarios, learned-CVI raises hard/ambiguous-regime success from 5.0% (local-only) to 95.0%. It outperforms one-shot and periodic reasoning baselines by 20.0 and 32.5 percentage points, and reduces remote-agent calls by 16.7% and logged tokens by 29.2% compared with a manually tuned rule-based invocation baseline.
AI.conclusion: A selective remote reasoning approach with a learned admission gate significantly enhances UAV recovery performance while curbing remote-call costs, indicating practical viability for resilient UAV autonomy.

title: Non-Parametric Machine Text Detection via Multi-View Gaussian Processes
summary: Adversarial conditions such as paraphrasing and targeted style transfer sharply degrade the accuracy of machine text detectors. A document, however, carries multiple complementary signals (e.g., stylistic features, likelihood and rank-order features, and structural features), and an attack that suppresses one may leave others intact. While a parametric classifier can learn to combine these features given sufficient supervision, classifiers are prone to making confidently incorrect predictions when the distribution shifts (e.g., novel attacks or unseen language models). To address this, we propose a multi-view, non-parametric detection framework that extracts complementary feature views from the same document and aggregates per-view evidence through a Gaussian process ensemble. By aggregating evidence across views, an adversary must simultaneously defeat multiple independent axes of detection, substantially raising the cost of evasion. The Gaussian process formulation additionally provides calibrated probabilities and principled abstention on out-of-distribution inputs, supporting reliable deployment in high-stakes settings. We evaluate on three benchmarks spanning diverse generators and attacks: the DetectRL and RAID benchmarks, and the PAN2025 shared task and demonstrate that our multi-view detector maintains strong performance under the considered attacks, outperforming existing approaches against held out attacks.

AI.tldr: Multi-view GP ensemble detector robust to adversarial paraphrasing and style-transfer in machine text detection.
AI.motivation: Single-view, parametric detectors degrade under distribution shifts; need robust, calibrated, non-parametric detection leveraging complementary signals across views.
AI.method: Extract multiple feature views (stylistic, likelihood/rank-order, structural) per document and aggregate evidence via a Gaussian process ensemble, enabling calibrated probabilities and abstention on out-of-distribution inputs.
AI.result: Evaluated on DetectRL, RAID, PAN2025 benchmarks; maintains strong performance under attacks and outperforms existing methods on held-out attacks.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view GP ensemble raises evasion cost for attackers and provides reliable uncertainty quantification for deployment in high-stakes settings.

title: SkillAudit: Ground-Truth-Free Skill Evolution via Paired Trajectory Auditing
summary: Agent skills are structured procedural packages that guide frozen LLM agents in specialized workflows. Skills rarely remain sufficient after deployment: edge cases, API changes, and deployment constraints become visible only through use, making skill evolution a practical necessity. Existing methods depend on privileged feedback such as held-out validation scores, hidden test outcomes, or environment rewards -- signals often unavailable when a practitioner has only a task description and workspace data. We introduce SkillAudit, a framework for evolving agent skills without ground-truth feedback. The key idea is paired trajectory auditing: at each iteration, the same task is executed with and without the candidate skill, isolating how the skill changes agent behavior without external labels. To turn behavioral differences into edit guidance, SkillAudit uses Process-Aligned Contrastive Evaluation (PACE), a cluster of evaluators that maps trajectory divergences to diagnostic signals linked to specific passages in the skill document. A structural verifier, compiled once from the task specification and then fixed, checks task constraints and rolls back harmful updates. SkillAudit routes edits through two pipelines: Refine removes noisy or irrelevant guidance from broadly useful skills, while Repair replaces passages that conflict with the task. Across 89 containerized tasks spanning 8 professional domains, SkillAudit achieves 73.9% average task reward, outperforming an agent without skills (40.9%) and the static expert skill (56.7%). These gains are obtained without accessing hidden tests, reference solutions, or external scoring functions during evolution.

AI.tldr: SkillAudit introduces a ground-truth-free framework for evolving agent skills via paired trajectory auditing and PACE evaluators, with a structural verifier and two pipelines (Refine, Repair); achieves 73.9% average task reward across 89 containerized tasks, outperforming baselines without hidden tests or external scoring.
AI.motivation: Address lack of ground-truth feedback in real-world skill evolution for LLM-powered agents; existing methods rely on scores or rewards that may be unavailable.
AI.method: At each iteration, run the same task with and without candidate skill; measure trajectory differences; map divergences to diagnostic signals via PACE; compile a structural verifier from task spec; route edits through Refine and Repair; iterative updates.
