AI.result: 73.9% average task reward; outperforms no-skill (40.9%) and static expert skill (56.7%); no hidden tests or external scoring during evolution.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical, general approach to skill evolution without ground-truth labels; demonstrates broad applicability; potential limitations include dependency on task spec quality and evaluators; suggests room for extension and deeper ablations.

title: Conditioning Matters: Stabilizing Inversion and Attention in Diffusion Image Editing
comment: Accepted to ECML PKDD 2026 Research Track
summary: Inversion-based image editing offers flexible and training-free control but still struggles with inversion accuracy and the trade-off between editing fidelity and background preservation. While recent methods improve inversion formulations or attention interactions, the role of textual conditioning in shaping diffusion dynamics and editing behavior remains underexplored. We show both empirically and theoretically that the precision of textual conditioning influences inversion stability by modulating the geometry of the diffusion velocity field, while also affecting the consistency of cross-branch attention during editing. These effects directly impact background preservation and semantic fidelity. Building on this analysis, we propose SimEdit, a conditioning-aware framework with two complementary components: (a) conditioning refinement, which constructs conditioning signals with improved semantic precision and structural alignment to facilitate stable inversion and consistent attention manipulation, and (b) token-wise cross-branch attention control, which separates edit-relevant and structure-preserving components and modulates them asymmetrically during attention manipulation. Extensive experiments on PIE-Bench demonstrate that SimEdit consistently improves both inversion reconstruction quality and editing performance over previous attention-manipulation approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/zju-pi/SimEdit.

AI.tldr: Precise textual conditioning shapes diffusion dynamics and cross-branch attention during inversion-based editing; SimEdit refines conditioning and uses token-wise attention control to improve inversion stability, background preservation, and semantic fidelity, outperforming prior methods on PIE-Bench.
AI.motivation: Inversion-based image editing suffers from limited inversion accuracy and a trade-off between editing fidelity and background preservation; the role of textual conditioning in diffusion dynamics and editing behavior is underexplored.
AI.method: Propose SimEdit with two components: (a) conditioning refinement to provide conditioning signals with improved semantic precision and structural alignment; (b) token-wise cross-branch attention control to separate edit-relevant and structure-preserving components and modulate them asymmetrically during attention manipulation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on PIE-Bench show that SimEdit consistently improves both inversion reconstruction quality and editing performance over prior attention-manipulation approaches; code available.
AI.conclusion: A conditioning-aware design improves inversion stability and editing fidelity by aligning textual conditioning with image structure and by asymmetric) control of attention, enabling more reliable background preservation and semantic edits.

title: BIM-Loc: BIM-Integrated Discrepancy-Aware LiDAR-based Indoor Localization
comment: 24 pages, 21 figures, accepted by International Journal of Robotics Research (IJRR), to be published
summary: Accurate and robust localization is a fundamental requirement for service and inspection robots, particularly in feature-sparse indoor environments where traditional systems struggle due to a lack of distinct landmarks. While prior maps can enhance robustness, precise and compact maps capturing real-world details are often unavailable for new or frequently changing environments. This paper presents BIM-Loc, a novel discrepancy-aware LiDAR-based localization method that directly integrates Building Information Models (BIM) from the design phase. BIM-Loc simultaneously estimates trajectories aligned with the BIM coordinate system and identifies discrepancies between real-world observations and the as-designed BIM in an online fashion. Our core contributions include: (1) a novel multi-hit ray casting strategy for efficient BIM-point data association and projection of 3D observations into 2D texture space; (2) a pose graph optimization framework with BIM-integrated factors that enforces consistency among odometry, sequential scans, and BIM structures; and (3) a hierarchical Bayesian inference module that incrementally updates a continuous 2D surface representation for discrepancy detection, propagating updates from the pixel to the structure level. Extensive evaluations in both simulation and real-world applications demonstrate that BIM-Loc significantly outperforms state-of-the-art map-based methods in localization accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: BIM-Loc enables discrepancy-aware LiDAR localization by integrating BIM from the design phase, achieving robust localization in feature-sparse indoor environments and online discrepancy detection.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of accurate and robust localization without dense, up-to-date maps in new or dynamic indoor environments, by leveraging existing BIM models to constrain pose and detect as-designed vs. observed discrepancies.
AI.method: 1) Multi-hit ray casting to efficiently associate BIM points with LiDAR measurements and project observations into 2D texture space; 2) Pose-graph optimization with BIM-integrated factors enforcing consistency among odometry, sequential scans, and BIM structures; 3) Hierarchical Bayesian inference to incrementally update a continuous 2D surface representation for discrepancy detection, propagating updates from pixel to structure level.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world tests show BIM-Loc significantly outperforms state-of-the-art map-based localization methods in accuracy and robustness, particularly in feature-sparse indoor environments.
AI.conclusion: Integrating design-phase BIM into LiDAR localization enables more accurate, robust localization and online discrepancy detection between real-world observations and as-designed BIM, by combining geometry-based localization with probabilistic discrepancy analysis.

title: Rethinking Backdoor Adversarial Unlearning through the Lens of Catastrophic Forgetting in Continual Learning
summary: Existing studies reveal that current backdoor defenses exhibit limited robustness and often fail against specific types of attacks. More concerningly, prevailing safety tuning strategies tend to provide only superficial safety protection, as they fall short of completely eliminating the backdoor effects. In this work, we present a novel formulation of backdoor learning and unlearning as a sequential, three-stage process from a continual learning perspective. Within this framework, we formally define complete backdoor unlearning and further derive the necessary conditions for achieving it based on the mechanism of catastrophic forgetting. Guided by these insights, we propose Blind Inversion-Backdoor Adversarial Unlearning (BI-BAU), which formulates the generation of adversarial examples satisfying the unlearning conditions as a blind inversion problem. We solve this by integrating the bi-level optimization process of adversarial training into an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm framework to optimize the maximum a posteriori (MAP) objective. Furthermore, BI-BAU is extended to untargeted adversarial scenarios with unknown target classes, as well as to multi-modal contrastive learning tasks, enhancing its applicability to real-world deployment scenarios where pre-trained models may be compromised. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method exhibits general applicability across a wide spectrum of backdoor attacks and can effectively and thoroughly eliminate the backdoor effects from a backdoor model.

AI.tldr: Introduces Blind Inversion-Backdoor Adversarial Unlearning (BI-BAU), a three-stage continual-learning framework to achieve complete backdoor unlearning via a blind inversion formulation solved with a MAP-EM optimization, with untargeted and multimodal extensions; demonstrations show thorough backdoor removal across attacks.
AI.motivation: Current backdoor defenses are fragile and safety-tuning yields only superficial protection; there is a need for a formal notion and practical method for complete backdoor unlearning grounded in catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Formulates backdoor learning and unlearning as a sequential three-stage process in a continual-learning setting; formally defines complete backdoor unlearning and derives necessary conditions via catastrophic forgetting; proposes BI-BAU to generate adversarial examples that satisfy unlearning as a blind inversion problem; solves it by embedding bi-level adversarial training into a MAP objective within an Expectation-Maximization framework; extends BI-BAU to untargeted adversaries and to multi-modal contrastive learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show BI-BAU is broadly applicable across diverse backdoor attacks and can effectively and thoroughly remove backdoor effects from backdoored models.
AI.conclusion: BI-BAU provides a principled, effective mechanism for complete backdoor unlearning with broad applicability, including extensions to untargeted targets and multimodal settings, enhancing the reliability of pre-trained models in security-critical deployment.

title: AFFORDANCE20Q: Evaluating Affordance Reasoning from Physical Properties
summary: Affordance reasoning, the inference of an object's action possibilities from its physical properties (e.g., shape and material), is fundamental to human physical understanding and increasingly critical for Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing affordance benchmarks largely expose explicit object identities in the evaluation setup, allowing models to rely on memorized object-affordance mappings rather than reasoning over physical properties. To address this gap, we introduce Affordance20Q, a novel affordance reasoning benchmark formulated as a 20-Questions game without exposing the object's identity. In each game, the model identifies a hidden object's affordance from a candidate set by asking yes/no questions about its physical properties. Affordance20Q comprises 1,009 games over 454 objects and 59 affordances, all manually filtered, refined, and annotated. We conduct comprehensive experiments with 15 state-of-the-art LLMs and find a substantial gap (~20 points) compared to human performance. A KL-based information-gain (IG) analysis further shows that models fail to ask discriminating questions as the game progresses. To close the gap, we develop KB-Anchored Rule Induction (KARI), a pipeline based on LLMs that generates affordance rules grounded in evidence from knowledge bases (KBs). KARI improves open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points, while the limited coverage of KBs hinders further gains. We release all our code and data at https://github.com/1171-jpg/Affordance20Q.git

AI.tldr: A new 20-Questions-style affordance reasoning benchmark (Affordance20Q) that hides object identity, plus a KB-grounded rule induction (KARI) to boost LLM performance; shows notable gaps to humans and KB-limited gains.
AI.motivation: Address reliance on memorized object-affordance mappings in existing benchmarks; evaluate genuine reasoning over physical properties.
AI.method: Dataset: 1,009 games, 454 objects, 59 affordances; manual curation; yes/no interrogation of physical properties to infer an object's affordance; evaluation of 15 SOTA LLMs; KL-based information gain analysis; KARI pipeline generating affordance rules anchored to knowledge bases; code and data released.
AI.result: LLMs lag humans by ~20 points; IG analysis shows failures to ask discriminating questions; KARI improves open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points; KB coverage limits further gains.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a measurable gap in LLM affordance reasoning and provides a viable, KB-grounded approach (KARI) to mitigate it, though gains are bounded by KB coverage; dataset and code are released for reproducibility.

title: BoRAD: Bootstrap your Own Representations for Multi-class Anomaly Detection
summary: Reconstruction-based anomaly detection is attractive for industrial inspection, but scaling it from category-specific training to a one-for-all setting is challenging. A single model must reconstruct diverse normal appearances without copying abnormal details, which exposes two coupled failure modes: identical shortcut, where anomalies pass through the reconstruction path, and mis-reconstruction, where normal categories are confused with one another. We propose \textbf{BoRAD}, a label-free training framework that treats this as a representation-capacity allocation problem. BoRAD uses a shared learnable prototype bank to impose two complementary regularizers: spatial prototype alignment contracts local within-prototype variation to suppress anomaly copying, while prototype-relative global alignment preserves between-prototype structure and improves sensitivity to abnormal angular deviations. The prototype bank and prediction heads are used only during training; inference remains a standard teacher-student feature discrepancy pass, with no class labels, negative pairs, memory retrieval, or prototype lookup. BoRAD achieves competitive one-for-all anomaly detection performance, including 86.2\% mAD on MVTec AD, 80.7\% mAD on VisA and 73.1\% mAD on Real-IAD. Diagnostic analyses further show reduced anomaly leakage, improved normal-category separability, and stronger anomaly-normal score separation.

AI.tldr: BoRAD introduces a label-free, one-for-all anomaly detection framework that uses a shared learnable prototype bank to allocate representation capacity, addressing two failure modes—identical shortcut and mis-reconstruction—via two complementary regularizers. Training employs the prototype bank and prediction heads; inference proceeds with a standard teacher-student discrepancy pass without labels or prototype lookup. It achieves competitive one-for-all results on MVTec AD (86.2% mAD), VisA (80.7% mAD), and Real-IAD (73.1% mAD).
AI.motivation: Scaling reconstruction-based anomaly detection from category-specific models to a single, one-for-all model is challenging due to two coupled failure modes: (1) identical shortcut, where anomalies leak through the reconstruction path, and (2) mis-reconstruction, where normal categories are misidentified as each other. A label-free method that can allocate representation capacity to suppress anomalies while preserving normal-category structure is needed for practical industrial inspection.
AI.method: BoRAD introduces a shared learnable prototype bank and two regularizers. Spatial prototype alignment contracts local within-prototype variation to suppress anomaly copying; prototype-relative global alignment preserves the between-prototype structure and improves sensitivity to abnormal angular deviations. The prototype bank and prediction heads are used only during training; inference uses a standard teacher-student feature discrepancy pass, with no class labels, negative pairs, memory retrieval, or prototype lookup.
AI.result: BoRAD achieves competitive one-for-all anomaly detection performance: 86.2% mAD on MVTec AD, 80.7% mAD on VisA, and 73.1% mAD on Real-IAD. Diagnostic analyses indicate reduced anomaly leakage, improved separability among normal categories, and stronger separation between anomaly and normal scores.
AI.conclusion: BoRAD demonstrates that addressing representation-capacity allocation via a shared prototype bank enables effective label-free one-for-all anomaly detection, with robust anomaly–normal separation and reduced leakage, while keeping inference complexity simple and label-free.

title: When and How Severely: Scenario-Specific Safety Envelopes for Driving VLAs
summary: Safety certification of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving planners under ISO 21448 (SOTIF) rests on an Operational Design Domain (ODD) specification that answers two complementary questions: when does the planner start to fail, and how severely does it fail once it does? We evaluate Alpamayo R1, a 10B-parameter open-weight driving VLA, on 15,968 (clip, attack) pairs. We find a conservative-aggregate gap: an aggregate safe threshold of $σ\leq 50$ under a 15% average displacement error (ADE) budget masks well-sampled scenarios that tolerate the top of the tested grid ($σ= 70$). A Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) on the changed-explanation subset identifies six discrete severity bands (BIC-optimal $k{=}6$), so two perturbation conditions with the same mean error can differ materially in their share of high-severity (C4/C5) failures. Joining the two analyses on the same corpus surfaces a finding neither yields in isolation: the scenarios with the loosest noise thresholds are not those with the lowest high-severity rate: STOP_SIGNAL concentrates roughly $4\times$ the C4/C5 share of LANE_KEEPING despite tolerating a larger $σ$. A deployable SOTIF ODD specification for driving VLAs therefore requires a two-dimensional safety envelope, not a single aggregate value per hazard.

AI.tldr: Two-dimensional ODD safety envelope is needed for VLA driving planners under SOTIF. Conservative aggregate thresholds mask high-severity failures; Gaussian Mixture Modeling reveals six severity bands; STOP_SIGNAL shows disproportionately high high-severity rates in LANE_KEEPING despite higher tolerance, indicating that a single σ threshold per hazard is insufficient.
AI.motivation: Evaluate safety certification of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving planners under ISO 21448 (SOTIF) by quantifying when failures start and how severe they are, using a large perturbation dataset on Alpamayo R1.
AI.method: Assess Alpamayo R1 (10B parameters) on 15,968 (clip, attack) perturbation pairs. Compute a conservative safe threshold σ under a 15% average displacement error (ADE) budget. Apply a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) on the changed-explanation subset to identify discrete severity bands (BIC-optimal k=6). Compare two analyses on the same corpus to examine alignment between noise tolerance and high-severity outcomes.
AI.result: Conservative σ≤50 threshold masks scenarios that tolerate σ=70. GMM with k=6 reveals six severity bands, yielding six distinct categories of failure severity (C1–C5). The intersection analysis shows that the loosest noise thresholds do not coincide with the lowest high-severity rate: STOP_SIGNAL concentrates roughly 4× the C4/C5 share of LANE_KEEPING even though it tolerates larger σ. The findings imply that a single aggregate threshold is insufficient to certify safety in SOTIF; a two-dimensional safety envelope is necessary.
AI.conclusion: A deployable SOTIF ODD specification for driving VLAs requires a two-dimensional safety envelope (failure onset threshold and failure severity distribution) rather than a single aggregate value per hazard.

title: Deep Spectral Learning of Embedded Latent Transfer Operators for Stochastic Dynamical Systems
comment: Accepted at the 42nd Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2026)
summary: We propose a spectral learning method for stochastic nonlinear dynamical systems represented with embedded latent transfer operators in deep feature spaces. We instantiate the method as Deep Spectral Encoder (DSE), an operator-based latent state-space model in which a time-invariant neural encoder implements learnable nonlinear feature maps from observations, and these features define Markovian latent states whose temporal evolution and observation mapping are described by the transfer and observation operators, respectively. Functional canonical correlation analysis in a learnable Galerkin-projected feature space provides state coordinates from past and future observations, and the two linear operators are estimated on the state coordinates as ridge-regularized closed-form solutions that coincide with Galerkin projections of the associated covariance operators. On this representation, we generalize sequential Bayesian filtering and Koopman spectral mode decomposition in feature space. Experiments on several scenarios show stable and superior performance with sequential Bayesian filtering and dynamic mode decomposition baselines even under noise and partial observability.

AI.tldr: Introduces Deep Spectral Encoder (DSE), an operator-based latent state-space model that learns nonlinear feature maps via a neural encoder and uses Galerkin-projected operators to enable spectral analysis and Bayesian-style filtering in feature space; demonstrates robust performance under noise and partial observability.
AI.motivation: Address nonlinear stochastic dynamical systems by marrying operator-theoretic spectral methods (transfer/Koopman) with deep learning to obtain a tractable, robust latent representation for state estimation and spectral analysis.
AI.method: A time-invariant neural encoder maps observations to nonlinear feature maps forming latent states; temporal evolution and observation mapping are captured by transfer and observation operators. Functional canonical correlation analysis in a learnable Galerkin-projected feature space yields past/future state coordinates; the two linear operators are estimated as ridge-regularized closed-form solutions corresponding to Galerkin projections of covariance operators. The framework generalizes sequential Bayesian filtering and Koopman spectral mode decomposition in feature space.
AI.result: Empirical results across several scenarios show stable and superior performance compared to sequential Bayesian filtering and dynamic mode decomposition baselines, even under noisy conditions and partial observability.
AI.conclusion: The DSE framework provides a robust, operator-theoretic latent-state-space model in a learnable feature space, enabling effective spectral analysis and state estimation for nonlinear stochastic dynamics.

title: HarnessX: A Composable, Adaptive, and Evolvable Agent Harness Foundry
summary: AI agent performance depends critically on the runtime harness, comprising the prompts, tools, memory, and control flow that mediate how a model observes, reasons, and acts. Yet today's harnesses remain largely hand-crafted and static: each new model or task still demands bespoke scaffolding, and the rich traces produced during execution are rarely distilled back into systematic improvement. We introduce HarnessX, a foundry for composable, adaptive, and evolvable agent harnesses. HarnessX assembles typed harness primitives via a substitution algebra, adapts them through AEGIS, a trace-driven multi-agent evolution engine grounded in an operational mirror between symbolic adaptation and reinforcement learning, and closes the harness-model loop by turning trajectories into both harness updates and model training signal. Across five benchmarks (ALFWorld, GAIA, WebShop, tau^3-Bench, and SWE-bench Verified), HarnessX yields an average gain of +14.5% (up to +44.0%), with gains largest where baselines are lowest. These results suggest that agent progress need not come from model scaling alone: composing and evolving runtime interfaces from execution feedback is an actionable and complementary lever. The complete codebase will be open-sourced in a future release.

AI.tldr: HarnessX is a platform to compose and evolve AI agent runtimes by assembling typed harness primitives via a substitution algebra, guided by a trace-driven evolution engine (AEGIS) that converts execution traces into harness updates and training signals, yielding consistent performance gains across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current AI agent harnesses are static and bespoke for each model/task; exploiting execution traces to automatically adapt and improve the interface can unlock gains beyond model scaling.
AI.method: Introduce HarnessX with (1) typed harness primitives assembled via a substitution algebra, (2) AEGIS, a trace-driven multi-agent evolution engine that links symbolic adaptation with reinforcement learning, (3) an end-to-end loop that turns trajectories into harness updates and model training signals; evaluation on five benchmarks with open-code pledge.
AI.result: Across ALFWorld, GAIA, WebShop, tau^3-Bench, SWE-bench Verified, HarnessX achieves an average gain of 14.5% (up to 44.0%), with larger gains where baselines are weaker.
AI.conclusion: Evolving and composing runtime interfaces from execution feedback is a practical and complementary lever for agent progress, not solely dependent on larger models; code will be open-sourced in a future release.

title: Encoder Winners Do Not Reliably Transfer Across VLA Backbone Scale: A Frozen-Backbone Grafting Diagnostic
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies typically inherit their vision encoder from upstream VLM releases, but it is unclear whether an encoder choice validated on a small VLA transfers to a larger backbone. We introduce a frozen-backbone grafting diagnostic: the vision tower of a released VLA is replaced by a candidate encoder under a fixed protocol (adaptive average pooling, LayerNorm, and a single trainable linear projector), with the language model and action expert frozen. Across four encoders, two LIBERO suites, two backbones (SmolVLA-450M and $π_{0.5}$-3.3B), and two-to-three seeds per cell (40 main grafting runs plus native, LoRA, pooling, and zero-/shuffled-image controls, all scored by offline action MSE), the small-backbone winner does not reliably select the large-backbone top tier: SigLIP is best on SmolVLA across both suites, while on $π_{0.5}$ DINOv2-small leads the spatial suite and the object suite is a seed-sensitive near-tie band; three of the four backbone-suite comparisons (and 11 of 12 seed-level cells) support backbone-dependent rankings. The grafting wrapper is itself non-neutral with opposite sign across backbones (+45-56% MSE on the SmolVLA native tower, -50-52% on $π_{0.5}$), so all conclusions are conditional on the fixed grafting protocol. We position frozen grafting as a cheap target-backbone diagnostic to run before committing to an encoder at scale, not as a closed-loop deployment claim.

AI.tldr: Frozen-backbone grafting is a diagnostic to quickly assess whether an encoder choice validated on small VLA backbones transfers to larger backbones. It reveals backbone-dependent rankings and protocol-induced biases, suggesting that small-backbone performance is not a reliable predictor for large-backbone performance; the method serves as a pre-deployment screening tool rather than a deployment claim.
AI.motivation: To test whether an encoder validated on small VLA backbones generalizes to larger backbones under a fixed grafting protocol, addressing potential transferability gaps when scaling backbone size.
AI.method: Apply a frozen-backbone grafting protocol: replace the vision tower with a candidate encoder using adaptive average pooling, LayerNorm, and a single trainable linear projector; freeze the language model and action expert. Evaluate across four encoders, two LIBERO suites, two backbones (SmolVLA-450M and π0.5-3.3B), and multiple seeds (40 main grafting runs plus various controls). The objective is offline action MSE across tasks, with controls including native, LoRA, pooling, and zero-/shuffled-image variants.
AI.result: The small-backbone winner fails to consistently rank or predict top-tier performance on the larger backbone. SigLIP excels on SmolVLA across suites, while on π0.5 DINOv2-small dominates the spatial suite and the object suite is a seed-sensitive near-tie. Backbone-dependent rankings appear in 3 of 4 backbone-suite comparisons (11 of 12 seed-level cells). The grafting wrapper itself is non-neutral, with sign differences across backbones. Overall, conclusions are conditional on the fixed grafting protocol.
AI.conclusion: Frozen grafting offers a cheap, preliminary diagnostic to screen encoder/backbone compatibility before large-scale encoder commitments, but it should not be interpreted as a deployment-ready endorsement; results are contingent on the protocol and may not generalize across backbones.

title: SyLink Hand: A Synergy-Inspired Linkage-Driven Anthropomorphic Hand for Human-Like Dexterity
summary: Designing anthropomorphic robotic hands that balance functional dexterity with mechanical simplicity remains a significant challenge. Inspired by human hand synergies, this paper presents the SyLink Hand, an anthropomorphic dexterous hand that integrates biomechanical synergy principles with linkage-driven transmission mechanisms to achieve a high degree of anthropomorphism in appearance, kinematics, and functionality within a compact and cost-effective architecture. Biomechanical analysis of natural hand motions using motion capture gloves reveals strong kinematic correlations among hand joints, providing the basis for a simplified yet functional degree-of-freedom (DOF) configuration. Guided by these synergistic characteristics, optimized linkage mechanisms are employed to coordinate multiple joint motions and reproduce natural finger trajectories. A novel spherical four-bar linkage is further proposed to achieve decoupled flexion/extension (Flex/Ext) and abduction/adduction (Abd/Add) at the metacarpophalangeal joint within a compact form factor. The resulting prototype integrates 19 joints driven by 11 actuators, with a total mass of 520g and a manufacturing cost of approximately USD 400. Experimental evaluations demonstrate its human-like kinematic performance, high load-bearing capability, and versatile grasping and manipulation skills. These results validate that the synergy-inspired, linkage-based design effectively balances anthropomorphism, mechanical simplicity, and functional versatility, highlighting its potential for practical deployment in dexterity-demanding robotic applications.

AI.tldr: SyLink Hand is an anthropomorphic dexterous hand that fuses biomechanical hand synergies with linkage-driven transmission. It introduces a spherical four-bar linkage to decouple MCP flex/ext and abd/add, enabling compact, cost-effective design. The prototype has 19 joints driven by 11 actuators, weighs 520 g, and costs ~USD 400. Experimental tests show human-like kinematics, high load-bearing, and versatile grasping/manipulation.
AI.motivation: To reconcile functional dexterity with mechanical simplicity in anthropomorphic hands by leveraging human hand synergies to reduce DOFs while maintaining natural motion and grasp versatility, enabling a compact, low-cost design.
AI.method: 1) Analyze natural hand motion via motion-capture glove to identify kinematic correlations among joints; 2) design optimized linkage mechanisms to coordinate multiple joints and reproduce natural finger trajectories; 3) introduce a novel spherical four-bar linkage to decouple MCP flex/ext and abd/add; 4) build a prototype with 19 joints and 11 actuators; 5) evaluate kinematics, load capacity, and grasping performance.
AI.result: Prototype demonstrates human-like kinematic performance, high load-bearing capability, and versatile grasping/manipulation skills. Mass is 520 g; manufacturing cost ~ USD 400; 19 joints driven by 11 actuators.
AI.conclusion: A synergy-inspired, linkage-based design effectively balances anthropomorphism, mechanical simplicity, and functional versatility, suggesting strong potential for practical deployment in dexterity-demanding robotic applications.

title: Lyapunov-Based Sample Complexity Analysis for Weakly-Coupled MDPs
summary: We study the sample complexity of learning in average-reward weakly-coupled Markov decision processes (WCMDPs) and Restless Bandits (RBs) under a generative model. Naive reduction to a tabular MDP leads to high complexity bounds as the state-action space is exponentially large in the number of arms $N$. By exploiting the weakly coupled structure, we show that near-optimal policies can be learned with sample and computational complexities that are polynomial in $N$. Specifically, we analyze the plug-in approach, which applies an efficient planning algorithm to an empirical model estimated from data. For fully heterogeneous WCMDPs, we establish the first finite-sample PAC guarantee with polynomial complexity and an $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ optimality gap. For homogeneous RBs, we further prove that a smaller optimality gap is achievable under mild structural assumptions. A primary technical contribution of our work is a novel Lyapunov-based analysis framework. Unlike classical approaches that rely on the difficult-to-control bias function, our framework uses an explicitly constructed Lyapunov function along with a drift transfer technique between the true and empirical models. A key step of independent interest in our framework is a fine-grained perturbation analysis for the underlying linear programming (LP) relaxation, which provides a general tool for analyzing LP-based policies and weakly-coupled systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a sample-efficient learning framework for average-reward weakly-coupled MDPs (WCMDPs) and Restless Bandits (RBs) under a generative model by exploiting weak coupling. The plug-in approach achieves polynomial complexity in the number of arms N, with PAC guarantees: O(1/√N) optimality gap for fully heterogeneous WCMDPs and smaller gaps for homogeneous RBs.
AI.motivation: Overcome the exponential state-action blowup in naive tabular reductions for WCMDPs/RBs by leveraging weakly-coupled structure to obtain scalable, near-optimal learning algorithms.
AI.method: Develops a plug-in learning framework that estimates an empirical model from data and applies an efficient planning algorithm. Introduces a Lyapunov-based analysis framework with a constructed Lyapunov function and drift transfer between true and empirical models. Performs fine-grained perturbation analysis of the LP relaxation underlying LP-based policies.
AI.result: First finite-sample PAC guarantee with polynomial complexity for fully heterogeneous WCMDPs. Achieves an O(1/√N) optimality gap. For homogeneous RBs, achieves even smaller gaps under mild structural assumptions. Provides a general perturbation tool for LP-based policies in weakly-coupled systems.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that near-optimal learning in average-reward WCMDPs and RBs is possible with polynomial complexity in the number of arms by leveraging weak coupling. The Lyapunov-based framework and LP perturbation analysis are key technical advances with potential applicability to other weakly-coupled, LP-reliant settings.

title: Communication Policy Evolution for Proactive LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents have rapidly evolved into autonomous systems, yet a persistent information gap remains between users and agents: communication is costly, while users' identical preferences further limit information exchange. To investigate how agents should communicate across modalities, this paper formalizes Communication Policy, establishes textual and UI-based policies, and then evaluates communication policies across diverse environments, personas, and model combinations. Building information asymmetry for proactive agents, we set up two complementary settings, User-Agent and Planner-Executor. Experimental results reveal complementary strengths between interaction channels: text-based interaction often facilitates task performance, while structured UI improves agents' response quality and persona compliance. Motivated by that, a hybrid method combines these advantages. We further propose Communication Policy Evolution (CPE), a self-evolution framework for refining communication policies through rollout and prompt-level evolving. Without model modification, CPE achieves the best task success across multiple settings using prompt refinement alone. Our findings identify communication behavior as a critical yet underexplored design dimension for LLM agents.

AI.tldr: Formalizes Communication Policy for LLM agents across text and UI modalities; shows complementary strengths of each channel; introduces a prompt-only self-evolution framework (Communication Policy Evolution, CPE) and a hybrid policy to maximize task success without model changes.
AI.motivation: Address the information gap between users and agents caused by costly communication and homogeneous user preferences; investigate how cross-modal communication policies can improve task performance and adherence to persona.
AI.method: Define and formalize Communication Policy; establish textual and UI-based policies; evaluate across diverse environments, personas, and model combinations; implement two complementary settings (User-Agent and Planner-Executor); propose a hybrid communication policy; introduce Communication Policy Evolution (CPE) with rollout and prompt-level evolution guided by prompt refinement.
AI.result: Text-based interaction generally enhances task performance, while structured UI improves response quality and persona compliance; a hybrid approach leveraging both channels yields synergistic gains; CPE achieves the best task success across settings using only prompt refinement, without modifying models.
AI.conclusion: Communication policy is a critical, underexplored design dimension for LLM agents, and self-evolving, prompt-based refinement (CPE) provides a practical pathway to optimize cross-modal communication.

title: VideoWeave: Unlocking Geometric Consistency in Video Generation via Joint Geometry-Video Modeling
summary: Large-scale video diffusion models often fail to preserve 3D structure over time, causing geometric drift and implausible motion under viewpoint changes. Existing methods usually enforce geometric consistency by using explicit geometry reconstructions, such as depth maps, point clouds, or reconstructed 3D structures, to define conditions, supervision, or reward signals, making the generator sensitive to errors from upstream geometry pipelines. We propose VideoWeave, a latent-space post-training framework that uses implicit geometry-model features to constrain the generative distribution, providing a more flexible and non-rigid form of guidance that mitigates the impact of reconstruction errors from geometry models. Specifically, VideoWeave adapts these features into geometry latents and jointly models them with video latents in a shared denoising space, allowing geometry to shape the generative distribution during training. To support this process, we build GeoVid-80K, an 80K-video dataset with paired appearance and geometry representations. Experiments on text-to-video and image-to-video generation show that VideoWeave improves geometric coherence while preserving strong visual quality. VideoWeave project page at https://videoweave.github.io/

AI.tldr: VideoWeave: a latent-space post-training approach that leverages implicit geometry-model features to constrain video diffusion, reducing dependence on explicit geometry reconstructions; introduces GeoVid-80K; improves geometric coherence while preserving visual quality in text-to-video and image-to-video generation.
AI.motivation: Large-scale video diffusion models often fail to preserve 3D structure over time, leading to geometric drift and implausible motion under viewpoint changes. Using explicit geometry reconstructions as supervision makes the model brittle to upstream geometry errors; a flexible, geometry-aware latent guidance is needed.
AI.method: Post-training in latent space: convert implicit geometry-model features into geometry latents and jointly model them with video latents in a shared denoising space. This binds geometry guidance without relying on explicit reconstructions. The approach is supported by GeoVid-80K, an 80K-video dataset with paired appearance and geometry representations.
AI.result: Empirical results on text-to-video and image-to-video generation show reduced geometric drift and improved geometric coherence, while maintaining strong visual quality. The GeoVid-80K dataset supports the evaluation and future research.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space geometry guidance via VideoWeave provides robust, flexible constraints that mitigate errors from geometry pipelines and enhance structure consistency in video diffusion; GeoVid-80K is a valuable resource for geometry-aware video generation research.

title: Optimality-Preserving Decomposition for Scalable QAOA in Natural-Language-Guided Multi-Drone Assignment
summary: As multi-drone fleets scale, zone assignment rapidly evolves into an intractable NP-hard combinatorial problem that overwhelms classical exhaustive search. While quantum optimization promises to shatter these classical bottlenecks, mapping complex spatial tasks from human intent to restricted quantum hardware remains a severe challenge. To bridge this gap, we present an end-to-end framework integrating a fine-tuned Large Language Model (LLM) front-end with a highly scalable, domain-specific quantum-classical backend. The front-end utilizes Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to translate free-form natural language instructions into structurally robust Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) constraints without false negatives. To overcome the strict qubit limits of near-term quantum devices, our framework features a novel constraint-preserving graph partitioner and a compressed separator-based dynamic programming (DP) merge. By structurally encoding constraints via W-state initialization and XY-mixers in Conditional Value-at-Risk Quantum Approximate Optimization (CVaR-QAOA), the pipeline stays highly compact. Empirical results demonstrate that this architecture circumvents classical scaling walls, recovering the global optimum on 100% of idealized oracle cases and 96.3% under real QAOA sampling, enabling natural-language-guided task allocation at previously intractable scales.

AI.tldr: End-to-end NL-to-QUBO framework enabling scalable quantum optimization for multi-drone zone assignment, achieving global optimum in idealized cases and 96.3% under real QAOA, addressing NP-hardness and NL-to-hardware mapping challenges.
AI.motivation: Zone assignment for large drone fleets is NP-hard and intractable with classical search; quantum optimization promises performance gains but mapping complex human intent to hardware is challenging; need an end-to-end NL-to-quantum pipeline to enable scalable, constraint-aware task allocation.
AI.method: A front-end fine-tuned LLM (SFT + DPO) translates natural language instructions into robust QUBO constraints. A constraint-preserving graph partitioner and a compressed separator-based dynamic programming (DP) merge address qubit limits. Constraints are encoded via W-state initialization and XY-mixers in CVaR-QAOA to keep the circuit compact. The pipeline couples a scalable quantum-classical backend with a natural-language front-end.
AI.result: Empirical results show the architecture recovers the global optimum on 100% of idealized oracle cases and 96.3% under real QAOA sampling, enabling natural-language-guided task allocation at scales previously intractable.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NL-to-QUBO framework with constraint-preserving partitioning and CVaR-QAOA encoding withstands classical scaling limits and enables large-scale, NL-guided task allocation using quantum optimization.

title: Numbers Already Carry Their Own Embeddings
comment: Presented at the MATH-AI Workshop at NeurIPS 2025
summary: We introduce Adelic operation-preserved embeddings (AOE), a training-free representation that captures both a number's real value and its modular (p-adic) signatures. This construction preserves additive and multiplicative structure by design, turning numerical input into embeddings that "speak in the language of mathematics." Unlike prior approaches that rely on task-specific retraining, AOE is plug-and-play and drops seamlessly into existing architectures. On algebraic combinatorics benchmarks, it delivers consistent gains including the first-ever perfect accuracy on the Weaving Pattern task-while suggesting a principled path forward for overcoming the long-standing "number problem" in AI.

AI.tldr: A training-free, plug-and-play embedding that encodes a number's real value and its p-adic modular signatures while preserving addition and multiplication, achieving strong results on algebraic combinatorics benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the longstanding 'number problem' in AI by embedding mathematical structure directly into representations without task-specific retraining.
AI.method: Introduce Adelic operation-preserved embeddings (AOE) that map numerical input to embeddings preserving additive and multiplicative structure and encoding modular (p-adic) information; compatible with existing architectures without training.
AI.result: Consistent performance gains on algebraic combinatorics benchmarks, including the first-ever perfect accuracy on the Weaving Pattern task.
AI.conclusion: AOE provides a principled, training-free path to incorporate number-theoretic structure into AI representations, facilitating plug-and-play integration and suggesting a direction for overcoming the number problem.

title: CSPO: Constraint-Sensitive Policy Optimization for Safe Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted as a Spotlight paper at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Safe reinforcement learning (Safe RL) aims to maximize expected return while satisfying safety constraints, typically modeled as Constrained Markov Decision Processes (CMDPs). While primal-dual methods scale well to deep RL, they often suffer from delayed constraint correction, leading to oscillatory behavior and prolonged safety violations. In this paper, we propose Constraint-Sensitive Policy Optimization (CSPO), a first-order primal-dual method that incorporates local constraint sensitivity into policy updates. CSPO augments the primal objective with a constraint-sensitive correction derived from the shortest signed distance to the safety boundary, enabling smarter recovery steps back to safety, compensating for delayed Lagrange multiplier updates, reducing oscillations near the boundary, and preserving the KKT solutions of the original constrained problem. Experiments on navigation and locomotion benchmarks demonstrate that CSPO achieves faster safety recovery and high reward preservation, resulting in higher constrained returns compared to state-of-the-art primal-dual and penalty-based methods

AI.tldr: CSPO is a first-order primal-dual safe RL method that adds a constraint-sensitive correction based on the shortest signed distance to the safety boundary, enabling smarter recovery from safety violations, preserving KKT solutions, and achieving faster recovery with higher constrained returns than existing primal-dual and penalty-based methods.
AI.motivation: Primal-dual Safe RL methods suffer from delayed constraint correction, causing oscillations and extended safety violations near the boundary; a method that provides informed, boundary-aware updates could improve safety recovery while preserving optimality conditions.
AI.method: Introduce Constraint-Sensitive Policy Optimization (CSPO), which augments the primal objective with a correction term derived from the shortest signed distance to the safety boundary. This local constraint sensitivity guides policy updates toward safety, compensates for delayed Lagrange multiplier updates, reduces oscillations at the boundary, and preserves the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) solutions of the original constrained problem.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on navigation and locomotion benchmarks shows faster recovery to safe regions, higher constrained returns, and better reward preservation, outperforming state-of-the-art primal-dual and penalty-based methods.
AI.conclusion: CSPO provides a practical, theoretically grounded modification to primal-dual Safe RL that improves safety recovery and constrained performance without sacrificing optimality, validated on standard benchmarks.

title: MUSE: Agentic 3D Scene Authoring via Memory-Grounded Incremental Requirement Satisfaction
summary: Text-driven 3D scene generation is a promising technique for digital content creation, embodied AI simulation, and interactive design, yet practical workflows often require refining, extending, or correcting existing scenes while preserving non-target content. Existing methods can produce realistic and structurally plausible scenes, but they generally lack editability with requirement-level state tracking, so part-level failures often lead to full-scene regeneration or manual intervention. To tackle this challenge, we formulate controllable 3D scene authoring as incremental requirement satisfaction, unifying construction and editing. In this paper, we present MUSE, a memory-grounded multi-agent framework in which an Architect compiles instructions into structured requirements, a Sculptor executes local scene operations, and an Inspector verifies each step while updating Working, Scene, and Skill Memory. To evaluate requirement-level controllability and preservation-aware editing, we introduce AuthorBench, offering 145 constrained construction cases and a 1,584-case preservation-aware editing pool paired with external structured checks. On full construction cases, MUSE improves All-Goal success from 37.9 to 80.7 and surface-constraint fulfillment from 35.0 to 92.6 over the strongest baseline. On a stratified 240-case editing test split, MUSE achieves 49.6 All-Goal success, 99.9 preservation rate, and only 0.6 unintended change rate. Beyond automated metrics, human evaluations on compared local-editing baselines support stronger alignment with user intent, and downstream navigation-proxy tests indicate stronger spatial stability. Combined with ablations validating our memory designs, these results establish MUSE as an effective framework for controllable 3D scene authoring.

AI.tldr: MUSE is a memory-grounded multi-agent framework for controllable 3D scene authoring that treats editing as incremental requirement satisfaction, using Architect, Sculptor, and Inspector with memory modules; evaluated on AuthorBench showing large gains in construction and preservation during edits.
AI.motivation: Address editability and state-tracking shortcomings in text-driven 3D scene generation by enabling stepwise, memory-augmented requirement satisfaction that preserves non-target content during edits.
AI.method: Three-agent architecture: Architect compiles prompts into structured requirements; Sculptor executes local scene edits; Inspector verifies steps and updates Working, Scene, and Skill Memory. Memory-grounded loop supports incremental construction and editing. Evaluation on AuthorBench with 145 constrained construction cases and 1,584 constrained editing cases, plus external checks, ablations, human studies, and navigation proxy tests.
AI.result: On full construction, All-Goal success improved 37.9→80.7; surface-constraint fulfillment 35.0→92.6. In a 240-case editing test, All-Goal 49.6, preservation rate 99.9%, unintended change 0.6%. Humans prefer MUSE-aligned local edits; improved spatial stability; memory design ablations support effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: MUSE demonstrates effective, controllable 3D scene authoring via memory-grounded multi-agent coordination that unifies construction and editing, validated by comprehensive automated and human evaluations and a dedicated benchmark (AuthorBench).

title: ReactVLA: Fast and Lightweight Reactive Robot Manipulation via Improved Mean Flow Action Generation
summary: Diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have demonstrated strong capability in modeling expressive and multimodal action distributions. However, their reliance on iterative sampling introduces substantial inference latency, which limits their applicability to reactive closed-loop robot manipulation. To address this limitation, we propose \texttt{ReactVLA}, a lightweight and low-latency VLA framework for real-time robotic manipulation. \texttt{ReactVLA} combines two complementary designs: (1) an improved Mean Flow (iMF) action generator that reduces expensive multi-step diffusion sampling to one-to-few-step action generation, and (2) Attention Residuals (AttnRes), a dynamic depth-wise feature routing mechanism that replaces uniform residual accumulation to better preserve task-relevant multimodal representations. We evaluate \texttt{ReactVLA} on large-scale simulation benchmarks, including LIBERO and RoboIMI, as well as real-world robotic manipulation tasks. Experimental results show that \texttt{ReactVLA} consistently outperforms similarly sized VLA baselines, including SmolVLA and $π_0$. On challenging precision manipulation tasks, \texttt{ReactVLA} achieves up to a 1.65$\times$ improvement in task performance while providing more than a 4$\times$ increase in inference speed compared with leading VLA models. Finally, it reduces real-world policy latency to below 38.6 ms, enabling fast reactive control on physical robot platforms. Please check out our project website at: https://game-loader.github.io/ReactVLA/.

AI.tldr: ReactVLA introduces two design innovations, iMF and AttnRes, to cut diffusion sampling cost and preserve multimodal representations, yielding real-time control with superior performance on VLA benchmarks and real robots.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based VLA methods achieve expressive multimodal policies but incur high inference latency due to iterative sampling, hindering reactive closed-loop manipulation.
AI.method: iMF reduces diffusion sampling to one-to-few steps for action generation; AttnRes is a depth-wise attention-based residual routing that maintains task-relevant features; evaluated on LIBERO, RoboIMI, and real-robot tasks; compared against SmolVLA and pi_0.
AI.result: Outperforms similarly sized baselines; up to 1.65x improvement in task performance on hard precision tasks; >4x faster inference; real-world latency <38.6 ms.
AI.conclusion: ReactVLA enables fast reactive control in real-time robotic manipulation with preserved multimodal representations; demonstrates practical viability of diffusion-based VLA in low-latency settings; potential for broader adoption.

title: DTVEM-RE: A Hierarchical Random-Effects Extension of the Differential Time-Varying Effect Model for Person-Specific Multi-Lag Estimation in Intensive Longitudinal Data
summary: The Differential Time-Varying Effect Model (DTVEM) of Jacobson et al. (2019) is a popular tool for finding the best time lag in intensive longitudinal data, but it assumes everyone shares the same lag structure. The original authors named fixing this as future work, and it clashes with the premise of modern clinical research, which is that people differ. We present DTVEM-RE, an extension that lets each person have their own lag coefficients, with two versions of the confirmatory step: a discrete-time hierarchical Bayesian VAR in Stan, which pools across people and gives calibrated uncertainty, and a continuous-time per-person Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model in ctsem, which handles unevenly spaced beeps directly. We report four results. A simulation shows the Bayesian version recovers the between-person spread tau_a with bias below 0.01 and coverage of 90 to 93 percent. On the Fisher et al. (2017) EMA dataset (N=40), person-specific lag-1 effects vary by an order of magnitude across three mood items, the Bayesian and GAMM estimates agree closely (r=0.87 to 0.92), and DTVEM-RE gives the best one-step-ahead prediction among four discrete-time methods. A multi-lag version shows all nine tau_k values have credible intervals excluding zero, and the lag where people differ most changes across items, something lag-1-only methods like mlVAR cannot detect. Finally, the two versions agree almost exactly on person-specific lag-1 estimates (r >= 0.995), differing only as shrinkage predicts. DTVEM-RE is, to our knowledge, the first person-specific implementation of DTVEM-style lag detection, and it contains standard DTVEM as a special case.

AI.tldr: DTVEM-RE extends DTVEM to per-person lag heterogeneity via two confirmatory paths: a discrete-time hierarchical Bayesian VAR in Stan and a continuous-time Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model in ctsem. It demonstrates accurate recovery of between-person lag variance, strong agreement with GAMM, and improved one-step-ahead prediction, with a first per-person lag-detection approach that subsumes standard DTVEM.
AI.motivation: DTVEM assumes a common lag across individuals, conflicting with observed heterogeneity in intensive longitudinal data. DTVEM-RE introduces person-specific lag coefficients and two complementary inferential routes to handle heterogeneity and irregular timing, addressing a key gap in current practice.
AI.method: Two confirmatory versions: (i) discrete-time hierarchical Bayesian VAR in Stan that pools across people to estimate individual lag coefficients and between-person variance (tau_a); (ii) continuous-time per-person Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model in ctsem to handle unevenly spaced observations. A multi-lag extension estimates tau_k for multiple lags. Evaluated on Fisher et al. EMA data (N=40) and via simulation; compared to four discrete-time methods (including mlVAR) and GAMM.
AI.result: Simulation shows Bayesian version recovers tau_a with bias <0.01 and coverage ~90–93%. In EMA data, person-specific lag-1 effects vary by an order of magnitude across three mood items; Bayesian and GAMM estimates align closely (r=0.87–0.92); DTVEM-RE yields best one-step-ahead prediction among four discrete-time methods. Multi-lag tau_k credible intervals exclude zero for all nine lags; the lag with strongest between-person variation differs by item; lag-1 estimates from Stan and ctsem agree almost exactly (r≥0.995), differing mainly due to shrinkage. DTVEM-RE is the first person-specific DTVEM-like lag detector and contains standard DTVEM as a special case.
AI.conclusion: DTVEM-RE successfully enables person-specific lag detection, offering two complementary inferential paths and demonstrating substantive gains in inference and prediction; it generalizes standard DTVEM and supports detecting heterogeneity in lag structure across items.

title: Causal Object-Centric Models for Planning with Monte Carlo Tree Search
summary: We introduce COMET (Causal Object-centric Model for Efficient Tree search), a model-based reinforcement learning algorithm that performs Monte Carlo Tree Search in a slot-structured latent space. COMET pairs a frozen unsupervised object-centric encoder with a transformer-based world model, in which actions are bound to objects through a novel action-slot fusion mechanism that is used in slot transition prediction. Policy and value heads use object-causal attention, modulating token interactions by learned per-slot relevance scores so that decision-making concentrates on task-relevant entities. COMET adds an explicit object-level inductive bias to MuZero-style latent planning. Across eight visually and dynamically diverse tasks from the Object-Centric Visual RL benchmark, ManiSkill, Robosuite, and VizDoom, COMET achieves a higher mean normalized score during the early stages of training compared to object-centric and monolithic baselines.

AI.tldr: COMET introduces a slot-based, object-centric model-based RL framework with action-slot fusion and object-causal attention, achieving superior early-training performance across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: To imbue MuZero-style latent planning with explicit object-level inductive bias by leveraging object-centric representations and slot-based reasoning in visually rich, dynamic environments, aiming for improved data efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: COMET uses a frozen unsupervised object-centric encoder and a transformer-based world model. Actions are bound to objects via a novel action-slot fusion mechanism used for slot transition prediction. Policy and value heads employ object-causal attention with learned per-slot relevance scores to focus decisions on task-relevant entities, adding an explicit object-level inductive bias to latent planning.
AI.result: Across eight tasks from benchmarks such as Object-Centric Visual RL, ManiSkill, Robosuite, and VizDoom, COMET achieves higher mean normalized score during early training than object-centric and monolithic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicit object-centric inductive bias and slot-based reasoning improve data efficiency and early performance in model-based RL, demonstrating the value of object-level structure in diverse visual and dynamic tasks.

title: Hybrid Classical-Quantum (HCQ) Alzheimer's Classification via Supervised $β$-VAE and Quantum Kernels
summary: This paper presents a two-stage Hybrid Classical-Quantum (HCQ) pipeline for binary Alzheimer's disease (AD) classification from 3D T1-weighted structural MRI volumes, where the classical and quantum components are designed to complement each other rather than operate independently. A supervised 3D $β$-variational autoencoder (VAE) is trained end-to-end under voxel-wise reconstruction, KL-divergence, and focal classification losses that compress each 3D MRI volume (resized from 152 x 184 x 152 to 96 x 96 x 96) into a 64-dimensional latent code. Partial Least Squares (PLS) regression selects the six components in the latent code that best separate Alzheimer's Disease (AD) from cognitively normal (CN) subjects and rescales them into rotation angles, which are encoded onto a six-qubit register using the ZZ quantum feature map to give us the respective quantum states. The input to a precomputed-kernel Support Vector Machine (SVM) is an N x N Gram matrix (N = 308), created by calculating the overlap between every pair of quantum states. The novelty of this work lies in the fact that the quantum kernel operates directly on disease-aware features that are learned end-to-end by a supervised autoencoder, rather than on pre-extracted inputs. On 308 ADNI-1 subjects, consisting of 137 AD and 171 CN subjects, the baseline achieved 67.2% accuracy and 0.759 AUC, while the stability-enhanced variant reached 72.1% accuracy and 0.799 AUC with cross-fold variance halved. 3D Grad-CAM further helped validate our model's focus on brain regions linked to Alzheimer's. The HCQ pipeline could serve as a general-purpose framework for diagnostic classification across biomedical imaging domains that present similar challenges for classical approaches.

AI.tldr: A two-stage Hybrid Classical-Quantum pipeline for binary Alzheimer's disease classification from 3D MRI, combining a supervised 3D beta-VAE with a quantum kernel SVM, yielding improved accuracy (72.1%) and AUC (0.799) on 308 subjects, with Grad-CAM validation.
AI.motivation: To exploit the complementary strengths of learned, disease-aware features and quantum kernel methods for biomedical imaging, aiming to surpass purely classical or purely quantum baselines in AD classification from 3D MRI.
AI.method: Train a 3D beta-VAE with voxel-wise reconstruction, KL-divergence, and focal classification losses to compress 3D MRI (96^3) into a 64-d latent code. Use PLS to select six latent components that best separate AD and CN, rescale to rotation angles, and encode onto a six-qubit register via a ZZ quantum feature map. Compute an N x N Gram matrix (N=308) from overlaps of quantum states and train a precomputed-kernel SVM for binary AD/CN classification.
AI.result: On 308 ADNI-1 subjects (137 AD, 171 CN): baseline accuracy 67.2%, AUC 0.759; proposed stability-enhanced variant 72.1% accuracy, AUC 0.799, with cross-fold variance halved. 3D Grad-CAM corroborates focus on AD-relevant brain regions.
AI.conclusion: The HCQ pipeline demonstrates a generalizable framework that leverages end-to-end learned disease-aware features and quantum kernels, potentially improving diagnostic classification in biomedical imaging domains.

title: FloVerse: Floor Plan-Guided Multi-Modal Navigation
summary: Floor plans encapsulate compact spatial priors, enabling agents to navigate unseen scenes more efficiently. While prior work has explored floor plan-guided navigation, it has focused mainly on PointNav and a limited set of environments. To bridge this gap, we introduce FloVerse, a new task for floor plan-guided embodied navigation that unifies PointNav, ObjectNav, and ImageNav. To support FloVerse, we assemble FloVerse-1.6K, a large-scale dataset of 1.6K scenes from HM3D and Gibson 4+, paired with corresponding floor plans, comprising 240K expert trajectories and 12M RGBD frames. We further propose ThreeDiff, a two-stage imitation learning policy comprising a planner, a diffusion-based multimodal goal-reasoning module trained via masked-modality modeling, and a refiner, a depth-based trajectory-refinement module for safe execution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that (1) floor-plan priors improve navigation performance across all goal modalities, and (2) ThreeDiff implicitly captures spatial information from floor plans. These results underscore the effectiveness of spatial priors and validate our proposed unified approach for floor plan-guided embodied navigation.

AI.tldr: FloVerse introduces a unified floor plan-guided embodied navigation task (FloVerse) and a large dataset (FloVerse-1.6K) pairing 1.6k scenes with floor plans, enabling 240k expert trajectories and 12M RGBD frames. It proposes ThreeDiff, a two-stage policy with a planner, a diffusion-based multimodal goal-reasoning module trained via masked-modality modeling, and a depth-based refiner. Results show floor-plan priors improve PointNav/ObjectNav/ImageNav and ThreeDiff captures spatial information from floor plans, validating the approach.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between PointNav, ObjectNav, and ImageNav under a common floor-plan guidance paradigm; provide a scalable, paired dataset to study how floor plans encode spatial priors for embodied navigation.
AI.method: Data: FloVerse-1.6K dataset (1.6k scenes from HM3D and Gibson 4+; 240k expert trajectories; 12M RGBD frames) with corresponding floor plans. Model: ThreeDiff with a two-stage policy—(1) planner; (2) diffusion-based multimodal goal-reasoning module trained via masked-modality modeling; (3) depth-based trajectory refiner for safe execution.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation indicates floor-plan priors boost navigation performance across PointNav, ObjectNav, and ImageNav. ThreeDiff implicitly leverages spatial information from floor plans, supporting the utility of the proposed unified framework and priors.
AI.conclusion: The work validates the effectiveness of spatial priors from floor plans for unified floor plan-guided embodied navigation and presents a scalable benchmark and a strong baseline (ThreeDiff) for future research.

title: Recovering Stranded Discrimination in Knowledge Tracing: Per-Item Bias Correction via Empirical-Bayes Shrinkage
comment: 25 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Research Track). Code: https://github.com/xiaoran-y/SLC
summary: Deployed knowledge-tracing models are typically frozen after training, yet systematic per-item logit bias arises, from limited per-item expressivity in backbone architectures and from post-deployment shifts in item properties, degrading prediction quality. Global post-hoc calibrators such as Platt scaling, temperature scaling, and isotonic regression improve probability estimates but leave discriminative ability, as measured by AUC, unchanged. This AUC invariance is a structural consequence of monotone score-only transforms; recovering the stranded discrimination requires conditioning on item identity. We propose SLC (State-space Logit Correction), which converts binary observations to Gaussian pseudo-observations via Laplace/IRLS, applies empirical-Bayes shrinkage through a Kalman smoother, and fits an offset-Platt link. The state-space formulation also yields a detectability bound that characterizes the Bernoulli information floor, explaining why temporal tracking provides no benefit at current data densities. Across four datasets, five backbones, and three seeds, SLC improves AUC on all four datasets and NLL on three, with the advantage concentrating on sparse items. Cross-domain controls suggest that the same phenomenon can arise beyond education when the deployed backbone leaves entity-level bias.

AI.tldr: Proposes SLC (State-space Logit Correction) to fix per-item logit bias in deployed knowledge-tracing models, overcoming AUC stagnation from post-hoc calibrators by conditioning on item identity and using a state-space, Kalman-smoothing framework with Laplace/IRLS pseudo-observations and an offset-Platt link; shows improvements in AUC and NLL across multiple datasets, backbones, seeds, especially for sparse items, and derives a detectability bound explaining limited temporal gains.
AI.motivation: Deployed KT models are often fixed post-training and exhibit item-specific logit biases due to limited per-item expressivity and shifts in item properties after deployment. Global post-hoc calibrators improve calibration but do not enhance discriminative ability (AUC) due to monotone score-only transforms. There is a need to recover discrimination by conditioning on item identity and to understand how information density affects temporal tracking.
AI.method: Introduce SLC: convert binary observations to Gaussian pseudo-observations using Laplace/IRLS; apply empirical-Bayes shrinkage via a Kalman smoother; fit an offset-Platt link. The approach is framed as a state-space model, deriving a detectability bound that characterizes the Bernoulli information floor. Evaluation across four datasets, five backbones, and three seeds.
AI.result: SLC consistently improves AUC on all four datasets and reduces NLL on three, with the largest gains for sparse items. Cross-domain controls indicate the phenomenon extends beyond education when the deployed backbone leaves entity-level bias.
AI.conclusion: SLC effectively addresses per-item logit bias and the AUC invariance of global calibrators by conditioning on item identity within a state-space framework, providing a theoretical detectability bound and demonstrable empirical gains across diverse datasets and architectures, with potential applicability beyond education.

title: GitOfThoughts: Version-Controlled Reasoning and Agent Memory You Can Replay, Diff, and Merge
summary:
Large language model (LLM) reasoning is ephemeral: chains of thought vanish with the context window, pruned search branches leave no record, and memory buffers cannot be diffed, merged, or audited. Every other complex software process (code, infrastructure, data, experiments) is version-controlled; reasoning is not. We introduce GitOfThoughts, which stores an agent's reasoning tree as a git repository: every scored thought is a commit, scores are notes, outcomes are tags, and retrieval is "git log" over the agent's own history. This makes reasoning replayable, auditable, and mergeable across agents at near-zero engineering cost.
  We then ask the harder question: does memory, in any substrate, actually improve accuracy? Across five substrates (none, markdown, vector, graph, git), two benchmarks, two model scales, and pre-registered replications, the answer for novel problems is no. No memory format reliably helps, and a promising early result collapsed under its own pre-registered replication. Memory pays only above what we call the copyability threshold: when the retrieved case is a near-duplicate of the current problem (similarity >~ 0.8), accuracy jumps sharply; below it, nothing. The gain is answer retrieval, not method transfer: a 4.5x larger model doubles the near-duplicate payoff yet still cannot extract a transferable method from a worked example. The only general lever we find is test-time sampling. The case for git-as-substrate is therefore auditability, provenance, and mergeability at accuracy parity. We document a retracted result and a refuted hypothesis to model the evaluation standard we hold ourselves to.

AI.tldr: GitOfThoughts stores an agent’s reasoning as a git repository to enable auditability, provenance, and mergeability, but careful memory (external retrieval) generally doesn’t improve accuracy except when retrieved cases are near-duplicates; test-time sampling remains the main lever.
AI.motivation: Address the ephemeral nature of LLM reasoning (context window limits, non-mergeable traces) by imposing version-control on internal reasoning to enable replay, auditing, and collaboration across agents.
AI.method: Introduce GitOfThoughts that encodes each scored thought as a commit, scores as notes, outcomes as tags, and retrieval via git log over the agent’s history. Conduct memory studies across five substrates (none, markdown, vector, graph, git), two benchmarks, two model scales, with pre-registered replications to test whether memory formats help transfer or improve accuracy.
AI.result: Across novel problems, memory formats do not reliably improve accuracy; only near-duplicate retrieval (similarity ≈ 0.8) yields sharp gains, primarily from answer retrieval. A larger model increases the near-duplicate payoff but does not enable transferable method transfer. Test-time sampling is the only general lever. The gains of a git substrate lie in auditability, provenance, and mergeability at parity of accuracy; one result was retracted and a hypothesis refuted under pre-registered evaluation standards.
AI.conclusion: Memory is not a universal booster for LLM reasoning; the primary benefits of a versioned reasoning substrate are auditability, provenance, and easy mergeability, with performance parity achieved except in limited near-duplicate retrieval scenarios.

title: A Multi-Domain Feature Fusion Framework for Generalizable Deepfake Detection Across Different Generators
summary: Deepfakes are artificially generated images, audio, or videos that threaten privacy, security, and information integrity. Detecting such content is crucial for countering disinformation, as the latest models generate highly realistic content. While spatial- or frequency-based approaches achieve good detection rates on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)-based generated deepfakes, they often struggle with recent diffusion model-generated images. In particular, existing approaches rarely exploit complementary multi-domain representations or systematically evaluate cross-generator robustness. To address these challenges, we propose a multi-domain deepfake detection framework called SGFF-Net (Spatial-Gradient-Frequency Fusion Network) that integrates spatial, gradient, and DWT (Discrete Wavelet Transform)-based frequency representations within a dual residual learning architecture. Experimental results show that the SGFF-Net achieves 98.95\% accuracy in intra-dataset evaluation and improves performance in both cross-model (70.46\%) and cross-paradigm (69.94\%) settings. Incorporating multi-source training and data augmentation further enhances robustness, increasing accuracy from 70.46\% to 79.80\% in cross-model evaluation, from 69\% to 78\% in cross-paradigm evaluation, and from 61.50\% to 75.80\% on real-world data. Unlike single-domain detectors, the SGFF-Net learns complementary forensic cues across spatial, gradient, and wavelet-frequency domains, resulting in greater robustness under cross-generator and cross-paradigm evaluation. The results further show that combining multi-domain representations with data diversity and augmentation substantially improves generalization, providing practical insights for developing more reliable deepfake detection systems.

AI.tldr: SGFF-Net: a multi-domain detector that fuses spatial, gradient, and wavelet-frequency cues via dual residual learning to boost cross-generator/paradigm robustness in deepfake detection.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of single-domain detectors, particularly weak generalization to diffusion-model deepfakes and lack of systematic cross-generator evaluation; develop a framework that leverages complementary domain cues and robust data augmentation.
AI.method: Propose SGFF-Net that integrates spatial, gradient, and DWT-based frequency representations in a dual residual architecture, with multi-domain fusion and training strategies (multi-source data and augmentation).
AI.result: Intra-dataset accuracy: 98.95%. Cross-model accuracy: 70.46% (baseline) rising to 79.80% with augmentation/multi-source training. Cross-paradigm accuracy: 69.94% (baseline) rising to 78.00% with augmentation. Real-world data accuracy: 61.50% (baseline) rising to 75.80%. The method demonstrates learning of complementary cues across domains and improved generalization under cross-generator and cross-paradigm settings.
AI.conclusion: Multi-domain spatial/gradient/wavelet-frequency fusion with data diversity yields more robust, generalizable deepfake detectors across generators and paradigms, offering practical guidance for reliable deployment.

title: Robust Fall Recovery for Armless Bipedal-Wheeled Robots Via Force-Guided Learning
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, accepted by IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Fall recovery is critical for autonomous legged locomotion. Existing methods have demonstrated that some legged robots, such as humanoids and quadrupeds, are capable of fall recovery from diverse postures by utilizing arms or coordinating multi-legs to generate support forces. Without arms or other legs to provide supportive assistance, a bipedal-wheeled robot must rely solely on the actuation of its legs, making recovery particularly difficult. To address this, we introduce FTSR (Force-guided Teacher-student framework with Stage-wise Rewards). The force-guided method constructs an external auxiliary force during simulation training that correlates directly with the robot's real-time height, explicitly formulating this force as an optimizable constraint. Through constrained reinforcement learning, the policy is guided toward reducing force dependency gradually and increasing the body height, developing internal recovery strategies despite having no arms for support. Height-progressive stage-Wise rewards progressively structure posture stabilization during recovery and transition to sustained locomotion, integrated with teacher-student architecture distilling privileged knowledge of force effects and recovery dynamics. After simulation training, the policy is deployed on a physical armless bipedal-wheeled robot and extensively evaluated. Experiments confirm robust and reliable fall recovery under diverse challenging conditions, demonstrating strong environmental adaptability and motion robustness, while maintaining full post-recovery motion capability. The framework also generalizes effectively to a high-DOF humanoid, confirming its practical generalizability. The project page is available at https://2350575870.github.io/force-guided.github.io/

AI.tldr: Introduce FTSR, a force-guided teacher-student reinforcement learning framework with stage-wise rewards enabling fall recovery for armless bipedal-wheeled robots, validated in simulation and on hardware and generalizing to high-DOF humanoids.
AI.motivation: Falls are a major failure mode for legged robots lacking arms; prior work relies on limbs for support, leaving armless configurations requiring internal strategies. The goal is robust fall recovery and seamless post-fall locomotion without external support.
