AI.motivation: Address the challenge of robust and precise manipulation from a single global view, where policy must capture fine-grained interactions and identify task-relevant regions without relying on multi-camera wrist views.
AI.method: A diffusion-based visuomotor policy with two components: (i) a visual encoder producing multi-scale feature maps for broad context and fine details, and (ii) a spatial conditioning module that samples point-wise features along intermediate end-effector trajectories inside the diffusion loop, using end-effector trajectories as attention anchors.
AI.result: In simulation, SCDP outperforms strong single-view baselines and matches multi-camera baselines; in real-world tests, it demonstrates precise manipulation and robustness to visual distractors.
AI.conclusion: Single-camera imitation learning can achieve competitive performance with diffusion-based policies and spatial conditioning centered on end-effector trajectories, reducing reliance on multi-camera setups.

title: Curvature-Guided Geometric Representation for Protein-Ligand Binding Affinity Prediction
summary: Protein-ligand binding affinity (PLA) prediction is critical in drug discovery. Despite the notable advancements in machine learning-based approaches, existing methods struggle to jointly characterize local geometric organization and globally coordinated cross-molecular interactions, limiting their ability to model complex binding mechanisms. Here, we propose RicciBind, a geometric representation framework that integrates curvature-guided hierarchical structure learning with optimal transport (OT)-based cross-domain alignment to model molecular interactions. Specifically, RicciBind leverages Ricci curvature to capture local interaction tightness within molecular structures, enhancing structural awareness and organizing atomic interactions into curvature-aware hierarchical representations. An OT-based cluster matching mechanism then aligns protein and ligand clusters across heterogeneous domains under geometric constraints, enabling globally consistent correspondences and revealing higher-order interaction patterns beyond local neighborhoods. By coupling curvature-guided structure encoding with OT-driven cross-domain alignment, RicciBind effectively models complex interaction semantics and substantially improves both the accuracy and interpretability of binding affinity prediction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RicciBind achieved superior predictive performance and generalization across PLA benchmarks and virtual screening tasks. Ablation studies further confirmed the essential role of Ricci curvature in enhancing molecular interaction representations.

AI.tldr: Introduces RicciBind, a geometric framework for protein–ligand binding affinity prediction that jointly encodes local curvature-driven structure with OT-based cross-domain alignment of protein/ligand clusters, improving accuracy, generalization, and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Current PLA models struggle to jointly capture local geometric organization and globally coordinated cross-molecular interactions; a curvature-guided representation plus cross-domain alignment may better model complex binding mechanisms.
AI.method: 1) Use Ricci curvature to capture local interaction tightness and build curvature-aware hierarchical representations of molecular structures. 2) Apply an optimal transport-based cluster matching to align protein and ligand clusters across heterogeneous domains under geometric constraints, producing globally consistent correspondences.
AI.result: Empirical results show RicciBind achieves superior predictive performance and generalization on PLA benchmarks and virtual screening tasks; ablation studies confirm the essential role of Ricci curvature in enhancing representations.
AI.conclusion: Coupling curvature-guided encoding with OT-driven cross-domain alignment yields improved binding affinity prediction, offering improved accuracy and interpretability and suggesting broader applicability to modeling higher-order interaction patterns.

title: Abstracting Cross-Domain Action Sequences into Interpretable Workflows
summary: Sequential or time-stamped interaction logs provide objective records of digital application usage, yet their granularity and noise often obscure meaningful insights into people's work. Such insights are essential for improving digital products in ways grounded in real-world user interactions. Prior research has applied deep learning models to cluster user actions into high-level activities, but these approaches are highly sensitive to noise and struggle to generalize across applications. To address this limitation, we introduce WorkflowView, a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to abstract low-level action sequences into high-level activities. We establish the effectiveness and generality of our approach across three distinct, challenging sequential tasks and diverse domains: (a) zero-shot task description reconstruction from browser logs (achieving high semantic similarity, $μ_{sim} = 0.91$), (b) few-shot student dropout prediction using MOOC interaction logs (reaching weighted $F_1 = 0.90$ with only five few-shot examples), and (c) anonymized, privacy-preserving analysis of AI tool integration within document workflows in Microsoft Word. Our work demonstrates that LLM-based abstraction is a robust and efficient path forward for transforming low-level behavioral data into high-level, interpretable, and actionable insights. We also discuss practical considerations for deploying LLM-based inferences within logging infrastructures, including computational efficiency and user privacy.

AI.tldr: WorkflowView uses large language models to convert noisy, low-level user action sequences into high-level activities, enabling robust, domain-general insights across multiple sequential-task domains (zero-shot semantic reconstruction, few-shot dropout prediction, and privacy-preserving analysis in Word).
AI.motivation: Sequential logs are granular and noisy; traditional deep models struggle to generalize across applications. High-level, interpretable abstractions can improve real-world product insights and cross-domain applicability.
AI.method: Apply LLM-based abstraction to map low-level action sequences to high-level activities. Evaluate on three tasks: (a) zero-shot task description reconstruction from browser logs (μ_sim=0.91); (b) few-shot student dropout prediction using MOOC interaction logs (weighted F1=0.90 with five shots); (c) anonymized, privacy-preserving analysis of AI tool integration within Word workflows.
AI.result: LLM-based abstraction demonstrates effectiveness and generality across diverse domains and tasks, yielding high semantic similarity, strong predictive performance with minimal labeled data, and privacy-preserving insights into tool usage in documents.
AI.conclusion: Abstraction of low-level behavioral data via LLMs is a robust, efficient approach for producing high-level, interpretable insights; practical deployment requires attention to computational efficiency and user privacy.

title: ForceForget: Reinforcement Concept Removal for Enhancing Safety in Text-to-Image Models
summary: With the advance of generative AI, the text-to-image (T2I) model has the ability to generate various contents. However, T2I models still can generate unsafe contents. To alleviate this issue, various concept erasing methods are proposed. However, existing methods tend to excessively erase unsafe concepts and suppress benign concepts contained in harmful prompts, which can negatively affect model utility. In this paper, we focus on eliminating unsafe content while maintaining model capability in safe semantic meaning interpretation by optimizing the concept erasing reward (CER) with reinforcement learning. To avoid overly content erasure, we introduce the Safe Adapter to project partial text embedding for efficient concept regulation in cross-attention layers. Extensive experiments conducted on different datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in alleviating unsafe content generation while preserving the high fidelity of benign images compared with existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) concept erasing methods. In terms of robustness, our method outperforms counterparts against red-teaming tools. Moreover, we showcase the proposed approach is more effective in emerging image-to-image (I2I) scenarios compared with others. Lastly, we extend our method to erase general concepts, such as artistic styles and objects. Disclaimer: This paper includes discussions of sexually explicit content that may be offensive to certain readers. All images used in this work are synthesized or from public datasets.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement-learning-based concept erasing framework (CER) with a Safe Adapter reduces unsafe content generation in text-to-image models while preserving benign semantics and image fidelity; robust to red-teaming and extends to image-to-image and general concept erasure.
AI.motivation: Existing concept erasing methods tend to over-erase, suppressing benign content and harming utility. The work targets safe content removal while maintaining the model’s capability to interpret safe semantic meaning.
AI.method: Optimize a concept erasing reward (CER) with reinforcement learning. Introduce a Safe Adapter that projects partial text embeddings to regulate cross-attention, enabling partial concept regulation without full erasure. Applies to both text-to-image and image-to-image scenarios and extends to general concepts such as artistic styles and objects.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art concept erasing methods across datasets, reducing unsafe content generation while preserving high-fidelity benign images. Demonstrates robustness against red-teaming tools and superior performance in I2I scenarios. The approach generalizes to erasing broader concepts beyond unsafe content.
AI.conclusion: CER with Safe Adapter offers safer generation without sacrificing utility, improving safety and robustness while maintaining semantic fidelity. It generalizes to broader concept erasure tasks, though practical limitations and potential biases should be explored in future work.

title: TRACE: Trajectory-Routed Causal Memory for Delayed-Evidence Visuomotor Imitation
summary: Robots under autonomous operation may require decisions based on evidence that is no longer visible. We study \emph{delayed-evidence} tasks, where an early cue disappears before a later decision point, so visually similar observations can require different actions. In these settings, the current observation is not a sufficient state for control. We introduce TRAjectory-routed Causal Evidence (TRACE), a memory framework for visuomotor imitation policies. TRACE stores task-relevant visual and robot-state evidence, such as object identity, target choice, or route-dependent state, in a fixed-size latent memory that remains bounded over long episodes. Instead of indexing memory by raw time or manually provided task labels, TRACE uses \emph{path signatures}: compact, order-sensitive features of the executed robot-state trajectory. These signatures do not store the visual cue itself; rather, they provide trajectory-conditioned keys for writing and retrieving the evidence stored when the cue was visible. When the robot later reaches an ambiguous observation, the policy conditions on TRACE memory to recover the missing context and choose the correct branch. TRACE attaches through lightweight adapters to policies, without changing the policy backbone, action head, or imitation objective. Across real-world long-horizon manipulation tasks with visually ambiguous branch points, TRACE improves branch selection and task success over alternative baselines, including short-history and recurrent memory. Project page: https://jeong-zju.github.io/trace

AI.tldr: TRACE introduces a memory module for visuomotor imitation policies that uses path signatures to store and retrieve task-relevant evidence from past observations, enabling correct branch decisions when cues are no longer visible.
AI.motivation: In delayed-evidence tasks, early cues vanish before a decision, making current observations insufficient for control; a bounded, trajectory-conditioned memory can improve decision quality in long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Attach a memory module to policies via lightweight adapters; store task-relevant visual/robot-state evidence in a fixed-size latent memory indexed by path signatures (order-sensitive features of the executed trajectory) rather than raw time or labels; use the memory to condition policy at ambiguous observations.
AI.result: Empirically improves branch selection and task success over baselines (short-history, recurrent memory) across real-world long-horizon manipulation tasks with visually ambiguous branch points.
AI.conclusion: TRACE provides an effective, bounded memory mechanism that leverages trajectory-conditioned keys to retrieve context, enhancing decisions without altering policy backbone or learning objective.

title: Machine Learning for Biomedical Raman Spectroscopy: From Spectral Acquisition to Clinical Translation
summary:
Raman spectroscopy provides label-free, chemically specific characterization of biological systems and has become an important tool for cancer diagnosis, molecular subtyping, microbiological identification, and intraoperative decision support. Biomedical Raman spectra are, however, high-dimensional, noisy, and affected by fluorescence background, acquisition variability, and biological heterogeneity, making robust computational analysis essential.
  This review examines the role of machine learning across the biomedical Raman spectroscopy pipeline, from preprocessing and signal correction to unsupervised structure discovery, supervised diagnosis and molecular stratification, representation and transfer learning, explainability, biomarker discovery, and multimodal integration with imaging, pathology, and molecular profiling. Emphasis is placed on the use of machine learning not only for diagnostic classification, but also for biologically interpretable and clinically actionable analysis.
  We also discuss the main barriers to clinical translation, including limited dataset sizes, inter-instrument variability, inconsistent preprocessing, insufficient external validation, reproducibility concerns, and limited sharing of software, data, and metadata. We argue that progress will require methodological advances together with standardization, robust validation, explainability, and deployment-ready analytical frameworks. By integrating methodological, biomedical, and translational perspectives, this review outlines key directions for developing reliable and clinically deployable Raman-AI systems.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of machine learning in biomedical Raman spectroscopy, outlining pipeline stages, challenges, and directions for clinically deployable Raman-AI systems.
AI.motivation: Biomedical Raman spectra are high-dimensional, noisy, and affected by fluorescence/background, acquisition variability, and biological heterogeneity, necessitating ML for robust interpretation and clinical translation.
AI.method: Systematic literature review and synthesis across the Raman-AI pipeline: preprocessing and signal correction; unsupervised structure discovery; supervised diagnosis and molecular stratification; representation/transfer learning; explainability; biomarker discovery; and multimodal integration with imaging, pathology, and molecular profiling; plus discussion of translational barriers.
AI.result: The review maps how ML contributes across the Raman spectroscopy pipeline, highlights the emphasis on not only diagnostic accuracy but biologically interpretable and clinically actionable insights, and identifies core barriers to translation (limited data, inter-instrument variability, preprocessing inconsistency, lack of external validation, reproducibility concerns, and limited sharing of software/data/meta). It advocates standardization, robust validation, explainability, and deployment-ready analytical frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Progress will require methodological advances paired with standardization, robust external validation, explainable models, and broader software/data sharing to enable reliable, clinically deployable Raman-AI systems.

title: Towards Direct Latent-Space Synthesis for Parallel Branches in LLM-Agent Workflows
summary: Large language models increasingly serve as execution engines for agentic systems, yet they still consume context through a sequential text interface. This creates a mismatch with modern structured agent workflows, in which independent branches explore subtasks, retrieve evidence, or generate candidate solutions before a final synthesis step. Existing systems typically merge these branches by concatenating their textual outputs, which discards the parallel structure and incurs redundant prefill computation. In this work, we introduce Parallel-Synthesis, a plug-and-play framework that enables a synthesizer to directly consume the KV caches produced by parallel worker agents. Parallel-Synthesis combines a cache mapper that calibrates independently generated branch caches with a fine-tuned synthesizer adapter that enables generation from this non-sequential cache interface. We train Parallel-Synthesis using data that exposes the synthesizer to parallel cache contexts, teaches aggregation across cached branches, and distills reasoning behavior from standard text-concatenation-based synthesis. Across nine downstream datasets spanning math, science QA, code generation, GAIA, and multi-agent database diagnosis, Parallel-Synthesis matches or outperforms text-based synthesis on seven datasets and remains close on the other two. It also reduces time-to-first-token by 2.5x-11x, suggesting that direct cache-based synthesis is a promising interface for more native and efficient synthesis over parallel agent branches.

AI.tldr: A plug-and-play framework enabling syntheses to consume parallel workers' KV caches for direct, cache-based synthesis, yielding faster and competitive results across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM agent workflows are increasingly parallel, but traditional synthesis merges branches via text concatenation, which wastes computation and loses parallel structure.
AI.method: Introduce a cache mapper to calibrate independently generated branch caches and a synthesizer adapter to generate from the non-sequential cache interface; train with parallel-cache contexts, aggregation across branches, and distillation from text-concatenation synthesis.
AI.result: On nine downstream datasets (math, science QA, code generation, GAIA, multi-agent database diagnosis), matches or surpasses text-based synthesis on seven datasets and is close on the remaining two; time-to-first-token reduced by 2.5x to 11x.
AI.conclusion: Cache-based synthesis is a promising, more native and efficient interface for synthesizing over parallel agent branches.

title: Point Cloud Upsampling through Patch-based Frequency Superposition
summary: In recent years, neural networks have become the dominant models in most point cloud upsampling methods. Although these approaches are achieving good results, they do have drawbacks, such as a lack of interpretability and data dependency. Moreover, they have to be trained on a dataset that is similar to the test data in order to perform well. To avoid these disadvantages, we propose Point Cloud Upsampling through Patch-based Frequency Superposition (PUtPFS), an optimization-based approach that selects subsets of points and estimates the surface of this set through superpositioning spatial frequencies. Then, new points are placed on this surface. By successively selecting points in the least dense regions of the point cloud, a uniform upsampling can be reached. With this method, we surpass the current best upsampling results in the commonly considered point-to-surface distance. Furthermore, we achieve the best Chamfer and Hausdorff distance among the optimization-based approaches. As an additional advantage, our method does not need any training data and is mathematically interpretable.

AI.tldr: An optimization-based, patch-level frequency superposition approach for training-free point cloud upsampling, achieving state-of-the-art among optimization-based methods.
AI.motivation: Address the interpretability and data-dependency issues of neural upsampling by providing a training-free, mathematically interpretable alternative.
AI.method: Select subsets of points, estimate their surface via superposition of spatial frequencies on patches, place new points on this surface, and iteratively sample in the least-dense regions to achieve uniform upsampling.
AI.result: Outperforms existing optimization-based upsampling methods on point-to-surface distance; achieves best Chamfer and Hausdorff distances among optimization-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates that optimization-based, training-free approaches can be competitive with neural methods, offering interpretability and reduced data dependency.

title: ORCA: A Platform for Open-Source Dexterity Research
summary: Robotics manipulation research increasingly focuses on two-finger parallel grippers for their effectiveness, affordability, and ease of teleoperation. Grippers are nonetheless limited by their form factor, often requiring bimanual setups even for simple reorientation tasks. Anthropomorphic hands are a more natural platform for dexterous robot learning -- closer to the human hand, and capable of learning from human video -- yet they remain hard to use in learning research: even where open and accessible hand hardware exists, the software for control, simulation, teleoperation, and retargeting is scattered in one-off code bases, and largely disconnected from the robot-learning ecosystem. In this work, we introduce the \orca~learning stack, an open-source research stack for dexterity as a first-class robot learning domain. Our \orca~stack unifies low-level control, simulation, teleoperation from a range of consumer platforms, and hand retargeting, behind a single interface, and integrates natively with popular robot-learning frameworks such as \lerobot, so dexterous hand researchers can leverage the same data, training, and evaluation pipelines used for non-dexterous robot learning. We demonstrate a complete end-to-end workflow, collecting expert demonstrations of an in-hand reorientation task by teleoperation with a consumer-grade VR headset, training an autonomous policy with \lerobot, and evaluating the learned policy in a fully reproducible and observable setup. We open-source the entire stack as a shared, reproducible foundation for dexterous-manipulation research.

AI.tldr: Open-source ORCA stack unifies control, simulation, teleoperation, and hand retargeting for dexterous manipulation, enabling end-to-end demonstrations and RL integration.
AI.motivation: Dexterous hand research is hampered by fragmented tools; a unified, reproducible stack that aligns dexterous manipulation with standard robot-learning pipelines is needed.
AI.method: Develop ORCA stack consolidating low-level control, simulation, teleoperation from consumer hardware, and hand retargeting; integrate natively with popular robot-learning frameworks (e.g., lerobot); demonstrate end-to-end workflow: teleoperation-based demonstrations via VR, autonomous policy training using lerobot, reproducible evaluation; open-source release.
AI.result: Demonstrates a complete end-to-end workflow for in-hand reorientation; expert demonstrations collected via teleoperation; autonomous policy trained; evaluated in a reproducible, observable setup; entire stack released as a shared foundation for dexterous manipulation research.
AI.conclusion: ORCA provides a reusable, community-friendly platform that brings dexterous hand manipulation into the standard robot-learning ecosystem, enabling reproducible research and easier experimentation.

title: Context-aware Modality-Topology Co-Alignment for Multimodal Attributed Graphs
summary: Multimodal Attributed Graphs (MAGs) model real-world entities by coupling graph topology with heterogeneous attributes such as text and images. They support graph-centric tasks requiring structural and class-discriminative representations, and modality-centric tasks requiring fine-grained cross-modal correspondence. However, existing MAG methods often rely on fixed graph contexts or uniformly fused representations, causing task-agnostic propagation and over-compressed fusion that hinder diverse task requirements and modality-specific evidence preservation. To address this, we propose CoMAG, a unified MAG backbone that learns task-adaptive reliable contexts and modality-preserving alignment within them. CoMAG first conducts Reliable Context Learning by estimating edge reliability from multimodal semantic consistency, complementing raw topology with semantic neighbors, and selecting context components through a task-aware gate. It then performs Modality-preserving Hop-token Alignment by maintaining modality-specific multi-hop trajectories, matching modality-hop tokens across modalities, and decoupling shared and private representations. Thus, CoMAG produces graph and modality representations from one forward pass while retaining modality-specific cues. We further analyze stable propagation, over-smoothing mitigation, and modality-collapse control. Experiments on nine OpenMAG datasets compare CoMAG with feature-only, graph-only, multimodal, and unified MAG baselines across graph-level prediction, modality matching, and graph-conditioned generation. Results show that CoMAG achieves the best reported performance, demonstrating that task-adaptive reliable contexts and modality-preserving alignment improve structural prediction, cross-modal matching, and graph-conditioned generation while retaining sparse edge-linear complexity.

AI.tldr: CoMAG presents a unified MAG backbone that learns task-adaptive reliable contexts and modality-preserving cross-modal alignment, enabling graph-centric and modality-centric tasks in one pass with sparse complexity.
AI.motivation: Current MAG methods rely on fixed graph contexts or uniform fusion, leading to task-agnostic propagation and loss of modality-specific evidence. There is a need for task-aware context selection and modality-preserving alignment.
AI.method: 1) Reliable Context Learning: estimate edge reliability from multimodal semantic consistency, augment topology with semantic neighbors, and select context via a task-aware gate. 2) Modality-preserving Hop-token Alignment: maintain modality-specific multi-hop trajectories, align modality-hop tokens across modalities, and decouple shared/private representations. 3) One-pass computation producing graph and modality representations; analysis of propagation stability, over-smoothing, and modality-collapse.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on nine OpenMAG datasets shows CoMAG achieving state-of-the-art performance across graph-level prediction, modality matching, and graph-conditioned generation, while maintaining sparse edge-linear complexity.
AI.conclusion: Task-adaptive reliable contexts and modality-preserving alignment enhance structural prediction, cross-modal matching, and generation with efficient computation.

title: Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning
summary: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

AI.tldr: Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB) optimizes ensemble weights on the probability simplex with a concave quadratic penalty to sparsify bootstrap ensembles by minimizing OOB loss; achieves substantial compression and better calibration while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Improve probability calibration and reduce ensemble size of bootstrap ensembles without sacrificing accuracy, addressing overconfidence from uniform voting and the lack of sparsity with L1 on the simplex.
AI.method: Formulates joint optimization of ensemble weights on the probability simplex, minimizing the OOB loss. Introduces a concave quadratic penalty to induce sparsity (addressing the L1-simplex paradox). Model-agnostic, applicable to RFs, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks; reports high compression, speedups, and improved calibration.
AI.result: Up to 96% ensemble compression with linear inference speedups and improved calibration (lower Expected Calibration Error), while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SCSB provides a principled, model-agnostic approach to post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap ensembles, solving the L1-simplex paradox and delivering practical gains in efficiency and predictive reliability.

title: FLaRA: Predicting Future Latent Representations for Accident Anticipation
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC 2026)
summary: Anticipating traffic accidents from dashcam videos is a critical challenge in intelligent transportation systems. Existing methods typically map visual context directly to a collision probability without explicitly modeling the future evolution of the driving scene. In this paper we propose FLaRA (Predicting Future Latent Representations for Accident Anticipation), a novel predictive architecture that shifts this paradigm by forecasting future latent representations for accident anticipation. Building upon the Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA2), our model conditions a predictor network on observed context frames to predict the forthcoming latent features of the scene. A classifier then operates on these predicted future representations rather than only on past observations. To ensure these forecasts remain grounded in realistic future dynamics, we introduce a joint training objective that simultaneously optimizes an auxiliary feature-level reconstruction loss and a cross-entropy classification loss. Extensive evaluations on the Nexar dataset, alongside cross-domain validations on the DAD, DADA-2000, and DoTA benchmarks, demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining realistic early warning capabilities.

AI.tldr: Predicts future latent representations to anticipate dashcam accidents, using FLaRA on V-JEPA2 with joint feature reconstruction and classification losses; achieves state-of-the-art results and robust early warnings.
AI.motivation: Existing accident-anticipation methods map current visuals to collision probability without explicitly modeling how the scene will evolve. Forecasting future dynamics could enable earlier and more reliable warnings.
AI.method: Condition a predictor network on observed frames to forecast upcoming latent features; a classifier operates on these predicted future representations instead of only past frames; training uses a feature-level reconstruction loss plus a cross-entropy loss for accident anticipation, extending Video JEPA (V-JEPA2).
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on the Nexar dataset and strong cross-domain results on DAD, DADA-2000, and DoTA benchmarks; demonstrated realistic early warning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Forecasting future latent representations is a viable strategy for accident anticipation, improving early warning and generalization across datasets; the joint objective helps keep forecasts grounded in plausible future dynamics.

title: Sensitivity Shaping for Latent Modeling
summary: Generative dynamics models enable planning in challenging robotic systems, but safe deployment requires reliably detecting policy-induced out-of-distribution (OOD) transitions. Existing methods typically treat the learned dynamics as fixed and attach post hoc support surrogates. We show that these surrogates can fail when the dynamics are locally insensitive to critical action choices: unsupported control actions may produce latent predictions that resemble demonstrated transitions, suppressing OOD signals despite large true predictive errors. To address this, we introduce support-conditioned control-sensitivity regularization, which promotes sensitive local response to control input changes in learned dynamics in high-support training regions. This preserves control-induced variation while limiting unstable extrapolation due to weak empirical support. Experiments in vision-based obstacle avoidance, manipulation, and real-robot navigation show improved OOD detection and safer closed-loop planning.

AI.tldr: Introduce a method called support-conditioned control-sensitivity regularization (SC-CSR) for learned generative dynamics to ensure they remain sensitive to control inputs in regions with high data support, improving OOD detection and safe planning in robotics.
AI.motivation: Existing OOD detection for learned dynamics relies on post hoc support surrogates that may fail when the dynamics are locally insensitive to certain actions, causing unsafe planning due to masked predictive errors.
AI.method: Propose SC-CSR: a regularization term that promotes sensitive local response to control input changes in learned dynamics within high-support training regions, preserving control-induced variation while constraining extrapolation in under-supported regions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across vision-based obstacle avoidance, manipulation, and real-robot navigation shows improved OOD detection and safer closed-loop planning when using the proposed regularization compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: SC-CSR improves reliability of OOD detection in learned dynamics used for planning, enabling safer deployment of robot systems by aligning model sensitivity with data support.

title: Zeta: Dual Whitening for Matrix Optimization via Coordinate-Adaptive Preconditioning
summary: Large-scale neural network training increasingly relies on matrix-aware optimizers that exploit the structure of weight parameters beyond element-wise adaptation. However, existing matrix-aware methods such as Muon have an underappreciated vulnerability: their core operation, Newton-Schulz iteration, depends critically on input conditioning, yet the raw momentum matrices exhibit severe coordinate-wise scale heterogeneity. In this paper, we first verify this scale heterogeneity through a chi-square uniformity test, showing that intra-matrix scale imbalance is prevalent across Transformer layers and that coordinate whitening effectively corrects it. Motivated by this finding, we propose Zeta, a dual whitening optimizer that applies coordinate whitening and spectral whitening in a strictly ordered pipeline. The ordering is not a tunable choice but follows from a mathematical dependency: coordinate whitening establishes the statistical isotropy that spectral whitening requires to function reliably. We further prove that this dual pipeline strictly reduces orthogonalization error relative to pure spectral methods by improving the condition number of the input. Empirically, Zeta matches or surpasses strong baselines across language modeling (0.6B to 8B parameters), mixture-of-experts architectures, and vision tasks, demonstrating that resolving scale imbalance before orthogonalization leads to faster convergence and better generalization. Code is available at https://gitcode.com/kevin259/MindSpeed.

AI.tldr: Zeta is a dual whitening optimizer that first whitenes coordinates (to isotropy) and then applies spectral whitening, in a fixed order, to improve conditioning and reduce orthogonalization error for matrix-aware optimizers like Muon. It addresses scale heterogeneity in momentum matrices found in Transformer layers, validated by a chi-square test, and yields faster convergence and better generalization across language, MoE, and vision tasks; code released.
AI.motivation: Matrix-aware optimizers (e.g., Muon) rely on the Newton-Schulz iteration, which is sensitive to input conditioning. Raw momentum matrices exhibit scale heterogeneity across coordinates, hurting stability and performance. Whitenings aim to restore isotropy and improve conditioning.
AI.method: Propose Zeta, a dual whitening pipeline that applies coordinate whitening followed by spectral whitening in a strictly ordered fashion. Prove that this ordering reduces orthogonalization error by improving the input condition number compared to spectral whitening alone. Verify scale heterogeneity with a chi-square uniformity test and demonstrate empirical benefits across large-scale language models (0.6B–8B params), MoE architectures, and vision tasks. Provide code.
AI.result: Zeta matches or surpasses strong baselines, converges faster, and generalizes better across diverse tasks by correcting scale imbalance prior to orthogonalization. The dual whitening pipeline effectively reduces orthogonalization error and improves conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Resolving scale imbalance before orthogonalization is essential for stable and efficient matrix-aware optimization. The strictly ordered dual whitening (coordinate then spectral) yields better conditioning and generalization than spectral whitening alone; the approach generalizes to language, MoE, and vision domains.

title: IndustryBench-MIPU: Benchmarking Multi-Image Attribute Value Extraction for Industrial Products
summary: Industrial products such as valves and circuit breakers are defined by dense technical specifications that govern procurement, compatibility, and safety across supply chains. These specifications are scattered across multiple heterogeneous product images, including specification tables, nameplates, and technical drawings, yet whether Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can reliably recover them remains underexplored. To fill this gap, we introduce IndustryBench-MIPU, the first large-scale benchmark for multi-image industrial product understanding, built around structured attribute extraction -- recovering property-value pairs from product images. This task jointly probes text recognition on specification tables and nameplates, visual reasoning over technical drawings, domain knowledge to decode industrial terminology, and cross-image evidence integration to assemble scattered specifications. Concretely, the benchmark comprises 4,559 products across 27,652 images with 103,703 annotations spanning 18 industrial categories, constructed through multi-model consensus and three-tier quality assurance. Evaluating nine MLLMs under both single-image and product-level multi-image settings reveals a stark completeness gap: models achieve high precision (86--94%) but the best recovers only 49.9% of product-level attributes; moving from single-image to multi-image extraction costs 15--34 percentage points of recall. Multi-image completeness, not single-image accuracy, is the core bottleneck. Dataset and code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: A new large-scale benchmark, IndustryBench-MIPU, evaluates multi-image industrial product understanding by extracting structured attribute-value pairs from product images. It reveals a high precision but strong completeness (recall) gap due to multi-image integration; dataset with 4,559 products, 27,652 images, and 103,703 annotations across 18 categories; nine MLLMs evaluated, multi-image extraction incurs large recall losses. Dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: Dense, heterogeneous industrial specifications are scattered across spec tables, nameplates, and drawings. There is a gap in understanding how Multimodal LLMs can reliably recover complete property-value pairs when evidence is distributed across multiple images, requiring text recognition, visual reasoning, domain knowledge, and cross-image integration.
AI.method: Construct IndustryBench-MIPU, a large-scale benchmark for multi-image attribute extraction. It contains 4,559 products, 27,652 images, and 103,703 annotations across 18 categories, built via multi-model consensus and three-tier QA. Evaluate nine MLLMs under single-image and product-level multi-image settings, reporting precision and recall for property-value extraction and analyzing the impact of multi-image aggregation.
AI.result: Model precision is high (86–94%), but the best product-level attribute recall is only 49.9%. Transitioning from single-image to multi-image extraction costs 15–34 percentage points in recall. The core bottleneck is multi-image completeness rather than single-image accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Advancing industrial spec extraction requires improving multi-image evidence integration and domain-knowledge decoding. The benchmark provides a resource to evaluate and push progress in cross-image reasoning for dense, structured attribute extraction. The dataset and code are publicly available.

title: What Robots Do Matters More Than What They Look Like: Task Context Shapes Trust in Educational HRI
comment: Accepted in the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026), Kitakyushu, Fukuoka, Japan
summary: Socially assistive robots (SARs) are increasingly deployed in educational and information-sharing contexts, supported by advances in large language models that enable fluent real-time interaction. Despite the growing diversity of robot embodiments, it remains unclear whether a single robot appearance is appropriate across different interaction tasks or whether trust depends primarily on contextual factors. In this study, we examine how robot appearance and task type jointly influence trust in robots. Using a within-subjects video-based experiment (N = 81), participants evaluated three robots with distinct appearances while performing three educationally relevant tasks: teaching, procedural instruction, and personal-information discussion. Results from repeated-measures analyses show a strong main effect of task on trust, with participants reporting the highest trust during instructional guidance, moderate trust during teaching activities, and significantly lower trust when robots requested personal information. In contrast, robot appearance showed no significant main effect, and the interaction between appearance and task was marginal. These findings suggest that trust in human-robot interaction is shaped more strongly by task context than by physical embodiment alone. By focusing on future educators as end users, this work contributes empirical evidence toward task-aware robot deployment in educational environments and highlights the importance of aligning robot roles and behaviors with interaction goals rather than relying solely on anthropomorphic design.

AI.tldr: Trust in SARs is more driven by task context than robot appearance; appearance effects are non-significant.
AI.motivation: Determine whether a single robot embodiment suffices across tasks and how trust is shaped by context versus form to guide task-aware education deployments.
AI.method: Within-subjects video-based experiment (N=81); three robots with distinct appearances; three educational tasks: teaching, procedural instruction, and personal-information discussion; trust assessed via self-report after each condition.
AI.result: Strong main effect of task on trust: highest during instructional guidance, moderate during teaching, and significantly lower when robots requested personal information; robot appearance had no significant main effect; appearance-by-task interaction was marginal.
AI.conclusion: Trust in HRI is more strongly influenced by task context than by physical embodiment; implies prioritizing task-aware robot roles and behaviors in educational settings rather than relying on anthropomorphic design.

title: DRIVE: Distributional and Retrieval-Augmented Bidding with Value Evaluation
summary: Auto-bidding is a core component of real-time advertising systems, where decisions must optimize long-term performance under budget and cost constraints, while online exploration is prohibitively risky. Offline reinforcement learning and, more recently, Transformer-based sequence modeling have shown promise for learning bidding policies from logged data, but their unimodal and purely parametric formulations often collapse multiple effective bidding strategies into suboptimal averaged actions and perform unreliably under sparse or long-tail traffic. To mitigate these limitations, we propose DRIVE (Distributional and Retrieval-Augmented Bidding with Value Evaluation), a unified Transformer-based framework that decouples candidate action generation from decision making for offline auto-bidding. DRIVE combines distributional action modeling, retrieval-augmented candidate generation from high-quality historical decisions, and value-based evaluation to select the most promising bid at inference time. Extensive experiments on AuctionNet and additional offline reinforcement learning benchmarks demonstrate that DRIVE consistently improves bidding performance and generalizes well across multiple Transformer-based methods.

AI.tldr: DRIVE is a Transformer-based offline auto-bidding framework that decouples candidate action generation, uses distributional action modeling, retrieval augmentation from high-quality historical decisions, and value-based evaluation to select bids, yielding improved performance and generalization across Transformer methods.
AI.motivation: Offline RL and Transformer-based bidding often suffer from unimodal/parametric limitations and fragility on sparse/long-tail traffic; there is a need for diverse candidate actions and robust evaluation in offline auto-bidding.
AI.method: A unified Transformer-based framework (DRIVE) with three components: (1) distributional action modeling to capture multiple effective bid actions; (2) retrieval-augmented candidate generation using high-quality historical decisions; (3) value-based evaluation to select the most promising bid at inference, enabling decoupled generation and decision making.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on AuctionNet and additional offline RL benchmarks shows that DRIVE consistently improves bidding performance and generalizes across multiple Transformer-based methods.
AI.conclusion: DRIVE effectively addresses unimodality and data sparsity in offline auto-bidding by combining distributional action modeling, retrieval augmentation, and value evaluation, producing better, more robust bidding policies and broad applicability across Transformer-based approaches.

title: MooMIns -- Monocular 3D Reconstruction and Object Pose Estimation from Multiple Instances
summary: Simultaneous 3D reconstruction and 6D object pose estimation from a single monocular image is an inherently ill-posed problem. In industrial settings, however, multiple instances of an object are often randomly arranged in bins, implicitly providing several views of the same object within a single image. We show that this implicit multi-view geometry can be exploited to simultaneously reconstruct the object in 3D and estimate the 6D pose of each visible object instance. We present MooMIns, a new Gaussian-splatting-based approach that inverts the original Gaussian splatting formulation: instead of rendering a single scene from multiple cameras, we render multiple object instances from a single camera. Our method is initialized with SAM3 instance segmentation masks and a modified Structure from Motion (SfM) pipeline. In contrast to learned monocular depth estimation, we perform true geometry-based reconstruction from image evidence, avoiding hallucinations caused by training data priors. We evaluate MooMIns on synthetic and real bin-picking scenarios, and demonstrate accurate reconstruction of previously unseen objects as well as reliable pose estimation of individual instance

AI.tldr: MooMIns uses a monocular image with multiple object instances in a bin to jointly reconstruct 3D shapes and estimate 6D poses for each instance, via a Gaussian-splatting-based inversion initialized from SAM3 masks and a modified SfM pipeline.
AI.motivation: Monocular 3D reconstruction is ill-posed; bin-picking provides implicit multi-view cues within one image. A geometry-based, learned-free approach can reduce hallucinations from priors and enable unseen object reconstruction.
AI.method: Develop MooMIns: invert Gaussian splatting to render multiple object instances from a single camera; initialize with SAM3 instance segmentation masks; use a modified Structure-from-Motion pipeline to recover 3D geometry and pose; operate in a true, geometry-based framework rather than depth estimation from learning.
AI.result: Evaluations on synthetic and real bin-picking scenarios show accurate reconstruction of unseen objects and reliable per-instance 6D pose estimation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of geometry-based, single-image joint reconstruction and pose estimation in industrial settings by exploiting intra-image multi-view cues and Gaussian-splatting inversion.

title: Impedance MPC with Disturbance Estimation for Dexterous Hand Control
summary: Dexterous hands must simultaneously track precise finger trajectories and maintain safe, compliant contact -- objectives in tension for any fixed-gain controller. We present an actuator-agnostic Impedance Model Predictive Control (Impedance MPC) framework for dexterous fingers, instantiating the constant-$A_d$ offset-free architecture established for physical human-robot interaction (pHRI); its stability, recursive-feasibility, and input-to-state-stability guarantees are inherited by preserving the architectural assumptions. An algebraic feedforward reduces the tendon transmission -- hydraulic, cable, pneumatic, twisted-string, or series-elastic -- to a constant-coefficient double integrator, so the QP cost inverse is precomputed offline and a 10-step receding-horizon quadratic program runs at 500\,Hz while enforcing hard constraints on contact force (ISO/TS 15066), actuation limits, and jerk. An encoder-only augmented-Kalman disturbance state drives steady-state error to zero under any constant contact load. On a hydraulically actuated finger -- the worked example platform, adding pressure and cavitation constraints -- the 500\,Hz Kalman MPC attains 0.5\,mrad RMS, 0.1\,mrad steady-state, and 6.6\,mrad peak deflection under 1.5\,Nm contact: 183$\times$, 1500$\times$, and 23$\times$ better than classical impedance. The realized first-move stiffness (18$\to$323\,Nm/rad with update rate) is independently verified. The architecture scales to a 16-DOF LEAP Hand MuJoCo simulation, recovering from 2.5\,N grasp-load disturbances within 0.7\,s.

AI.tldr: Actuator-agnostic Impedance MPC for dexterous fingers combines constant-A_d offset-free architecture with an offline-precomputed QP cost and 10-step receding-horizon MPC at 500 Hz to achieve precise tracking under hard contact constraints, improving over classical impedance and scalable to 16-DOF hands.
AI.motivation: Dexterous manipulation requires simultaneous precise finger trajectory tracking and safe, compliant contact. Fixed-gain controllers struggle to balance performance and safety; an actuator-agnostic, offset-free MPC aims to unify impedance and contact safety across actuators.
AI.method: Algebraic feedforward reduces tendon transmission to a constant-coefficient double integrator; offline precomputation of QP cost; 10-step receding-horizon QP at 500 Hz with hard constraints on contact force (ISO/TS 15066), actuation limits, and jerk; encoder-only augmented Kalman disturbance state drives zero steady-state error under constant contact load; applies 500 Hz Kalman MPC to hydraulically actuated finger with pressure and cavitation constraints; first-move stiffness verified; architecture demonstrated on 16-DOF LEAP Hand MuJoCo simulation recovering from 2.5 N disturbances within 0.7 s.
AI.result: Under 1.5 Nm contact: 0.5 mrad RMS, 0.1 mrad steady-state, 6.6 mrad peak deflection; improvements over classical impedance by factors 183×, 1500×, and 23× for RMS, steady-state, and peak deflection respectively; realized stiffness increases from 18 to 323 Nm/rad with update; scalable to 16-DOF hand in MuJoCo.
AI.conclusion: The Impedance MPC architecture is actuator-agnostic and scalable, delivering high-precision, touch-safe manipulation under contact constraints, with significant performance gains over classical impedance and robust disturbance rejection; suitable for diverse actuator technologies and large-scale dexterous hands.

title: Structured Noise Adaptation for Sequential Bayesian Filtering with Embedded Latent Transfer Operators
summary: Kalman filters based on the Embedded Latent Transfer Operators (ELTO) emerge as novel statistical tools for sequential state estimation. However, a critical limitation stems from their use of simplified noise models, which fail to dynamically adapt to non-stationary processes. To address this limitation, we introduce an ELTO-based Bayesian filtering approach with a new structured parameterization for the filter's noise model. This parameterization enables structured noise adaptation, which couples the data-driven learning of an optimal time-invariant noise model with dynamic parameter adaptation that responds to changes in dynamics within non-stationary processes. Empirical results show that our structured noise adaptation improves the filter's dynamic state estimation performance in noisy, time-varying environments.

AI.tldr: ELTO-based Bayesian filtering with a structured noise parameterization that learns a base time-invariant noise model and dynamically adapts to non-stationary changes, improving state estimation in noisy, time-varying settings.
AI.motivation: Standard ELTO/Kalman filters rely on simplified, static noise models that fail to track non-stationary dynamics; this limits robustness and accuracy in real-world, time-varying environments.
AI.method: Introduce a structured parameterization for the filter's noise model within the ELTO-based Bayesian framework, coupling data-driven learning of a nominal (time-invariant) noise model with a dynamic adaptation mechanism that responds to changes in system dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that structured noise adaptation yields improved dynamic state estimation performance in noisy, time-varying environments.
AI.conclusion: Structured noise adaptation enhances ELTO-based Bayesian filtering under non-stationary conditions, offering a principled approach to jointly learn and adapt noise characteristics for better sequential state estimation.

title: Value-order Decomposition for Generalist Anomaly Detection
summary: Industrial anomaly detection suffers from limited data, making cross-domain generalization particularly challenging. Generalist Anomaly Detection (GAD) aims to train a unified model on a source domain that can effectively detect anomalies in unseen target domains. In the initial semantic feature space, strong entanglement between anomalies and object categories or defect types hinders effective generalization across domains. Recent works address this issue by projecting features into a residual space; however, such methods primarily increase cross-domain overlap for normal features, while anomalous features remain specific to object categories, defect types and data domains, leading to poor alignment and generalization. To address this limitation, we propose Value-order Decomposition (VOD), a simple yet effective technique that bridges \textbf{three types of generalization gaps} across object categories, defect types (including real and synthetic defects), and data domains. VOD disentangles and suppresses object-category-, defect-type-, and domain-specific information, promoting alignment within normal and abnormal samples while preserving their separability, thereby enabling robust generalization across the three gaps. Leveraging the strong alignment between real and synthetic defects within the same object, we perform anomaly detection using only normal and synthetic-abnormal reference, and effectively generalize to unseen real defect types. Experiments on diverse industrial and medical benchmarks demonstrate that our method, using a simple cut-and-paste anomaly simulation strategy, achieves strong generalization across the three gaps.

AI.tldr: Value-order Decomposition (VOD) for Generalist Anomaly Detection (GAD) that disentangles object-category, defect-type, and domain cues to bridge three generalization gaps. It uses real/synthetic defect alignment and a simple cut-and-paste augmentation, achieving robust cross-domain performance using only normal and synthetic-abnormal references.
AI.motivation: Industrial anomaly detection has limited data and poor cross-domain generalization. Anomalies are entangled with object categories and defect types, and residual-space methods mainly align normals, leaving anomalies domain-/category-specific. A method that decouples these factors can improve generalization across object categories, defect types (real/synthetic), and data domains.
AI.method: Value-order Decomposition (VOD) disentangles and suppresses object-category-, defect-type-, and domain-specific information. It aligns normal and abnormal samples in a shared space while preserving their separability. The approach leverages strong real/synthetic defect alignment within the same object and uses only normal and synthetic-abnormal references for anomaly detection, with a simple cut-and-paste anomaly simulation to synthesize training data.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse industrial and medical benchmarks demonstrate strong generalization across the three gaps (object category, defect type, and domain) using VOD and the proposed augmentation strategy.
AI.conclusion: VOD provides robust cross-domain generalization for GAD by suppressing multiple sources of variation and aligning normal and abnormal samples, enabling effective detection of unseen real defect types with a simple data augmentation strategy.

title: Safe Reinforcement Learning of Autonomous Highway Driving: A Unified Framework for Safety and Efficiency
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) offers a compelling route to decision-making for advanced autonomous vehicles (AVs), yet its trial-and-error nature makes it difficult to guarantee safety during training and to achieve both safety and efficiency at deployment. We propose a unified safe reinforcement learning (SRL) framework that integrates safe distance (SD), reward machines (RM), and mixture-of-experts (MoE), termed MoE-RM-SRL. For deployment, SD and RM jointly shape a rule-aware reward that encodes highway traffic regulations and stage-wise objectives, enabling safe and reliable behavior without sacrificing efficiency. For training, we introduce a sparsely gated MoE layer comprising up to 11 deep Q-networks (DQNs); an SD-based gating rule activates a minimal set of experts for lane-keeping and lane-changing, mitigating the instability, discontinuities, and impulsive transients commonly induced by switching between heterogeneous controllers (e.g., MPC/rule-based modules and learned policies). We implement the proposed architecture in CARLA and integrate it with a 6-DoF driver-in-the-loop virtual-reality (DiL-VR) platform. Experiments in stochastic two-lane traffic show that MoE-RM-SRL substantially improves safety and efficiency over state-of-the-art baselines, and the framework naturally extends to multi-lane driving as well as on-ramp merging and exiting scenarios.

AI.tldr: A unified safe reinforcement learning framework (MoE-RM-SRL) for autonomous driving that combines safe distance controls, reward machines, and sparsely gated mixture-of-experts to improve safety and efficiency, validated in CARLA with a driver-in-the-loop VR setup.
AI.motivation: DRL shows promise for AV decision-making but suffers safety and stability issues during training and deployment. There is a need to enforce safety and regulatory compliance without sacrificing learning efficiency, particularly for complex highway scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce MoE-RM-SRL: a) safe distance (SD) and reward machines (RM) to shape a rule-aware reward aligned with traffic regulations and stage-wise objectives; b) a sparsely gated MoE layer with up to 11 DQNs; SD-based gating selects a minimal subset of experts for lane-keeping and lane-changing to reduce instability and transients; c) implementation in CARLA and integration with a 6-DoF driver-in-the-loop VR platform; d) evaluation in stochastic two-lane traffic and extension claims to multi-lane, on-ramp merging/exiting.
AI.result: MoE-RM-SRL substantially improves safety and efficiency over state-of-the-art baselines in stochastic two-lane traffic; the SD-guided gating mitigates instability and abrupt transients associated with switching between heterogeneous controllers; the framework demonstrates scalability to more complex driving tasks and scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The MoE-RM-SRL framework provides a practical, scalable approach to safe reinforcement learning for autonomous driving by unifying safe distance control, rule-informed rewards, and sparse mixture-of-experts to balance safety and efficiency during learning and deployment.

title: LapidaryEngine: Fully Conversational Crystal Generation
comment: 11 main pages, 5 main figures, and 1 table
summary: The emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) has inspired the vision of generating bespoke crystal materials directly from natural-language instructions, enabling users to design materials through intuitive, conversational interaction. Existing text-to-crystal generative models represent important early steps toward this goal, but they suffer from two critical limitations: (i) restricted input formats that require highly structured descriptions (e.g., chemical formulas), and (ii) one-directional generation, where models can map text to crystal but cannot perform the inverse. These limitations prevent fully conversational workflows and hinder alignment with users' inherently ambiguous and evolving desiderata. We address these challenges with LapidaryEngine, the first model to support fully conversational crystal generation. LapidaryEngine accepts free-form natural-language requests and performs iterative refinement and editing in a dialogue-like manner. The key innovation is a pivot representation, a third, intermediate form that enables bidirectional translation between text and crystal structures despite the absence of direct paired datasets. Leveraging this pivot allows robust interpretation of user feedback and precise structural control. We demonstrate LapidaryEngine across diverse tasks, including insulator discovery, stability optimization, compositional modification, and structural editing, showcasing its ability to align generated materials with user intent in an interactive manner.

AI.tldr: Conv. crystal design via pivot-based bidirectional translation enabling free-form NL input and iterative editing without paired data.
AI.motivation: Overcome restricted input formats and one-way generation in text-to-crystal models; enable fully conversational design aligned with ambiguous user intents.
AI.method: Introduce a pivot representation as an intermediate form to bridge text and crystal structures; enable bidirectional translation; use dialogue-style iterative refinement and feedback interpretation; do not require direct paired datasets.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates tasks such as insulator discovery, stability optimization, compositional modification, and structural editing, illustrating interactive alignment with user intent.
AI.conclusion: LapidaryEngine is the first model to support fully conversational crystal generation; the pivot enables robust bidirectional control and flexible design workflows.

title: Scratched Lenses, Shifted Depth: Passive Camera-Side Optical Attacks
summary:
Physical adversarial attacks on vision systems are typically studied through scene manipulation, such as adversarial patches or projections, where the adversary controls what the camera observes. Camera-side attacks using stickers or auxiliary optics have also been explored, but they treat attacks as image-space perturbations from designed patterns. This misses how physical imperfections interact with scene-dependent lighting and optics. We identify a threat: passive lens-side damage that is persistent yet trigger-conditioned, producing optical artifacts that bias geometric inference under particular visual conditions.
  We instantiate this threat through Scratch-induced Lens Adversarial Streak Hijacking SLASH, a physical-world attack caused by small scratches on a camera lens or protective cover. Scratches interact with bright light sources and specular reflections to create structured streak artifacts that distort depth cues. Since the perturbation is fixed in the optical path but triggered by the scene, it is both persistent and selective. We formulate the attack in optical space, model the scratch pattern as a trigger-conditioned optical channel, and optimize one fixed configuration across diverse viewing conditions. We evaluate SLASH on monocular depth estimation and monocular 3D object detection in digital and real-world settings. Under the fixed-scratch constraint, directional depth shifts reach up to 32% relative error for monocular depth estimation, with consistent effects on monocular 3D object detection. Physical experiments confirm transfer to real camera recordings, inducing depth shifts above the model's natural prediction baseline. These findings reveal an attack surface where benign-looking hardware imperfections act as latent, scene-triggered adversarial mechanisms, challenging assumptions about physical robustness and motivating defenses for secure vision systems.

AI.tldr: A persistent, scene-triggered lens-damage attack (SLASH) leveraging scratches on a camera lens to create optically induced streaks under bright light, biasing depth estimates and monocular 3D detection; demonstrates transfer to real cameras and reveals a hardware-in-the-loop vulnerability in vision systems.
AI.motivation: Current physical-adversarial-work focuses on scene manipulation or image-space perturbations, overlooking how fixed optical imperfections interact with scene-dependent lighting to bias geometric inference. The work identifies passive lens-side damage as a latent, trigger-conditioned threat that is persistent and selective.
AI.method: Model the scratch as a trigger-conditioned optical channel and optimize a single fixed scratch configuration that persists across varying viewing conditions. Implement Scratch-induced Lens Adversarial Streak Hijacking (SLASH), evaluate on monocular depth estimation and monocular 3D object detection in both digital and real-world settings, and verify transfer to real camera recordings.
AI.result: With a fixed scratch, directional depth errors reach up to 32% relative error in monocular depth estimation; consistent adverse effects observed in monocular 3D object detection; physical experiments confirm transfer to real recordings, producing depth shifts beyond the model’s baseline predictions.
AI.conclusion: The work exposes a novel attack surface where benign hardware imperfections can function as latent, scene-triggered adversarial mechanisms, challenging assumptions about physical robustness and underscoring the need for defenses against hardware-in-the-loop attacks in vision systems.

title: Whole-Body Impedance Model Predictive Control for Safe Physical Human--Robot Interaction on Floating-Base Platforms
summary: Floating-base robots must balance under rigid contact constraints while interacting safely with humans. Existing whole-body control~(WBC) frameworks allocate the full joint space to locomotion or rely on fixed-gain impedance feedback that accumulates steady-state error under sustained physical human--robot interaction~(pHRI) forces. This paper extends the authors' fixed-base two-layer Impedance MPC to floating-base platforms through a three-level architecture: a centroidal MPC plans contact forces over a 500\,ms horizon; a priority-driven WBC layer resolves balance into joint torques through contact-consistent null-space projection; and the residual null space is governed by a receding-horizon quadratic program~(QP) that predicts and rejects pHRI disturbances using a Kalman-augmented state. A contact-consistent feedback linearization reduces the arm end-effector plant to a double integrator with a \emph{constant} state matrix within each contact mode, enabling offline precomputation of the QP cost and ${\geq}1$\,kHz operation. A covariance-inflation protocol preserves the disturbance estimate across contact-mode switches, guaranteeing zero steady-state error under bounded constant pHRI loads, and an Impedance Equivalence Theorem shows the infinite-horizon limit recovers a classical task-space impedance law whose effective mass, damping, and stiffness adapt to posture and contact configuration. Simulations on a 17-DOF biped and the Unitree G1 humanoid validate the design.

AI.tldr: Three-level floating-base WBC: centroidal MPC for contact forces, priority-driven WBC with contact-consistent null-space, and a Kalman-augmented residual QP; achieves zero steady-state error under bounded pHRI, high update rate, and an impedance-equivalent behavior.
AI.motivation: Enable robust balance and safe interaction for floating-base robots (e.g., humanoids) under rigid contact constraints, addressing steady-state error in fixed-gain impedance controllers and extending fixed-base impedance MPC to floating bases.
AI.method: Introduce a three-level architecture: (1) centroidal MPC plans contact forces over a 500 ms horizon; (2) priority-driven WBC uses contact-consistent null-space projection to resolve balance into joint torques; (3) residual null-space governed by a receding-horizon QP predicting/rejecting pHRI disturbances via Kansman-augmented state. A contact-consistent feedback linearization turns the arm end-effector dynamics into a double integrator with constant state matrix within each contact mode, enabling offline QP cost precomputation and ≥1 kHz operation. A covariance-inflation protocol preserves disturbance estimates across contact switches; an Impedance Equivalence Theorem shows the infinite-horizon limit matches a classical task-space impedance with adaptable mass/damping/stiffness depending on posture/contact. Simulations on a 17-DOF biped and Unitree G1 validate the design.
AI.result: The architecture yields ≥1 kHz control, zero steady-state error under bounded constant pHRI loads due to disturbance estimation preservation, and validated performance in simulation on two platforms (17-DOF biped and Unitree G1).
AI.conclusion: In the limit, the scheme recovers an impedance law whose effective parameters adapt to configuration, and the three-tier approach enables stable, safe floating-base manipulation under human interaction, with practical offline computation and high-frequency operation.

title: Curvature-Informed Potential Energy Surface for Protein-Ligand Binding Affinity Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand binding affinity is essential for structure-based drug discovery. Recent geometric deep learning methods have achieved promising performance by representing protein-ligand complexes as three-dimensional graphs. However, most existing approaches mainly rely on static interaction geometry from a single bound conformation, while neglecting molecular flexibility and binding-induced conformational changes. To address this limitation, we propose a curvature-informed potential energy surface (CPES) graph neural network for protein-ligand binding affinity prediction, which incorporates physics-informed curvature representations to model conformational flexibility. CPES first derives curvature spectral descriptors from the Hessian of the potential energy surface evaluated at equilibrium configurations, whose eigenvalues define the local principal curvatures of the potential energy surface. It then uses spectral cross-attention to compare the unbound ligand and protein with the bound complex, thereby capturing binding-induced changes in conformational dynamics. In parallel, hierarchical protein-ligand interaction representations are learned from static structural features through geometry-aware message passing, soft clustering, and bidirectional cross-attention. Finally, CPES fuses the curvature-informed dynamic representations with static interaction representations for affinity regression. Extensive evaluations on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that CPES achieves improved predictive performance and offers physical interpretability.

AI.tldr: Curvature-informed potential energy surface (CPES) graph neural network for protein-ligand binding affinity integrates curvature descriptors from Hessian-based PES, spectral cross-attention to capture binding-induced conformational changes, and geometry-aware static features via hierarchical representations, fused for affinity regression with improved accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of relying on static interaction geometry by incorporating molecular flexibility and binding-induced conformational changes through physics-informed dynamic representations.
AI.method: Compute curvature spectral descriptors from the Hessian of the potential energy surface at equilibrium to obtain local principal curvatures; use spectral cross-attention to compare unbound ligand/protein with the bound complex. Learn hierarchical protein-ligand interactions from static structural features via geometry-aware message passing, soft clustering, and bidirectional cross-attention. Fuse curvature-informed dynamic representations with static interaction representations for affinity regression.
AI.result: CPES achieves improved predictive performance on multiple benchmark datasets and provides physical interpretability of the learned representations.
AI.conclusion: Integrating curvature-informed dynamic representations with static interaction features enhances protein-ligand binding affinity prediction and offers interpretable, physics-grounded insights.

title: A Lightweight Fiducial-Based Pipeline for 3D Hyperspectral Mapping of ex-vivo Lumpectomy Specimens
summary: Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI) is a promising modality for intraoperative assessment of resection margins in Breast-Conserving Surgery (BCS), but its clinical translation requires aligning the inherently 2D spectral information onto the 3D shape of the excised tissue so that suspicious regions can be precisely localized for targeted follow-up. We present a fully automated, calibration-free pipeline that produces a 3D hyperspectral point cloud of an ex-vivo lumpectomy specimen from a set of consumer-camera RGB images and a single top-down HSI acquisition. The 3D geometry is reconstructed with a deep-learning Structure-from-Motion backbone, stabilized in a metric reference frame by a custom bundle adjustment that enforces consistency on the corners of four ArUco markers placed around the specimen. The HSI cube is then registered to the reconstruction without recovering the HSI camera pose: the markers, visible in both modalities, define 16 corner correspondences that drive a planar homography, and 3D coordinates are recovered by lookup on an orthographically rendered depth map. Evaluated on two ex-vivo lumpectomy specimens, the pipeline achieves a median 3D registration error below 1~mm and a 2D reprojection error below 0.02 mm, with a total per-specimen processing time under 4 minutes on accelerated hardware. These results support the feasibility of integrating HSI-guided spatial localization into intraoperative margin assessment workflows for breast-conserving surgery.

AI.tldr: A calibration-free, automated pipeline registers 3D geometry to a 2D hyperspectral image for breast-conserving surgery, achieving sub-millimeter 3D registration and sub-0.02 mm reprojection errors on two ex-vivo specimens within ~4 minutes.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between 2D hyperspectral spectral data and the 3D excised tissue surface to enable precise localization of suspicious regions during intraoperative margin assessment, without requiring explicit HSI camera pose estimation.
AI.method: A deep-learning Structure-from-Motion pipeline reconstructs 3D geometry, stabilized to a metric frame via bundle adjustment using four ArUco markers. The HSI cube is registered to the 3D model by planar homography defined by 16 marker corners visible in both modalities, with 3D coordinates recovered through lookup on an orthographic depth map. No HSI camera pose recovery is needed.
AI.result: On two ex-vivo lumpectomy specimens, median 3D registration error < 1 mm; 2D reprojection error < 0.02 mm; per-specimen processing time < 4 minutes on accelerated hardware.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of integrating HSI-guided spatial localization into intraoperative margin assessment workflows for breast-conserving surgery.

title: EgoGuide: Egocentric Guidance for Efficient Robot-Free Demonstration Collection and Learning
summary: Robot learning from real-world demonstrations is currently constrained by data scaling. Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) provides an efficient robot-free data collection interface, yet current UMI-style pipelines often collect redundant demonstrations and lack global scene context. To improve data efficiency, we present EgoGuide, a collection interface that records synchronized wrist and head/egocentric observations and couples them with online visual-geometric data quality guidance. We also introduce a Gated Egocentric Residual Policy for robust learning from a viewpoint-varying egocentric camera, allowing head/egocentric context to correct ambiguous local observations while preserving stable wrist-view control. Real-world experiments show that EgoGuide reduces the required number of data episodes and improves data efficiency. The residual policy further improves robustness under visual occlusion. Project Page: https://silicx.github.io/EgoGuide

AI.tldr: EgoGuide presents an egocentric data-collection interface coupled with online data-quality guidance and a gated egocentric residual policy, achieving higher data efficiency and robustness in real-world robot manipulation by leveraging head/egocentric cues alongside wrist control.
AI.motivation: Address data inefficiency and redundant demonstrations in UMI-style pipelines, and overcome lack of global scene context by incorporating synchronized egocentric observations with quality guidance to improve sample efficiency and robustness.
