AI.method: EgoGuide records synchronized wrist (wrist-view) and head/egocentric observations, augmented by online visual-geometric data quality guidance. It introduces a Gated Egocentric Residual Policy that fuses egocentric context with the wrist-centric control, using a gating mechanism to retain stable wrist-view control while leveraging head/egocentric cues to disambiguate observations under viewpoint variation and occlusion.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show a reduction in the number of data episodes needed and improved data efficiency. The residual policy enhances robustness under visual occlusion.
AI.conclusion: Integrating synchronized egocentric observations with gated residual policies provides a more data-efficient and robust pathway for learning from real-world demonstrations, complementing UMI-based data collection by injecting global context and online quality guidance.

title: Learning the Context of Errors: Black-Box Online Adaptation of Time Series Foundation Models
summary: The rapid evolution of Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) has advanced zero-shot forecasting across diverse domains. Inspired by the current form of Large Language Models, future TSFMs may be offered as commercialized, closed-source API services. However, many existing online adaptation methods still rely on white-box access for parameter fine-tuning or gradient backpropagation. This paradigm mismatch raises a question: In black-box online adaptation for TSFMs, what should we learn? We answer this with an insight: the predictive errors of the base model are conditioned on both the input and output of the base model (i.e., the context of errors). To validate this insight, we propose ORCA (Online Residual Contextual Adaptation). We conduct extensive experiments across 5 state-of-the-art TSFMs and 8 datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Furthermore, through ablation studies, we quantitatively analyze the impact of different adapter learning hypotheses on the final adaptation performance in black-box online adaptation. Code available at https://github.com/Fifthky/ORCA.

AI.tldr: ORCA presents a black-box online adaptation method for Time Series Foundation Models by leveraging the residual context of prediction errors (conditioned on input and output) to adapt in real time. It demonstrates improved online adaptation across multiple TSFMs and datasets, with ablations validating design choices; code is provided.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between the desire for online adaptation of TSFMs and the reality that many systems will be closed-source API services (no gradient access). The key question is what aspects to learn/adapt when only black-box access is available. The insight is that base-model errors depend on both input and previous predictions, i.e., the error context.
AI.method: Introduce ORCA (Online Residual Contextual Adaptation). In a black-box setting, it learns adapters that operate on the residual/error context of the base model, without requiring gradient backpropagation through the base model. The method examines how prediction errors vary with input/output context and leverages this to update predictions online. The approach is evaluated through extensive experiments across 5 state-of-the-art TSFMs and 8 datasets, with ablation studies to analyze the impact of different adapter learning hypotheses.
AI.result: ORCA achieves improved online adaptation performance across the tested TSFMs and datasets, validating the proposed insight about error context. Ablation results quantify how various adapter-learning hypotheses affect final performance, demonstrating robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a principled framework for black-box online adaptation of TSFMs by exploiting the residual contextual information in errors. It supports practical deployment of TSFMs via API services and supplies open-source code for replication and further development.

title: Rethinking Global Average Pooling: Your Classifier Is Secretly a Multi-Instance Learner
summary: Modern image classifiers widely adopt global average pooling (GAP) followed by a linear classification head. This linearity ensures that the image-level logits equal the average of logits obtained by applying the classification head pointwise to the feature grid prior to GAP. Consequently, standard classifiers may inherently retain spatial class evidence that remains recoverable even when the image-level prediction is incorrect. This structure naturally suggests a multiple-instance learning (MIL) interpretation, where an image is viewed as a bag of spatial instances. Within this formulation, we demonstrate that standard classifiers trained with a single label per image can still learn the intended classification task in multi-object scenes. We further exploit this property to decompose image-level logits into a prediction grid, providing a post-hoc diagnostic to extract spatial class evidence that GAP otherwise obscures. Our systematic evaluation reveals that off-the-shelf models consistently recover the ground-truth class within foreground regions. The MIL interpretation further suggests that common classifier failures reflect known limitations of mean aggregation.

AI.tldr: Global average pooling with a linear head makes image-level logits equal to the average of per-location logits, enabling a MIL (multiple-instance learning) interpretation. Single-label training can still learn multi-object scenes; a post-hoc prediction-grid decomposition reveals spatial class evidence obscured by GAP; standard models often locate the true class within foreground regions. Mean aggregation explains common classifier failures.
AI.motivation: To elucidate why GAP-plus-linear-classification preserves spatial evidence and to provide a practical diagnostic for localizing class evidence, addressing interpretability and failure modes in multi-object scenes.
AI.method: Treat each image as a bag of spatial instances (MIL framing) and show that standard single-label training can learn the target in multi-object settings. Derive a post-hoc decomposition that expands image-level logits into a spatial prediction grid. Perform systematic evaluation on off-the-shelf models and datasets to extract spatial evidence using the grid-based diagnostic.
AI.result: Off-the-shelf models consistently recover the ground-truth class within foreground regions. The MIL interpretation explains classifier failures as artifacts of mean (GAP) aggregation rather than lack of learned signal.
AI.conclusion: The MIL perspective reconciles single-label training with multi-object recognition under GAP and provides a practical diagnostic tool to visualize spatial evidence, highlighting mean aggregation as a key source of failure and motivating alternative pooling strategies or interpretability methods.

title: Implicit Variational Rejection Sampling
summary: Variational Inference (VI) is a fundamental inference technique in Bayesian machine learning for approximating complex posterior distributions. Traditional VI often relies on the mean-field factorization, which can inadequately capture true posterior complexity. Recent advancements have leveraged neural networks to model implicit distributions, offering increased flexibility. However, the practical constraints of neural network architectures still produces inaccuracies. In this paper, we propose a method called Implicit Variational Rejection Sampling (IVRS), which integrates implicit distributions with rejection sampling to improve the posterior approximation. Our method uses neural networks to construct implicit proposal distributions, and rejection sampling with a discriminator network that estimates the density ratio between the implicit proposal and the true posterior for refining the approximation. Towards this end, we introduce the Implicit Resampling Evidence Lower Bound (IR-ELBO) as a metric to characterize the resampled distribution's quality and derive a tighter variational lower bound. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms traditional variational inference techniques.

AI.tldr: Implicit Variational Rejection Sampling (IVRS) merges neural-implicit proposals with rejection sampling guided by a density-ratio discriminator, yielding IR-ELBO and improved posterior approximations over standard VI.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of mean-field VI and finite approximation capacity of classical VI by leveraging flexible implicit distributions while correcting them via rejection sampling to better align with the true posterior.
AI.method: 1) Use neural nets to parameterize implicit proposal distributions; 2) Train a discriminator to estimate the density ratio between the proposal and the true posterior; 3) Apply rejection sampling to refine samples; 4) Define IR-ELBO as a resampled-distribution objective and derive a tighter variational lower bound.
AI.result: Empirical results show IVRS outperforms traditional VI methods on benchmark tasks; the IR-ELBO captures resampling quality and aligns with improved posterior approximation.
AI.conclusion: IVRS offers a principled hybrid between implicit variational families and rejection sampling, backed by a new bound (IR-ELBO) and demonstrated empirical gains; suggests potential for more accurate Bayesian inference with flexible proposals.

title: Visual Quality Score Assessment of Large White Goods in Remanufacture with Multi-View Deformable-DETR
summary: Remanufacturing large white goods is essential for a circular economy, yet visual quality assessment remains a manual bottleneck for training and pricing. Conventional detection methods require extensive annotation and struggle with small defects in high-resolution multi-view data. We present a multi-view framework based on Deformable-DETR for automated quality scoring that aggregates information across redundant views to extract fine-grained features. To enhance robustness with limited labels, we employ self-supervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning on expert-annotated scores. Additionally, a linear projection over frozen feature maps identifies regions of interest to explain model decisions. Evaluated on an industrial multi-view dataset, our approach delivers precise quality assessments while reducing reliance on manual annotation and per-part customization, enabling scalable and transparent inspection for remanufacturing lines.

AI.tldr: Multi-view Deformable-DETR with self-supervised pretraining for automated quality scoring of remanufactured large white goods, with ROI-based explanations.
AI.motivation: Remanufacturing large white goods relies on manual visual inspection, which is time-consuming and annotation-intensive. High-resolution, multi-view data increases detection challenges for small defects, necessitating scalable, label-efficient quality scoring with transparent decision rationale.
AI.method: A multi-view framework built on Deformable-DETR that fuses information across redundant views to extract fine-grained defect features. Self-supervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning on expert-annotated scores reduces labeling needs. A linear projection over frozen feature maps identifies regions of interest to explain model decisions.
AI.result: Evaluated on an industrial multi-view dataset, the approach delivers precise quality assessments while reducing reliance on manual annotation and per-part customization, enabling scalable and transparent inspection for remanufacturing lines.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports scalable, transparent remanufacturing inspection and contributes to circular-economy goals by streamlining quality scoring with limited labels and interpretable decisions.

title: Where Black-box Drug-Target Interaction Prediction Models Look: Cross-Method Explainability
summary: Drug-target interaction (DTI) and affinity (DTA) predictors increasingly achieve strong benchmark scores, yet their internal use of sequence, fingerprint, and graph features often remains opaque. We present an interpretability audit of BridgeDPI architecture on three different datasets including Gao, Human, and C.elegans. This study combines gradient-based attributions -- integrated gradients, saliency, layer-wise relevance propagation, SmoothGrad, and SmoothGrad-IG -- with feature-wise occlusion ablation and strict intersection consensus across methods to reduce single-explainer bias. We summarize sensitivity and signed effects at raw inputs, at the bridge similarity scaffold, and through the graph convolution, including edge-level sensitivities and targeted edge removals. The results show that explainability is most informative when treated as model criticism: it reveals modality dominance, padding and special-token artifacts, dataset-dependent cooperative versus suppressive effects across layers, and chemistry-consistent fragment and composition motifs where methods agree. These analyses do not substitute for structural or experimental ground truth, yet they can provide testable hypotheses for downstream validation in computational drug discovery pipelines. More broadly, applying modern XAI to contemporary DTI/DTA models is still an early pass over the rich structure implicit in trained weights and data -- yet even this first layer of scrutiny already helps researchers relate predictions to drug- and target-side representations and to prioritize external validation.

AI.tldr: An interpretability audit of BridgeDPI for drug-target interaction and affinity models using multiple gradient-based attribution methods and feature ablation across three datasets, revealing model criticisms and design artifacts to guide validation and hypothesis generation.
AI.motivation: To understand how modern DTI/DTA models relate predictions to molecule and target representations, beyond benchmark performance, and to identify artifacts and modality dominance that affect interpretability.
AI.method: Combine gradient-based attributions (Integrated Gradients, Saliency, Layer-wise Relevance Propagation, SmoothGrad, SmoothGrad-IG) with feature-wise occlusion and strict intersection consensus across methods; analyze raw inputs, bridge similarity scaffold, and graph convolution, including edge-level sensitivities and targeted edge removals.
AI.result: Explainability is most informative as model criticism, revealing modality dominance, padding/special-token artifacts, dataset-dependent interactions across layers, and chemistry-consistent motifs where methods agree; does not replace structural/experimental ground truth but yields testable hypotheses for downstream validation; early stage but useful for relating predictions to drug/target representations and prioritizing external validation.
AI.conclusion: Applying modern XAI to DTI/DTA models provides a first layer of scrutiny that aids interpretation and hypothesis generation, while acknowledging the need for external validation and future refinement as data and models evolve.

title: NEST3D: A High-Resolution Multimodal Dataset of Sociable Weaver Tree Nests
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures. Dataset available at https://huggingface.co/NEST3D
summary: Sociable weaver nests function as complex ecological structures offering thermoregulatory microhabitats and sustaining diverse species; however, datasets used in prior studies lack fine-grained 3D structural detail. Producing usable and accurate 3D weaver nest data is challenging due to their irregular geometry and integration with complex host vegetation. We bridge this gap with an open-access, 1.4 TB multimodal drone dataset of 104 nest-bearing trees, comprising 27,945 RGB images, 111,780 multispectral images, approximately 781 million 3D points, and expert-annotated semantic segmentation labels. We benchmark semantic segmentation using KPConv, RandLA-Net, and Point Transformer V3, with PT-v3 achieving an mIoU of 86.35% on the test set. While the results demonstrate strong performance for transformer-based and point-wise methods, they also highlight architecture-dependent challenges, particularly for convolution-based approaches such as KPConv. By uniquely combining spectral, spatial, and structural information, the presented dataset advances 3D reconstruction, segmentation, and classification algorithms, enabling ecological applications from nest volume estimation to species conservation, and serves as a demanding benchmark that exposes architecture-dependent performance under extreme class imbalance.

AI.tldr: Open-access 1.4 TB multimodal drone dataset of 104 nest-bearing trees for sociable weaver nests; benchmarks show transformer-based methods outperform conv-based ones; PT-V3 achieves 86.35% mIoU; dataset enables 3D reconstruction and ecological applications; highlights architecture sensitivity under class imbalance.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of fine-grained 3D structural detail for sociable weaver nests due to irregular geometry and vegetation integration; create a rich, multimodal dataset to enable accurate segmentation, reconstruction, and ecological analyses.
AI.method: Assemble a large multimodal dataset (RGB, multispectral, 3D points) from drones over 104 nest-bearing trees; provide expert semantic segmentation labels; benchmark three architectures (KPConv, RandLA-Net, Point Transformer V3); evaluate on test set; analyze architecture-dependent challenges.
AI.result: PT-V3 achieves mIoU 86.35% on test; transformer-based and point-wise methods perform well; convolution-based KPConv shows architecture-dependent difficulties; dataset fusion of spectral/spatial/structural data improves potential accuracy; dataset is a demanding benchmark under extreme class imbalance.
AI.conclusion: Dataset advances 3D reconstruction, segmentation, and classification for ecological applications (e.g., nest volume estimation, species conservation); provides a valuable resource and benchmark to probe architecture choices under complex geometries and imbalance.

title: Beyond a Single Explanation of the Adam--SGD Gap
summary: Prior work has identified several factors that can contribute to the performance gap between Adam and SGD, spanning data aspects, architecture design, and optimization properties. Yet these explanations are often studied in isolation, leaving their relative importance unclear. In this work, we revisit these hypotheses through a controlled empirical study across vision, language, genomics, and graph tasks, spanning modern and classical architectures, and carefully designed training setups. Our results suggest that no single factor consistently explains the Adam--SGD gap. For instance, the Adam advantage can (1) persist under a uniform vocabulary distribution yet nearly disappear under a heavy-tailed one; (2) reverse in favor of SGD in softmax-attention models; and (3) become larger under soft architectural modifications, e.g., when ReLU is replaced by a GeLU nonlinearity. This suggests that the gap arises from nontrivial data and architecture interactions, rather than from a single common factor. Yet, we observe a pattern across our settings: a \emph{crossover batch size} at which the relative advantage shifts from SGD to Adam as the batch size scales. These empirical results are captured by our theoretical gap model, which predicts this batch-size-dependent crossover. Our perspective helps reconcile several existing hypotheses while offering practical insights across domains.

AI.tldr: No single factor explains the Adam–SGD performance gap; the gap depends on data distribution, architecture, and training setup, with a cross-over batch size where Adam's advantage shifts to SGD. A theoretical gap model captures this batch-size–dependent crossover and helps reconcile competing hypotheses across domains.
AI.motivation: Clarify why Adam often outperforms or underperforms SGD by testing multiple interacting factors (data, architecture, optimization) across diverse tasks and settings, rather than studying each factor in isolation.
AI.method: Controlled empirical study across vision, language, genomics, and graph tasks using both modern and classical architectures and carefully designed training setups; systematically vary data distributions, architectural choices, and optimization settings to observe their impact on the Adam–SGD gap; develop a theoretical gap model to predict crossover batch sizes.
AI.result: No single factor consistently explains the Adam–SGD gap. Adam's advantage can persist or vanish depending on data distribution (uniform vs heavy-tailed), reverse in softmax-attention models, or enlarge with certain nonlinearities (GeLU). A crossover batch size emerges where SGD and Adam performance invert as batch size scales; the results align with a theoretical model predicting this crossover.
AI.conclusion: The gap arises from complex interactions between data and architecture rather than a single factor; the proposed gap model reconciles existing hypotheses and yields practical guidance across domains.

title: A Qualitative Review of GenAI-Based Methods for Data Generation and Augmentation in Industrial Computer Vision Applications
summary: AI-driven computer vision applications require a profound database to ensure predictable behaviors and performance. Such predictable behaviors are especially important for industrial applications in gaining trust from users. However, such a database is not readily available in industrial applications, and its acquisition is not trivial either. Active learning methods can be applied to ramp up data within a project deployment to iteratively increase the database, and thus the application predictability. Unfortunately, we observe that this often leads to a loss of user trust in the application, which is difficult to regain once lost. This leads to a "chicken-and-egg" dilemma in which neither the database nor the application is developed. In this work, we review state-of-the-art methods and approaches to further boost the database the initial active data ramp-up phase. Here, we focus on recent advancements in GenAI-based data generation and augmentation methods and review their adaptability on an industrial computer vision classification use case. Although we observe a potential for automatic data ramp-up, we also see a domain miss match in between the source (training environment) and target (industrial use-case) - regarding context defined in natural language and object characteristics.

AI.tldr: GenAI-based data generation and augmentation can accelerate data ramp-up for industrial CV, but domain mismatch between training and target use-cases challenges trust and applicability.
AI.motivation: Industrial AI vision requires predictable performance and user trust; acquiring a representative data database is hard; active data ramp-up can exacerbate trust issues.
AI.method: A review of state-of-the-art data-augmentation and GenAI-based data generation methods; assessment of their adaptability to industrial CV classification; analysis of domain shift between source training environments and target industrial use-cases defined by natural language context and object characteristics.
AI.result: Identifies potential for automatic data ramp-up using GenAI methods, but domain mismatch remains a critical obstacle; practical applicability in industry is limited without domain-aligned generation.
AI.conclusion: GenAI-based data generation/augmentation shows promise for addressing data ramp-up, but requires targeted domain adaptation and context-object alignment; further research is needed to reduce domain shift and preserve user trust during ramp-up.

title: DIFF-ERO: A Conformance-Aware Loss for Deep Learning in Process Mining
comment: Accepted at the 24th International Conference on Business Process Management
summary: Deep learning has driven many recent advances in process analytics, especially for predictive and prescriptive monitoring. However, standard objectives such as cross-entropy optimize local next-step likelihoods and only implicitly capture control-flow structure. As a result, models can achieve high token-level accuracy while permitting imprecise global behaviour. We introduce DIFF-ERO, a conformance-aware loss function for deep learning models on process data. DIFF-ERO is a differentiable formulation of entropy-based stochastic conformance that incorporates control-flow information during training. Our approach constructs batch-level stochastic transition matrices with soft edge memberships, allowing structural precision and recall signals to directly inform backpropagation. The loss is model-agnostic and can be applied whenever the final representation parametrizes stochastic transitions. We instantiate DIFF-ERO in transformer encoder-decoder pipelines for next-activity prediction and use it jointly with cross-entropy to analyse its theoretical components with respect to convergence. Across benchmarks comparing other loss functions and targets, DIFF-ERO shows improved predictive performance where structure matters most while maintaining parity elsewhere. At the same time, the learned stochastic automaton converges towards the structural ground truth, indicating that the network internalizes process model structure.

AI.tldr: Introduces DIFF-ERO, a differentiable conformance-aware loss for process data that integrates control-flow structure into training via soft, stochastic transitions, improving structure-aware performance and recovering underlying process structure.
AI.motivation: Standard objectives (e.g., cross-entropy) optimize local next-step likelihoods and miss global process structure; there is a need for a training signal that enforces conformance to process models during learning.
AI.method: DIFF-ERO builds batch-level stochastic transition matrices with soft edge memberships and entropy-based conformance signals, enabling backpropagation of structural information. It is model-agnostic and demonstrated on transformer encoder-decoder for next-activity prediction, used jointly with cross-entropy, with analysis of convergence to the structural ground truth.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, DIFF-ERO yields improved predictive performance in regions where structure matters, while maintaining parity in other cases. The learned stochastic automaton converges toward the true process structure, suggesting the network internalizes process-model information.
AI.conclusion: DIFF-ERO provides a principled, differentiable mechanism to inject conformance to process structure into deep learning training, improving structure-aware predictions and interpretability without sacrificing general performance; compatible with various architectures and targets.

title: S$^2$COPE: Self-Supervised Concept Discovery via Preference Learning
summary: Current representation learning paradigms force a fundamental compromise: self-supervised methods scale to massive datasets but yield opaque features, whereas interpretable models remain bottlenecked by the need for dense human annotation. We introduce Self-Supervised Concept discOvery via Preference lEarning (\model), a label-free framework that resolves this dilemma. Instead of treating Vision-Large-Language Models (VLLMs) as static feature extractors, \model leverages them as active participants in a self-supervised preference optimization loop. By autonomously hypothesizing, validating, and reinforcing candidate visual attributes directly from raw imagery, our framework discovers novel, structured concepts without a single label. Extensive experiments across natural, medical, and physics domains demonstrate that \model successfully extracts domain-specific concepts where standard VLLMs often fail to generate. By amortizing concept discovery directly into the VLLM backbone through our self-supervised preference objective -- rather than relying on static generation and disjoint filtering -- we achieve up to a 24-point absolute improvement in downstream top-1 classification accuracy on unseen data. Our work suggest that interpretability can emerge through a model's autonomous interaction with incidental visual structures, without any human supervision.

AI.tldr: Label-free self-supervised concept discovery using VLLMs via a self-supervised preference loop yields interpretable concepts and accuracy gains without labels.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised learning scales to large data but produces opaque features; interpretable models require dense human annotation. This work aims to reconcile scalability and interpretability without supervision.
AI.method: Introduce Self-Supervised Concept discovery via Preference Learning (name: \model). Treat Vision-Large-Language Models as active participants in a self-supervised preference optimization loop. They autonomously hypothesize, validate, and reinforce candidate visual attributes directly from raw imagery, discovering structured concepts without labels and incorporating the preference objective into the VLLM backbone.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across natural, medical, and physics domains show the framework extracts domain-specific concepts that standard VLLMs fail to generate; yields up to 24-point absolute improvement in downstream top-1 accuracy on unseen data.
AI.conclusion: Interpretability can emerge from a model's autonomous interaction with incidental visual structures without human supervision, with concept discovery amortized into the backbone via the self-supervised preference objective.

title: Provably Safe, Yet Scalable Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safe reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn policies that optimize rewards while satisfying constraints. Predominant approaches rely on soft-constrained policy optimization, which has achieved empirical success but does not provide formal safety guarantees for the learned policy. In contrast, methods with strict guarantees typically rely on explicit certificate functions, whose construction requires the direct synthesis and verification of control-invariant sets, a process that scales poorly with state dimension and often yields overly conservative behavior. In this paper, we present the Provably Safe, yet Scalable RL (PS2-RL) framework, a novel two-phase architecture for learning provably safe policies in a scalable manner, designed to overcome the key bottlenecks of prior methods. Rather than explicitly computing invariant sets, PS2-RL leverages a learned backup policy to forward-integrate the system dynamics, generating an implicit control-invariant set online. In the first phase, the backup policy is trained with our proposed safe-arrival value function, which characterizes the optimal backup policy for invariant-set construction. In the second phase, an RL policy is trained end-to-end through a differentiable projection layer that strictly enforces the safety guarantees induced by the learned backup policy. By maximizing the volume of the implicit control-invariant set in the first phase, the resulting PS2 policy from the second phase is performant and scalable, while maintaining provable safety. Crucially, PS2-RL imposes no restrictions on the underlying RL algorithm and can be plugged into any existing training pipeline. We establish theoretical guarantees for the proposed framework and evaluate it on robotic control tasks with state dimensions up to 10, a regime in which prior provably safe RL methods struggle or become impractical.

AI.tldr: PS2-RL introduces a two-phase framework for provably safe reinforcement learning that avoids explicit invariant-set computation by learning a backup policy to generate an implicit control-invariant set online. Phase 1 trains the backup policy via a safe-arrival value function; Phase 2 trains the RL policy with a differentiable projection enforcing safety. The approach yields theoretical guarantees, scales to higher-dimensional systems (up to 10D), and is plug-and-play with existing RL pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current safe RL methods either rely on soft-constrained objectives with no formal safety guarantees or on explicit invariant-set constructions that are computationally expensive and conservative. There is a need for scalable, provably safe RL that does not require computing invariant sets.
AI.method: Two-phase architecture: (1) learn a backup policy using a safe-arrival value function to create an implicit invariant set via forward simulation of the dynamics; (2) train an RL policy end-to-end with a differentiable projection layer that enforces the safety guarantees provided by the learned backup policy.
AI.result: The framework provides theoretical safety guarantees and demonstrates applicability to robotic control tasks with state dimensions up to 10, where prior provably safe RL methods are impractical.
AI.conclusion: PS2-RL offers a scalable, provably safe RL approach that is agnostic to the underlying RL algorithm, avoiding explicit invariant-set computations while delivering formal safety and competitive performance.

title: Hierarchical ODE: Learning Continuous-Time Physical Prototypes for Early Link Failure Detection
summary: Time series prototype learning is fundamentally challenged by observational ambiguity. Discrete architectures fail to resolve this, as they lack the capacity to decouple stochastic noise from continuous dynamics. Furthermore, rigid closed-set assumptions fail to capture unseen diversity. To address these limitations, we propose a hierarchical ordinary differential equation clustering network, which utilizes neural ordinary differential equation to model latent state evolution as a continuous integral curve. This formulation enforces temporal continuity to effectively disentangle smooth feature trends from stochastic noise, while our adaptive hierarchical mechanism autonomously determines the appropriate number of prototypes without rigid prior constraints. Validated on the early link failure detection task with irregularly sampled time series, the proposed method effectively extracts underlying physical prototypes, thereby enabling robust failure detection. Our code is available at https://github.com/NJ-LNN/Hierarchical-ODE.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical ODE-based clustering network for time-series prototype learning disentangles stochastic noise from smooth dynamics using neural ODEs and an adaptive hierarchical prototype count, validated on irregularly sampled data for early link failure detection; code available.
AI.motivation: Time series data suffer from observational ambiguity and stochastic noise; discrete architectures struggle to separate noise from dynamics and to generalize to unseen patterns due to fixed prototype counts. A continuous-time, adaptive approach can better capture latent dynamics and flexible prototype structures.
AI.method: Use neural ordinary differential equations to model latent state evolution as a continuous integral curve, enforcing temporal continuity to separate smooth trends from noise. Implement an adaptive hierarchical mechanism to determine the number of prototypes without fixed priors.
AI.result: The method effectively extracts underlying physical prototypes from irregularly sampled data and enables robust early link failure detection.
AI.conclusion: A continuous-time, adaptive prototype framework addresses observational ambiguity and unseen diversity in time series, offering robust failure detection and a flexible prototype count; code is publicly available for replication.

title: StereoGeo: an end-to-end stereo camera calibration method
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, accepted at the 34th European Signal Processing Conference (EUSIPCO 2026)
summary: In this work, we propose StereoGeo, an end-to-end network-based approach for stereo camera calibration. Our method estimates the focal lengths and gravity directions of the left and right cameras, as well as the relative extrinsic transformation relating them. Existing methods often rely on calibration patterns in structured environments or address only a single camera configuration, being limited to either intrinsic or extrinsic estimation, and depending on a multi-view setups. StereoGeo extends the GeoCalib algorithm, integrating deep neural network feature extraction with a differentiable optimizer. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks demonstrate that StereoGeo achieves competitive performance for intrinsic calibration and provides accurate stereo extrinsic estimation, outperforming existing methods that are limited to monocular settings. The dataset used in this work is partially publicly available at https://github.com/meddourimane/StereoGeo-dataset.

AI.tldr: StereoGeo is an end-to-end stereo calibration framework that jointly estimates left/right focal lengths, gravity directions, and the relative extrinsic transform, extending GeoCalib with differentiable optimization; it delivers competitive intrinsic calibration and accurate stereo extrinsics without relying on calibration patterns, and is evaluated on real-world benchmarks with a partially public dataset.
AI.motivation: Calibrating stereo cameras in non-ideal environments is challenging when relying on calibration patterns or monocular-only methods. There is a need for end-to-end approaches that jointly estimate intrinsic and extrinsic parameters in a differentiable pipeline.
AI.method: An end-to-end network that fuses deep feature extraction with a differentiable optimizer to estimate intrinsic (focal lengths), gravity directions, and the relative extrinsic transformation between left and right cameras, extending the GeoCalib framework to stereo settings.
AI.result: StereoGeo achieves competitive intrinsic calibration performance and accurate stereo extrinsic estimation on real-world benchmarks, outperforming methods limited to monocular setups. The authors also provide a partially public dataset at the referenced GitHub location.
AI.conclusion: StereoGeo successfully extends GeoCalib to stereo calibration, yielding robust intrinsic and extrinsic estimates in end-to-end fashion and enabling more flexible calibration without calibration patterns; the dataset availability enhances reproducibility.

title: Instruct-Particulate: Scaling Feed-Forward 3D Object Articulation with Kinematic Control
summary: Reconstructing articulated 3D objects is important for animation, gaming, and robotic simulations. Recent neural networks can estimate the articulated structure of 3D objects, but their generalization remains limited by the scarcity of annotated data for this task. To address this gap, we introduce Instruct-Particulate, a model that takes a 3D mesh together with a target kinematic specification, including part descriptions, connectivity, joint types, and optional point prompts, and predicts the corresponding kinematic part segmentation and joint motion parameters. The kinematic specification disambiguates the task and allows the model to target annotations of different granularity, thereby making it possible to use more abundant heterogeneous training data. At test time, the kinematic specification can be obtained automatically from large-scale vision-language models, so the model can be applied to any input mesh. To train our model at scale, we construct a heterogeneous dataset of more than 150,000 articulated 3D objects, extending existing publicly available collections with data obtained by partially labelling other 3D models (monolithic or already decomposed into parts) with kinematic labels by means of vision-language models. Experiments show that our model generalizes better across categories and to AI-generated meshes, enabling articulated asset reconstruction from real-world images via image-to-3D models.

AI.tldr: Instruct-Particulate presents a specification-driven pipeline for reconstructing articulated 3D objects from meshes, improving generalization by using explicit kinematic specifications and large-scale heterogeneous data, and enabling automatic spec extraction at test time via vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Generalization in articulated 3D reconstruction is hampered by limited annotated data. A flexible kinematic specification can leverage heterogeneous data and guide learning to target various granularities of annotation.
AI.method: Input: a 3D mesh and a target kinematic specification (part descriptions, connectivity, joint types, and optional point prompts). Output: a kinematic part segmentation and joint motion parameters. The model uses the specification to disambiguate the task and support variable granularity. Training data is built from a heterogeneous 150k+ articulated 3D objects dataset, expanded by partially labeling existing models with kinematic labels via vision-language models. At test time, the specification is automatically obtained from vision-language models, enabling application to arbitrary meshes.
AI.result: The approach yields better generalization across object categories and to AI-generated meshes, enabling articulated asset reconstruction from real-world images through image-to-3D pipelines.
AI.conclusion: A specification-driven, data-augmented approach can substantially improve scalable articulated 3D reconstruction, facilitating broad applicability to synthetic and real-world inputs and seamless integration with vision-language guided labeling and generation.

title: Riemannian Metric Matching for Scalable Geometric Modeling of Distributions
summary: High-dimensional datasets often concentrate near low-dimensional structures, but estimating their geometry from samples typically relies on graphs and kernels that scale poorly with dataset size and dimension. We propose Riemannian metric matching: a denoising probabilistic framework for learning the Riemannian geometry of data using neural networks. Specifically, we learn the carré du champ operator, which, using diffusion geometry, gives us access to the Riemannian geometry toolkit for downstream machine learning and statistical tasks. Our key observation is that the carré du champ operator can be formulated as a conditional expectation over random perturbations of the data, which can be exploited for sample-wise training and constant cost, amortized inference without explicit kernel construction. Empirically, metric matching rivals or improves the accuracy of $k$-NN-based diffusion geometry estimators, while enabling amortized inference that is up to $400\times$ faster, and supports graph-free geometric analysis on high-dimensional images where nearest neighbors break down.

AI.tldr: A denoising probabilistic framework learns the Riemannian geometry of high-dimensional data by estimating the carré du champ operator through random perturbations, enabling amortized, kernel-free diffusion geometry with faster inference.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional data lie on or near low-dimensional manifolds, but traditional graph/kernel-based geometry estimation scales poorly with dataset size and dimensionality. A scalable, differentiable approach to learn Riemannian geometry is desirable.
AI.method: Train neural networks to estimate the carré du champ operator as a conditional expectation over random perturbations of the data, leveraging diffusion geometry for downstream tasks. This yields amortized, kernel-free inference without constructing explicit graphs or kernels.
AI.result: Metric matching matches or surpasses k-NN-based diffusion geometry methods in accuracy, while delivering up to 400x faster amortized inference and enabling graph-free geometric analysis on high-dimensional data where nearest-neighbor methods fail.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a scalable, neural approach to learn the Riemannian geometry of data via the carré du champ, enabling efficient diffusion-geometry-based analyses without explicit kernel construction and with robust performance improvements.

title: SED:Lightweight Saliency prediction for Event-based data via Distillation
summary: Event-based saliency prediction has gained attention recently, as combining event cameras with saliency estimation can act as an upstream stage that naturally improves the efficiency of downstream eventbased perception at the edge. However, current approaches are either neuromorphic, underperforming on event-based saliency benchmarks, or too heavy for resource-constrained edge applications due to their reliance on transformers or 3D convolutions. Drawing inspiration from efficient convolutional modules, SED and aiming to exploit the temporal information in event data, we propose a lightweight network, trained through knowledge distillation, built on a Depthwise Spatio-Temporal Block (DSTconv) -- a factorization of the 3D depthwise separable convolution. Relative to its teacher, our model reduces the model size from 180 MB to 0.32 MB (562x) and the parameter count from 45M to 81k (554x), while matching or outperforming it on the N-DHF1K and N-UCF Sports datasets. Moreover, it generalizes strongly beyond its training distribution, transferring from synthetic to real event data where a model trained from scratch fails.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, distillation-trained DSTconv-based network for event-based saliency; achieves massive model/parameter compression while matching or surpassing a heavy teacher on N-DHF1K and N-UCF Sports, with strong synthetic-to-real generalization.
AI.motivation: Efficient, edge-ready event-based saliency is needed, as current neuromorphic approaches are underperforming or rely on heavy transformers/3D convs unsuitable for constrained devices; exploit temporal information with lightweight modules.
AI.method: Introduce Depthwise Spatio-Temporal Block (DSTconv), a factorized 3D depthwise separable convolution. Build a lightweight network trained via knowledge distillation from a larger teacher. Train/evaluate on synthetic data and test cross-domain generalization to real event data using N-DHF1K and N-UCF Sports benchmarks.
AI.result: Model size reduced from 180 MB to 0.32 MB (562x) and parameters from 45M to 81k (554x). The student matches or exceeds the teacher on the listed benchmarks and generalizes from synthetic to real event data, whereas a model trained from scratch fails.
AI.conclusion: DSTconv-based distillation yields a highly efficient, transferrable event-based saliency model suitable for edge deployments, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization and potential applicability to other event-based perception tasks.

title: More with LESS -- Local Scene Representations for Tactile Imaging
summary: Tactile imaging seeks to reconstruct the internal structure of soft objects through touch sensing, with applications in medical diagnosis and robotic manipulation. Recent self-supervised learning approaches have shown promising results, but rely on global, unstructured representations and robot-controlled sensing, limiting generalization and practical use. We propose Local Encoder for Spatial Sensing (LESS), an object-centric tactile representation that exploits the local nature of touch. The tactile scene is modeled as a grid of recurrent encoders with local receptive fields, whose states are fused to reconstruct 2D or 3D images of internal structure. This compositional design enables strong generalization: models trained on single-inclusion phantoms accurately image objects with multiple inclusions and varying sizes. The local structure further supports spatial uncertainty estimation. In addition, we enable hand-held tactile imaging via external pose tracking and human-like palpation data, and extend tactile imaging to full 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: LESS introduces a grid of local recurrent encoders with narrow receptive fields to form an object-centric tactile representation, enabling robust 2D/3D imaging, spatial uncertainty estimation, and practical hand-held sensing with pose tracking and full 3D reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of global, unstructured tactile representations and robot-controlled sensing by leveraging locality and compositionality to improve generalization, practicality, and uncertainty quantification in tactile imaging.
AI.method: A grid of recurrent encoders with local receptive fields models the tactile scene. States are fused to reconstruct 2D/3D internal structures. The approach is object-centric, enabling generalization from single-inclusion phantoms to multi-inclusion objects. Includes spatial uncertainty estimation, hand-held tactile imaging via external pose tracking, and extension to full 3D reconstruction.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong generalization: models trained on single-inclusion phantoms accurately image objects with multiple inclusions and varying sizes. Local structure supports spatial uncertainty estimation and enables hand-held sensing and 3D reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: A local, compositional tactile representation improves generalization, provides uncertainty awareness, and enhances practicality by supporting hand-held operation and 3D imaging.

title: Improving Lunar Topography with Deep Learning Schrödinger Bridges
summary: Increasing the resolution of planetary topography models can enable a better understanding of surface processes and geomorphology; however, existing analytical super-resolution methods are expensive and difficult to apply at large scales. Generative models provide the tools to learn complex relationships within data and can be applied at scale due to hardware accelerators and parallelization. We present a diffusion-based Schrödinger Bridge (SB) generative modeling approach for lunar topography super-resolution, connecting the distribution of low-resolution topography to that of high-resolution topography, incorporating physically-constraining optical imagery. Our approach is inspired by existing Shape-from-Shading methods, which improve a priori low-resolution topography by using optical images at the target resolution. We train SBs on a novel dataset of rendered lunar topography, emulating optical imagery from the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Narrow Angle Camera. The result is a flexible approach for topography super-resolution which can provide pixel-level uncertainties in the reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based Schrödinger Bridge model for lunar topography super-resolution that leverages optical imagery and a synthetic dataset to map LR to HR topography with pixel-level uncertainty estimates; scalable and physics-informed.
AI.motivation: Planetary topography at high resolution is valuable for surface processes and geomorphology, but current analytical SR methods are costly and not scalable. Diffusion-based generative models offer scalable, flexible learning and uncertainty quantification. Incorporating Shape-from-Shading-type physics and optical imagery can constrain reconstructions to physically plausible topography.
AI.method: Train Schrödinger Bridge diffusion models to learn a mapping from low-resolution to high-resolution topography distributions, conditioned on target-resolution optical images. Use a novel rendered lunar topography dataset simulating LRO NAC imagery to train and validate. The approach yields pixel-level uncertainty maps and integrates light-physics constraints.
AI.result: Proposes a flexible, scalable SR method producing high-fidelity topography with uncertainty quantification; validated on synthetic data and aligned with physics-inspired SHS methods; capable of large-scale application due to hardware acceleration and parallelization.
AI.conclusion: The SB-based diffusion framework provides a promising avenue for planetary topography super-resolution, combining physics-informed constraints with generative modeling to enhance resolution while offering uncertainty estimates; future work should test on real data and quantify improvements over existing SR methods.

title: Squeeze-Release: Iterative Pruning with Exact Structural Minimization
summary: Unstructured pruning produces sparse weight tensors, but the standard implementation keeps tensor shapes unchanged so the deployed model is no smaller than before pruning. We present an exact structural rewrite, which we call minimization, that converts a masked network into a smaller dense network with the same forward function up to floating-point rounding. The Squeeze-Release cycle iterates pruning and minimization with an intermediate release step that re-enables the exact-zero positions inside the compacted tensors as small calibrated noise, turning otherwise wasted capacity back into trainable parameters. Successive cycles use that capacity to find structural redundancy a single pass cannot reach. We additionally introduce CompensatedLayerNorm, a function-preserving replacement for LayerNorm that extends minimization to channel reduction across LayerNorm-equipped residual streams. Squeeze-Release compresses the deployable network to 39x smaller than the unpruned model on a fully-connected model network and 14.8x smaller on modern CNN (ConvNeXt-Tiny), at comparable accuracy. In addition we prove that the rewrite can be extended to transformer architectures.

AI.tldr: Proposes Squeeze-Release: a cycle of unstructured pruning and a novel exact structural rewrite (minimization) that converts masked networks into smaller dense networks with identical forward behavior (within FP rounding). Introduces CompensatedLayerNorm for channel-reducing LayerNorm in residuals, enabling broader compression. Demonstrates large deployable-size reductions (up to 39x for FC, 14.8x for ConvNeXt-Tiny) with comparable accuracy, and claims extendability to transformers.
AI.motivation: Overcome the inefficiency of unstructured pruning that leaves models with the same parameter shapes, by enabling actual density reduction without sacrificing forward function or accuracy; increase deployable compression through iterative pruning and exact rewrites.
AI.method: Minimization: an exact structural rewrite that removes masked parameters to yield a smaller dense network with the same forward function up to FP rounding. Squeeze-Release: iterative cycle of pruning and minimization; an intermediate release step reintroduces prunable positions as calibrated noise to recover trainable capacity. CompensatedLayerNorm: a function-preserving LayerNorm replacement that supports channel reduction across LayerNorm-equipped residuals. The authors also claim a formal extension of the rewrite to transformer architectures.
AI.result: On a fully-connected model network, deployable size is 39x smaller than the unpruned model; on ConvNeXt-Tiny (CNN) 14.8x smaller with comparable accuracy. The rewrite is purportedly general enough to extend to transformer architectures.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables substantial, function-preserving compression through a structured pruning-minimization loop and a channel-aware normalization replacement, with broad applicability across architectures including transformers.

title: HPSv3++: Scaling Reward Models Across the Full Spectrum of Diffusion Model Capabilities
summary: Reward models guide text-to-image (T2I) systems toward outputs aligned with human preferences. However, typical reward models such as HPSv3 are trained on pre-annotated data from earlier T2I models, without accounting for quality discriminative shifts arising from evolving model capabilities and reinforcement learning (RL) iterations, limiting their broader applicability. In this work, we propose HPSv3++, a reward model framework that elevates the HPSv3 model for varying T2I model capabilities and their RL iteration changes across the full capability-iteration spectrum. Specifically, we first introduce HPDv3++, a 212K dual-dimension preference dataset annotated for text fidelity and aesthetic quality using a recent high-capability (Qwen-Image) model with human supervision. We then propose a two-stage training framework. Stage 1 employs data-aware orthogonal gradient projection to incorporate diverse aesthetic perception from HPDv3++ while preserving the original effective human preference knowledge in HPSv3. Stage 2 further leverages unlabeled data from T2I models spanning different capability levels and RL iterations, and introduces a joint capability-iterations conditioned signal for the reward model together with a standard deviation-driven unsupervised guidance mechanism, strengthening reward model across the capability-iteration spectrum. HPSv3++ achieves state-of-the-art preference prediction, outperforming HPSv3 9.8% on HPDv3, 5.5% on GenAI-Bench, while achieving 79.1%/88.1% on our proposed HPDv3++. When used for T2I RL training, it consistently improves GenEval scores across diverse T2I models, demonstrating its wide-range capabilities. The code is available at https://github.com/PlantPotatoOnMoon/HPSv3-PlusPlus.

AI.tldr: HPSv3++ is a two-stage reward-model framework that generalizes to varying T2I capabilities and RL iterations, using HPDv3++ (a 212K dual-dimension dataset) and data-aware gradient projection plus unlabeled-data, capability-iteration conditioned guidance, achieving state-of-the-art preference prediction and improved GenEval performance.
AI.motivation: Address distribution shifts in reward models caused by evolving T2I model capabilities and RL iterations; prior models (e.g., HPSv3) trained on older data struggle to generalize across the capability-iteration spectrum.
AI.method: Stage 1: data-aware orthogonal gradient projection to integrate diverse aesthetic judgments from HPDv3++ while preserving existing HPSv3 knowledge. Stage 2: leverage unlabeled data across capability levels and RL iterations, introducing a joint capability-iterations conditioned signal for the reward model and a standard deviation-driven unsupervised guidance mechanism.
AI.result: HPSv3++ achieves state-of-the-art preference prediction, outperforming HPSv3 by 9.8% on HPDv3 and 5.5% on GenAI-Bench, and attaining 79.1%/88.1% on HPDv3++. In RL training for T2I, it yields consistent GenEval improvements across diverse models.
AI.conclusion: HPSv3++ generalizes reward modeling across the full capability-iteration spectrum, enabling better-aligned T2I outputs and robust RL performance; the accompanying HPDv3++ dataset supports broader evaluation and transfer.

title: When Language Representations Interact: Separability and Cross-Lingual Effects in LLMs
comment: Trustworthy AI for Good (AI4Good) Workshop @ ICML 2026
summary: Large language models exhibit strong multilingual capabilities, however, their internal representations are difficult to interpret. Understanding these interactions is important for ensuring reliable behavior in multilingual systems. Recent work has shown that causal-geometric structure can explain how certain concepts are encoded as approximately linear and separable directions, but whether this framework extends to multilingual models, where language identity is correlated and hierarchical, is underexplored. We apply causal-geometric analysis to multilingual LLMs, studying 28 bilingual contrasts across three models, allowing us to analyze when languages behave as approximately independent factors and when structured dependencies persist. We find evidence that language concepts admit stable linear representations that are largely separable under a covariance-adjusted (causal) inner product, with structured deviations reflecting linguistic similarity. Moreover, languages within the same family (such as Germanic or Romance) exhibit a simplex-like geometric structure, suggesting hierarchical organization. These results extend causal-geometric interpretability to multilingual settings and provide insight into how separability and similarity may exist in multilingual LLM representations, motivating interpretability analyses that diagnose when and how structured dependencies between concepts can be anticipated. This has implications for trustworthy deployment, as residual structure between languages may lead to unintended cross-lingual effects when models are monitored or intervened upon.

AI.tldr: Causal-geometric analysis reveals separable, stable linear representations of language concepts in multilingual LLMs; languages exhibit hierarchy-like, simplex structure with family-level similarities, highlighting residual cross-lingual dependencies.
AI.motivation: Understand multilingual representations to ensure trustworthy deployment and diagnose cross-lingual interactions in LLMs; extend causal-geometric interpretability to multilingual settings.
AI.method: Apply causal-geometric analysis to multilingual LLMs across 28 bilingual contrasts in three models; assess when language factors are approximately independent under a covariance-adjusted inner product; characterize structured deviations due to linguistic similarity; identify simplex-like geometry within language families.
AI.result: Language concepts have stable, largely separable linear representations under a covariance-adjusted inner product; deviations align with linguistic similarity; languages within the same family form a simplex-like geometric structure, indicating hierarchical organization; extends causal-geometric interpretability to multilingual settings.
AI.conclusion: The work extends causal-geometric interpretability to multilingual models, showing separability and similarity in multilingual representations and highlighting how residual language structure can entail cross-lingual effects during monitoring or intervention, with implications for trustworthy deployment.

title: Giving AI a Headache: Acoustic Adversarial Attacks to Computer Vision Applications
summary:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly used to automate a variety of real-world computer vision (CV) applications, such as autonomous vehicle control, facial recognition, and security cameras. Recent research has shown that acoustic vibration can induce real physical motion in cameras, interfering with their internal stabilization mechanisms. Because the motion falls outside the conditions the stabilization system was designed to handle, the system introduces artifacts into the frame, causing AI-based CV models to misclassify, miss targets, or hallucinate objects.
  Previous work used ultrasonic frequencies (>20 kHz) to perform short-range attacks, which limits them to short distances due to the attenuation exhibited by high frequencies. In this work, we investigate acoustic attacks using lower frequencies in the audible range (<20 kHz), and we further expand our analysis to include how various image and object features are affected by the attacks.
  Specifically, we performed physical experiments to demonstrate the viability of our attacks on an off-the-shelf object detection model (YOLO11) by resonating a commercially available camera with various frequencies. Based on our results, we provide insights into several factors that make an AI CV system more vulnerable to these attacks, which could help inform the development of future mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: Acoustic vibrations at audible frequencies can physically shake cameras, causing stabilization-induced frame artifacts that degrade AI computer vision models; the authors demonstrate the viability of such attacks against an off-the-shelf detector (YOLO11) and analyze factors that influence vulnerability, highlighting mitigation needs.
AI.motivation: To extend adversarial acoustics research beyond ultrasonic short-range attacks by exploring audible-range frequencies and to understand how different image/object features are affected, informing defense design for CV systems.
AI.method: Physical experiments resonating a commercially available camera with various audible-range frequencies (<20 kHz) and evaluating impact on an off-the-shelf object detector (YOLO11); analysis of how image and object features respond to these artifacts and identification of factors that increase vulnerability.
AI.result: The study demonstrates the viability of audible-range acoustic attacks against YOLO11, showing that stabilization-induced frame artifacts can lead to misdetections or corrupted feature representations; it also provides insights into factors that make CV systems more vulnerable and points toward mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Audible-range acoustic attacks pose a real threat to CV systems by inducing stabilization artifacts; the work underscores the need for defense mechanisms that consider camera dynamics and sensor-induced artifacts, and suggests directions for future mitigation research.

title: Can Deep Neural Networks Improve Compression of Very Large Scientific Data?
summary: Error-bounded lossy compression is a fundamental technique for managing the rapidly growing volumes of scientific data produced by modern simulations and observational instruments. Most state-of-the-art-compressors follow a prediction-residual paradigm, where compression effectiveness depends on the quality of the predictor: more accurate predictions generate smaller residuals that are easier to compress. This observation raises a question: can modern machine learning models serve as superior predictors for scientific data compression? Answering this question directly is challenging because developing compression-specific ML predictors requires substantial resources. Instead, we leverage the climate domain where highly accurate pretrained weather forecasting foundation models already exist, making them an ideal testbed. We present a framework that integrates spatial and temporal deep learning models into a conventional error-bounded compression pipeline. The framework supports auto-regressive forecasting models and avoids error accumulation. Using ERA5 climate data as a representative large-scale scientific dataset, we evaluate three distinct ML predictors: a VAEformer-based codec (CRA5), a graph neural network forecaster (GraphCast), and a vision-transformer forecaster (Aurora), against the state-of-the-art compressor SZ3.1 under identical quantization and entropy-coding backends. Our evaluation over approximately 1.7 TB of data reveals a surprising result: although ML predictors generate more accurate predictions and can improve reconstruction quality by up to 91% while achieving up to 9.6x higher compression ratios for highly predictable variables, they do not improve overall dataset-level compression ratio. We show that prediction accuracy alone is insufficient: the spatial structure of the resulting residuals plays a decisive role in entropy coding efficiency.

AI.tldr: ML-based predictors yield higher local prediction accuracy but do not improve dataset-level compression ratios due to residual spatial structure impacting entropy coding.
AI.motivation: Assess whether modern ML predictors can beat traditional compressors in error-bounded lossy compression within a climate-domain testbed using pretrained forecasting models.
AI.method: Integrate spatial-temporal DL predictors (CRA5 VAEformer codec, GraphCast GNN forecaster, Aurora ViT forecaster) into an error-bounded pipeline; compare against SZ3.1 with identical quantization and entropy coding; auto-regressive forecasting to avoid error accumulation; evaluate on ERA5 data (~1.7 TB).
AI.result: Predictions are more accurate and can improve reconstruction quality by up to 91% and achieve up to 9.6x higher compression for highly predictable variables, but fail to improve overall dataset-level compression; entropy coding efficiency is driven by residual structure, not solely prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Improving compression requires considering residual spatial structure and entropy-coder compatibility; ML predictors alone are not a silver bullet for global compression gains in this setting; future work should align predictor design with entropy coding characteristics.

title: Memento: Reconstruct to Remember for Consistent Long Video Generation
summary: Long-form video generation requires recurring subjects to remain consistent across various shots, viewpoints, motions, and scene transitions. Existing temporal decomposition methods improve scalability by generating videos shot by shot. However, they mainly focus on optimizing plausible next-shot continuations without verifying whether the historical memory preserves identity-critical subject evidence. Consequently, as generation proceeds, recurring subjects may be diluted, overwritten, or forgotten. In this paper, we propose Memento, a subject-reconstruction-guided framework that treats subject preservation as an explicit identity grounding problem, based on the premise that a memory bank faithfully preserving a subject should support reconstructing that subject from memory alone. Specifically, Memento jointly trains autoregressive next-shot generation with memory-based subject reconstruction, recovering target appearances using historical memory and global story captions. To disentangle long-range subject evidence from short-range cues, Memento introduces a dual-query memory mechanism, where one query retrieves identity-relevant memory and the other selects short-context keyframes for coherent continuation. Additionally, a subject-aware cinematic data pipeline provides precise reconstruction supervision via consistent, pronoun-free subject descriptions. Experiments demonstrate that Memento achieves state-of-the-art performance in long-term subject consistency, cross-shot coherence, and visual quality.

AI.tldr: Memento uses a memory-based subject reconstruction to enforce identity preservation in long-form video generation, with a dual-query memory and a subject-aware data pipeline, achieving state-of-the-art long-term consistency and coherence.
AI.motivation: Existing shot-by-shot generation methods risk forgetting or diluting recurring subjects across long videos; there is a need for explicit identity grounding to maintain subject memory over time.
AI.method: Joint autoregressive next-shot generation with a memory-based subject reconstruction. Introduces a dual-query memory: one for identity-relevant memory and one for short-context keyframes. A subject-aware cinematic data pipeline provides pronoun-free, consistent subject descriptions for supervision, using historical memory and global story captions to reconstruct target appearances.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance in long-term subject consistency, cross-shot coherence, and visual quality compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Memory-based identity grounding via dual-query memory effectively preserves recurring subjects over long-form video, enabling coherent long-range storytelling and high visual fidelity.

title: MUFFLe: Efficient Model Update Compression via Generalized Deduplication for Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning is well suited to edge environments but is often limited by the uplink cost of transmitting model updates. This Work-in-Progress paper presents MUFFLe, a communication-efficient update compression scheme that integrates generalized deduplication (GD) into the FedAvg pipeline. MUFFLe deduplicates repeated patterns across the update vector, yielding a fixed-rate, variable-count compression scheme. Preliminary experiments on IID MNIST with 20 clients show that MUFFLe reaches the target accuracy of $92.93\%$ with 38~MB cumulative uplink communication, compared with 75~MB for 8-bit quantization, 86~MB for Top-$k$ sparsification, and 310~MB for uncompressed FedAvg. These results demonstrate the feasibility of applying GD to communication-efficient federated learning.

AI.tldr: MUFFLe introduces generalized deduplication for FedAvg to compress federated updates, achieving substantial uplink savings (38 MB) while maintaining target accuracy (92.93%) on IID MNIST with 20 clients.
AI.motivation: To reduce the uplink communication bottleneck in federated learning by exploiting redundancy across update vectors via generalized deduplication.
AI.method: Integrate generalized deduplication into the FedAvg pipeline to create a fixed-rate, variable-count compression scheme that deduplicates repeated patterns across update vectors; evaluate on IID MNIST with 20 clients and compare against 8-bit quantization, Top-k sparsification, and uncompressed FedAvg.
AI.result: MUFFLe achieves the target accuracy of 92.93% with 38 MB cumulative uplink, compared to 75 MB (8-bit quantization), 86 MB (Top-k sparsification), and 310 MB (uncompressed FedAvg). Demonstrates feasibility of applying GD to communication-efficient FL.
AI.conclusion: GD-based update compression is viable for reducing FL communication overhead; MUFFLe provides a practical approach and baseline for further study on efficiency and scalability in FL.

title: HumP-KD: A Hybrid Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Stage Progressive Knowledge Distillation Framework for Efficient Fire Classification
summary: Real-time fire classification systems require models that are simultaneously accurate, computationally efficient, and deployable on resource-constrained hardware. This work proposes \textbf{HumP-KD}, a Hybrid Uncertainty-aware Multi-stage Progressive Knowledge Distillation framework for efficient fire classification. Two datasets, FlameVision and Dataset-II, containing 8,600 and 31,309 images, are used. Various CNN and transformer baselines are applied under standard preprocessing, online augmentation, Gaussian noise and motion blur robustness conditions. The proposed HumP-KD model distills knowledge from two frozen heterogeneous transformer teachers, Swin-Tiny and ViT-Base, along with their Meta-MLP ensemble, into a lightweight MobileViT-S student via three tightly integrated components. Hierarchical Progressive Knowledge Distillation employs a Hierarchical Feature Builder. It generates a fused spatial attention mask to guide distillation toward discriminative regions selectively. Multi-Stage Knowledge Distillation progressively activates three distillation stages across training. On Dataset-II, HumP-KD achieves a mean F1 score of $0.9876 \pm 0.0063$ across 10 independent trials, significantly outperforming the MobileViT-S baseline trained without distillation ($0.9537 \pm 0.0351$), with statistical significance confirmed by both independent t-test ($p = 0.0195$) and Wilcoxon signed-rank test ($W = 1$, $p = 0.0039$). The proposed method also demonstrates strong generalization across datasets and robustness under degraded visual conditions. The student model retains only 4.94M parameters and 19.01Mb model size, representing a $5.7\times$ parameter reduction over Swin-Tiny and a $17.5\times$ reduction over ViT-Base, while achieving 37.72 CPU FPS, making it suitable for real-time deployment.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical, multi-stage distillation framework (HumP-KD) distills knowledge from two frozen transformer teachers to a lightweight MobileViT-S for efficient real-time fire classification, achieving high accuracy with strong robustness and significant model compression.
AI.motivation: Real-time fire classification requires high accuracy while being computationally efficient and deployable on resource-constrained hardware; existing larger models hinder real-time performance and robustness to noise/blur.
AI.method: HumP-KD distills from two frozen heterogeneous transformer teachers (Swin-Tiny and ViT-Base) and a Meta-MLP ensemble into a lightweight MobileViT-S student via three integrated components: (1) Hierarchical Progressive Knowledge Distillation with a Hierarchical Feature Builder that creates a fused spatial attention mask to target discriminative regions; (2) Multi-Stage Knowledge Distillation that progressively activates three distillation stages during training; (3) integration with standard preprocessing, online augmentation, Gaussian noise, and motion blur robustness tests. Datasets FlameVision (8,600) and Dataset-II (31,309) are used for evaluation.
AI.result: On Dataset-II, HumP-KD achieves mean F1 = 0.9876 ± 0.0063 over 10 trials, outperforming the MobileViT-S baseline without distillation (0.9537 ± 0.0351). Statistical tests confirm significance (t-test p=0.0195; Wilcoxon W=1, p=0.0039). The method generalizes across datasets and remains robust under degraded visual conditions. The student model has 4.94M parameters and 19.01 MB, a 5.7× reduction vs Swin-Tiny and 17.5× vs ViT-Base, while delivering 37.72 CPU FPS.
AI.conclusion: HumP-KD enables accurate, robust, and real-time fire classification on resource-constrained hardware with substantial model compression and strong cross-dataset generalization, outperforming baselines.

title: SemPiper: Interactive Code Synthesis for Semantic Operators in Machine Learning Pipelines
summary: Machine learning (ML) pipelines require extensive data preparation, feature engineering, and integration across heterogeneous sources, making them tedious and error-prone to develop. While large language models (LLMs) have recently shown promise for assisting programming tasks, chat-based interfaces provide limited control over pipeline behavior and often produce code that is difficult to optimize or integrate into production systems. We demonstrate SemPipes, a novel programming model that extends ML pipelines with declarative, LLM-powered semantic data operators. SemPipes allows developers to specify high-level natural language instructions for data-centric operations, while seamlessly combining these operators with arbitrary Python code from standard data science libraries. For the semantic operators, it synthesizes specialized implementations at pipeline training time, conditioned on dataset characteristics and pipeline context, enabling the flexible yet controlled integration of LLM capabilities. We demonstrate SemPipes through SemPiper, an interactive interface that visualizes computational graphs of the pipelines, synthesized operator implementations, and optimization trajectories produced by an evolutionary search procedure. Attendees can explore three end-to-end scenarios, modify pipelines, inspect generated code, and observe how semantic operators are synthesized and iteratively optimized. The demonstration highlights how declarative semantic operators enable controllable, optimizable, and practical integration of LLMs into ML pipeline development.

AI.tldr: SemPipes introduces declarative, LLM-powered semantic data operators to extend ML pipelines; operators are synthesized at train time based on data and context, enabling controllable integration of LLMs, demonstrated via SemPiper with visualization and evolution-based optimization across three end-to-end scenarios.
AI.motivation: ML pipelines require extensive data prep, feature engineering, and cross-source integration, making development tedious and error‑prone. While LLMs help with programming tasks, chat interfaces offer limited control and hard-to-optimize code. There is a need for controllable, optimizable integration of LLM capabilities into pipelines.
AI.method: Extend ML pipelines with declarative semantic data operators powered by LLMs; synthesize specialized operator implementations at pipeline training time conditioned on dataset characteristics and pipeline context; combine these operators with arbitrary Python code from standard data science libraries; provide SemPiper interface that visualizes computational graphs, synthesized operator code, and optimization trajectories from an evolutionary search.
AI.result: Demonstration of SemPipes via SemPiper, an interactive interface showing pipeline graphs, generated operator implementations, and evolutionary optimization trajectories; supports three end-to-end scenarios, pipeline modification, and inspection of synthesized code, illustrating controllable, optimizable, and practical LLM integration into ML pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Declarative semantic operators enable controllable, optimizable, and practical integration of LLMs into ML pipeline development.

title: CottonLeafVision: An Explainable and Robust Deep Learning Framework for Cotton Leaf Disease Classification
