comment: This paper contains 11 figures and 4 tables. It was Presented at 18th IEEE International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Communication Networks (CICN) 2026
summary: Globally, cotton is a highly economically beneficial crop, as the textile industry heavily depends on it. So, the precise identification and detection of cotton leaf disease is crucial for economic stability. The development goal of "CottonLeafVision" is to accurately classify and detect cotton leaf disease. With this goal, we have evaluated multiple pretrained Deep Convolutional Neural Networks, including DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and VGG19 on a publicly available cotton leaf disease image dataset. This image dataset includes seven classes, six disease classes, and one healthy class, collected under various field conditions reflecting real-world challenges. Among these pretrained models, with DenseNet201, we have achieved the highest classification accuracy of 98%. To enhance the model reliability and interpretability, we have implemented different techniques and methods such as Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), occlusion sensitivity analysis and adversarial training to increase the noise resistance of the model. Finally, we have developed a prototype in order to utilize the model's capabilities on real life agriculture. This paper shows the deep learning model's capabilities to classify the disease in real-life cotton disease management situations.

AI.tldr: DenseNet201 achieves 98% accuracy for seven-class cotton leaf disease classification on a field-condition dataset, with interpretability (Grad-CAM, occlusion) and adversarial training; prototype deployed for real-world cotton disease management.
AI.motivation: Cotton is economically important; precise detection of cotton leaf diseases is crucial for economic stability and crop management; goal is to develop 'CottonLeafVision' to accurately classify and detect diseases.
AI.method: Evaluate pretrained deep CNNs (DenseNet201, InceptionV3, VGG19) on a seven-class cotton leaf disease image dataset (six diseases + healthy) collected under varying field conditions. DenseNet201 achieved best performance (98%). Incorporate Grad-CAM, occlusion sensitivity analysis, and adversarial training to improve reliability/noise robustness. Develop a prototype for real-life agricultural use.
AI.result: DenseNet201 achieved 98% classification accuracy on the seven-class dataset. Added interpretability via Grad-CAM and occlusion analyses and boosted robustness through adversarial training. A prototype was developed for real-world application in cotton disease management.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a deep learning model can classify cotton leaf diseases effectively in real-life management scenarios, with measures to improve interpretability and robustness and a practical deployment prototype.

title: Be My Tutor: On-Policy Co-Distillation for Mutual LLM Improvement via Peer Feedback
summary: We study multi-domain LLM training in which two models, each stronger in a different domain, co-evolve by tutoring each other through on-policy feedback. Unlike one-way distillation or single-model fine-tuning, our goal is mutual Pareto improvement: each model improves across domains without losing its original strength. To this end, we propose On-Policy Co-Distillation (OPCoD), where each student's self-distillation is conditioned on its own correct rollout and feedback from its peer. To make feedback exchange effective, OPCoD uses cognizance-based gating to decide when to give feedback and feedback anchoring to ground feedback in the problem. On Science Q\&A tasks, OPCoD consistently outperforms baselines and achieves Pareto improvement across all evaluated domain pairs and students.

AI.tldr: OPCoD enables mutual Pareto improvement in multi-domain LLMs via on-policy co-distillation with peer feedback, gating, and anchoring, achieving Pareto improvements on Science Q&A tasks.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of one-way distillation and fine-tuning by enabling two specialized models to mutually improve across domains without sacrificing strengths.
AI.method: Propose On-Policy Co-Distillation (OPCoD): each student self-distills conditioned on its own correct rollout and receives feedback from its peer; uses cognizance-based gating to decide when to give feedback, and feedback anchoring to ground feedback; operates in an on-policy feedback loop.
AI.result: OPCod consistently outperforms baselines and achieves Pareto improvement across all evaluated domain pairs and students on Science Q&A tasks.
AI.conclusion: Mutual co-distillation with on-policy feedback yields Pareto improvements across domains, offering a viable framework for dual-domain LLM co-training.

title: ClinHallu: A Benchmark for Diagnosing Stage-Wise Hallucinations in Medical MLLM Reasoning
summary: Building trustworthy medical multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is critical for reliable clinical decision support. Existing medical hallucination benchmarks mainly focus on data collection, but often ignore where hallucinations originate within the reasoning process. We find that hallucination sources vary across samples: errors may arise from visual misrecognition, incorrect medical knowledge recall, or flawed reasoning integration. To enable source-level hallucination diagnosis, we introduce ClinHallu, a benchmark for stage-wise hallucination diagnosis in medical MLLM reasoning. ClinHallu contains 7,031 validated instances, where each instance is augmented with a structured reasoning trace decomposed into Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration. We also use stage-replacement interventions to measure how correcting specific stages affects the final answer. Beyond evaluation, we show that trace-supervised fine-tuning reduces stage-wise hallucinations. ClinHallu provides a fine-grained hallucination testbed for diagnosing and mitigating reasoning failures in medical MLLMs. The benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/alibaba-damo-academy/ClinHallu.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ClinHallu, a 7,031-instance benchmark for stage-wise hallucination diagnosis in medical multimodal LLM reasoning. It provides traces decomposed into Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration, enables stage-replacement interventions, and demonstrates trace-supervised fine-tuning to reduce stage-wise hallucinations; dataset is publicly available.
AI.motivation: Current medical MLLM benchmarks emphasize data-level hallucinations and overlook where errors arise in the reasoning process. A source-level diagnosis is needed to improve reliability and trust in clinical decision support.
AI.method: Construct ClinHallu with 7,031 validated instances augmented with structured reasoning traces (Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, Reasoning Integration). Use stage-replacement interventions to quantify the impact of correcting each stage on the final answer. Apply trace-supervised fine-tuning to mitigate stage-wise hallucinations.
AI.result: The authors observe that hallucination sources vary across samples (visual misrecognition, incorrect medical knowledge recall, flawed reasoning integration). Stage-replacement interventions reveal how correcting specific stages affects final answers. Trace-supervised fine-tuning reduces stage-wise hallucinations, and ClinHallu serves as a fine-grained testbed for diagnosing and mitigating reasoning failures in medical MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: ClinHallu provides a fine-grained benchmark for diagnosing and mitigating reasoning failures in medical MLLMs, enabling stage-wise analysis and targeted mitigation. The benchmark is publicly available at the stated repository.

title: Discovery under Hypothesis Redundancy: A Geometric Theory of Discovery Bottlenecks
summary: Scientific discovery saturates when new hypotheses cease to provide independent information, even if the nominal hypothesis space remains large. We study hybrid discovery systems that combine structured local search with LLM-generated non-local proposals and pose the Search Compression Hypothesis: non-local exploration helps only when three geometric conditions co-occur: spectral compression, orthogonal escape from the explored span, and residual signal alignment with the target. We formalize these conditions, derive necessary conditions for hybrid advantage, and test the mechanism in controlled synthetic environments, large-scale A-share factor discovery, and symbolic-regression benchmarks; a public tabular operational sanity check tests the associated budget-allocation implication. Signal-planting and directed-versus-random experiments show that novelty alone is insufficient: random orthogonal jumps expand coverage but do not improve yield without predictive alignment. Across compression sweeps, real factor archives, and LLM-SRBench tasks, hybrid gains concentrate in weakly represented but target-bearing directions and vanish as the hypothesis space approaches full rank. The framework turns LLM-guided discovery from generic novelty search into a diagnostic procedure for deciding when directed non-local exploration is warranted.

AI.tldr: Hybrid discovery systems combining structured local search with non-local LLM-generated proposals yield gains only under specific geometric conditions; the paper formalizes these conditions, derives necessary criteria for advantage, and validates them across synthetic and real-world discovery tasks, showing novelty alone is insufficient.
AI.motivation: Scientific discovery saturates when new hypotheses fail to provide independent information. The work investigates when and how non-local, LLM-guided proposals can augment local search by injecting orthogonal, predictive directions, aiming to avoid wasted exploration and to diagnose when directed exploration is warranted.
AI.method: 1) Formalize three geometric conditions—spectral compression, orthogonal escape from the explored span, and residual signal alignment with the target—as necessary ingredients for non-local proposals to add value. 2) Derive necessary conditions for hybrid advantage. 3) Empirically test the framework in controlled synthetic environments, large-scale A-share factor discovery, and symbolic-regression benchmarks, plus a public tabular sanity check for budget allocation. 4) Conduct signal-planting and directed-vs-random experiments to separate novelty from alignment.
AI.result: Hybrid gains concentrate in directions that are weakly represented yet carry the target signal; random orthogonal jumps increase coverage but do not improve yield without predictive alignment. Across compression sweeps, real factor archives, and LLM-SRBench tasks, the benefits diminish as the hypothesis space approaches full rank. Novelty alone is insufficient for improvement. The framework reframes LLM-guided discovery as a diagnostic to decide when directed non-local exploration is warranted.
AI.conclusion: The work reframes LLM-guided discovery as a diagnostic procedure that determines when to apply directed non-local exploration, rather than a general-purpose novelty search. It provides formal conditions, empirical validation, and practical implications for budget allocation and targeted exploration in large hypothesis spaces.

title: A Low-Rank Subspace Analysis of LLM Interventions
summary: Interventions designed to modify a particular behavior in LLMs, such as refusal or sycophancy, often produce unintended changes in other behaviors. This lack of targeted control makes it difficult to design and implement reliable safety controls. To understand these side-effects, we introduce a diagnostic framework for analyzing interacting behaviors in LLMs. We model behaviors as low-rank subspaces in activation space, and study how interventions influence across behaviors. Across multiple instruction-tuned models (7B-70B) and across refusal, jailbreak, and sycophancy settings, we find that different behaviors share internal representations, and intervening on one behavior alters others in asymmetric ways. Some behaviors act as upstream control points whose interventions propagate broadly across other behaviors, while others remain more isolated. We relate these effects to two geometric quantities: (i) the overlap between behavior subspaces, measured as the average squared cosine of principal angles, and (ii) the angle between each behavior subspace and the decision subspace (capturing the model's final decision e.g., refuse vs. comply). Empirically, intervention effects on other behaviors tend to be larger for behavior pairs with higher subspace overlap, and for source behaviors whose subspaces lie closer (smaller angle) to the decision subspace. These findings highlight a challenge for targeted behavior control: behaviors are difficult to modify independently, as interventions can propagate through shared representations and asymmetric interactions.

AI.tldr: A framework treats LLM behaviors as low-rank subspaces in activation space to study how safety interventions affect related behaviors; cross-behavior changes correlate with subspace overlap and proximity to the final decision subspace.
AI.motivation: Designing targeted safety controls is difficult because interventions for one behavior often alter others. Understanding inter-behavior dependencies can enable better, more reliable controls in LLMs.
AI.method: Model behaviors (e.g., refusal, jailbreak, sycophancy) as low-rank subspaces in activation space, across 7B–70B instruction-tuned models. Analyze how interventions propagate across behaviors. Quantify overlap with average squared cosine of principal angles between subspaces and the angle between each behavior subspace and the decision subspace (final refuse/comply).
AI.result: Behaviors share internal representations; intervening on one behavior alters others asymmetrically. Some behaviors act as upstream control points with broad propagation; others are more isolated. Interventions have larger cross-behavior effects when subspaces have greater overlap and when the source behavior subspace is closer (smaller angle) to the decision subspace.
AI.conclusion: Independent modification of behaviors is challenging due to shared representations and asymmetric interactions. Geometry of behavior subspaces offers a predictive lens for cross-behavior effects, informing safer, more targeted control strategies.

title: RepFusion: Leveraging Multimodal Priors for Denoising in Representation Space
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are widely used in text-to-image (T2I) systems, but they are typically limited to text encoding, while denoising is handled by newly trained generative backbones. The emergence of representation autoencoders (RAEs) shifts the generation target toward semantically structured visual representations, creating a latent space that is more compatible with pretrained LLM priors. Inspired by multimodal LLMs (MLLMs), where an MLP projector is sufficient to align clean visual representations with a pretrained LLM, we repurpose the MLLM itself as a noisy representation encoder, extending this mechanism from clean to noisy inputs. We present RepFusion, which uses the resulting MLLM outputs as the conditioning signal for a diffusion transformer. In controlled comparisons at similar inference budgets, RepFusion outperforms baselines that devote comparable capacity to newly initialized denoisers. These results demonstrate that MLLMs provide strong priors for denoising visual representations and that, by conditioning on evolving noisy representations, test-time compute can be productively spent on repeated MLLM conditioning in modern T2I systems.

AI.tldr: RepFusion leverages multimodal LLMs as noisy representation encoders to condition diffusion-based T2I, obtaining stronger denoising priors and better performance within fixed inference budgets.
AI.motivation: Address the misalignment between representation autoencoders’ latent spaces and pretrained LLM priors; exploit LLMs’ alignment capabilities to denoise and structure visual representations efficiently.
AI.method: Repurpose the multimodal LLM (MLLM) as a noisy representation encoder and use its outputs as the conditioning signal for a diffusion transformer; repeatedly condition on evolving noisy representations during inference to guide generation.
AI.result: Controlled comparisons at similar inference budgets show RepFusion outperforms baselines that allocate comparable capacity to newly initialized denoisers, indicating that MLLMs provide strong priors for denoising and that test-time compute can be productively spent on repeated MLLM conditioning.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs can provide powerful priors for denoising visual representations; conditioning on evolving noisy representations enables more effective text-to-image generation with limited extra trainable parameters, suggesting a paradigm where test-time conditioning leverages pretrained multimodal priors.

title: Running the Gauntlet: Re-evaluating the Capabilities of Agents Beyond Familiar Environments
summary: As agentic systems continue to evolve and are widely deployed in real-world scenarios, there is a growing demand to faithfully evaluate their capabilities. However, current benchmarks are typically built on popular applications with relatively simple tasks and focus on a narrow set of capabilities while overlooking broader dimensions, resulting in saturated performance on modern agents and failing to probe their limitations. To this end, we introduce GauntletBench, a web-based benchmark for evaluating agent generalisation in challenging scenarios, focusing on three underexplored capabilities (temporal perception, graphical understanding, and 3D reasoning), across five less-covered professional applications (Video Editor, Workflow Builder, 3D Modeller, Flight Analyser, and Circuit Designer), each with 20 vision-intensive tasks (100 in total). Our benchmark provides a modular pipeline that comprises an environment compatible with both open- and closed-source agent frameworks, a controlled web-based application, a well-structured task suite, and an automated evaluation engine with diverse metrics. Contrary to widespread expectations, our empirical results reveal that frontier agentic systems remain far from achieving human-level performance. Even the state-of-the-art agent achieves only a 19.1% success rate on our GauntletBench, highlighting the limitations in these overlooked capabilities and generalisation. By comparison, non-expert human annotators achieve over 80% success on our challenging yet feasible tasks, revealing the substantial gap between current agent capabilities and those required for complex real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: GauntletBench provides a web-based, modular benchmark to test agent generalisation on temporal perception, graphical understanding, and 3D reasoning across five professions (Video Editor, Workflow Builder, 3D Modeller, Flight Analyser, Circuit Designer) with 100 tasks; frontier agents perform ~19% vs humans ~80%.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks focus on narrow, popular tasks and saturate performance, failing to probe broader capabilities and real-world generalisation; there is a need for challenging, modular benchmarks that cover understudied but critical competencies.
AI.method: A modular, web-based pipeline including an environment compatible with open- and closed-source agent frameworks, a controlled web application, a 100-task suite (20 per domain) targeting three capabilities (temporal perception, graphical understanding, 3D reasoning), and an automated evaluation engine with diverse metrics.
AI.result: State-of-the-art agent achieves 19.1% success on GauntletBench; non-expert human annotators exceed 80% success, indicating a substantial gap between current agents and human-level performance in these capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals significant limitations in agent generalisation and in the underexplored capabilities, underscoring the need for targeted research to improve temporal, graphical, and 3D reasoning across real-world tasks.

title: RATS! Patches Talk Through Registers: Emergent Parts in Register Attention Transformers
summary: When humans see a bird, they recognize far more than just "bird" -- they see a head, wings, and talons, a structured assembly of reusable parts that can be identified across every bird they have ever seen. We ask whether a self-supervised visual model can discover the same compositional structure on its own. To this end, we propose RATS (Register Attention Transformers), which decomposes the classification token into N learnable register tokens that route patch information through an L->N->N->L bottleneck via a three-step compress-communicate-broadcast attention. The N registers are partitioned across the H attention heads, so that registers assigned to different heads do not interact with each other. Without auxiliary losses or part annotations, each register spontaneously specializes into a proto-semantic region whose emerging structure resembles object parts. RATS surpasses all baselines by +12 mIoU on average across five segmentation benchmarks, with consistent gains on ADE20K (+1.11 mIoU) and COCO (+0.2 AP^m). Its register dictionary further exhibits part-level consistency and semantic proximity across related categories. Our results suggest that RATS may provide a useful architectural prior for structured and interpretable visual representation learning.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised ViT-based model, RATS, learns compositional object parts by decomposing the class token into N register tokens threaded through a compress-communicate-broadcast attention; achieves strong segmentation gains and emergent part-level semantics without supervision.
AI.motivation: Humans perceive structured parts of objects; aim to discover comparable compositional structure in vision models without part annotations or auxiliary losses.
AI.method: Introduce RATS (Register Attention Transformers): decompose the classification token into N learnable register tokens that route patch information through an L->N->N->L bottleneck via a three-step compress-communicate-broadcast attention. Registers are partitioned across H attention heads so different heads do not interact. No auxiliary losses or part annotations; registers spontaneously specialize into proto-semantic regions resembling object parts.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines by +12 mIoU on average across five segmentation benchmarks; ADE20K gains of +1.11 mIoU and COCO gains of +0.2 AP^m. The register dictionary shows part-level consistency and semantic proximity across related categories.
AI.conclusion: RATS provides an architectural prior for structured and interpretable visual representation learning; emergent, semantically meaningful parts arise in a self-supervised setting, with strong segmentation performance to support its usefulness.

title: A theoretical model for task routing in mixture-of-expert transformers
summary: Mixture-of-experts (MoE) layers enable the scaling of transformer models while keeping the inference compute fixed. While task-expert specialization has been observed in empirical studies of frontier MoE transformer models, existing theoretical work analyzes this using continuous mixture models that cannot be used to model natural language effectively. An important open question is to \textit{theoretically explain task-expert specialization in transformer MoE models using discrete models of language}. To address this, we represent structured knowledge via syntactic templates and finite key-value dictionaries, and prove formally that a single-layer MoE transformer can encode knowledge by using experts that specialize in the corresponding tasks. Our construction shows how queries are routed to unique, task-specific experts whose size depends solely on the intrinsic complexity of the given task (i.e. the combined size of its syntactic templates and factual dictionary). Our construction provides a theoretical support for empirical results on localized knowledge circuits in MoE models. We support our theoretical findings with experiments evaluating model performance under varying MoE loss functions.

AI.tldr: Discrete-language MoE transformers can encode task-specific knowledge by routing queries to task-specialized experts; a single-layer MoE suffices when knowledge is represented by syntactic templates and finite dictionaries; expert size scales with task complexity.
AI.motivation: Explain observed task specialization in MoE transformers using discrete language models and bridge theory with natural language, addressing limitations of continuous mixture models.
AI.method: Use syntactic templates and finite key-value dictionaries to represent structured knowledge; construct a single-layer MoE transformer where each task has dedicated experts; prove that queries are routed to unique experts; bound expert size by joint complexity of templates and dictionary; validate with experiments under different MoE loss functions.
AI.result: Formal proof that a single-layer MoE can encode knowledge via task-specific experts; experimental results showing performance changes under various MoE loss functions, supporting the existence of localized knowledge circuits in MoE models.
AI.conclusion: Provides theoretical justification for empirical localization of knowledge in MoE models; demonstrates feasibility of discrete-language-based task specialization and guides design of MoE architectures leveraging templates and dictionaries.

title: OmniVideo-100K: A Dataset for Audio-Visual Reasoning through Structured Scripts and Evidence Chains
summary: Current automated pipelines for audio-visual Question Answering (QA) generally adopt a ``video-caption-QA'' paradigm. However, these methods typically segment videos into short clips and generate separate descriptions for audio and visual modalities. This decoupled processing severs inherent associations between sounds and their visual sources, while independent clip processing often causes inconsistent descriptions of the same entity across segments. Furthermore, coupling long-text comprehension and QA synthesis into a single step often restricts models to localized events, yielding questions lacking long-term temporal connections and deep cross-modal reasoning. To address these issues, we propose an automated data engine featuring two mechanisms: (1) \textbf{Entity-Anchored Video Scripting} transforms videos into structured scripts, comprising summaries, main entity lists, and segment-wise audio-visual descriptions. The entity list serves as a global prior to ensure cross-segment referential consistency and reconstruct audio-visual associations. (2) \textbf{Clue-Guided QA Generation} prompts models to first mine cross-segment, multimodal clues from the script, and subsequently generate QA pairs based on these high-value clues. Leveraging this pipeline, we construct the instruction-tuning dataset \textbf{OmniVideo-100K} and a human-verified test set, \textbf{OmniVideo-Test}. Fine-tuning VITA-1.5, Qwen2.5-Omni-7B and Qwen3-Omni-30B on OmniVideo-100K yields performance gains of up to 20.59% on OmniVideo-Test, demonstrating strong generalization (up to 12.64% improvements) across established benchmarks like Daily-Omni and JointAVBench.

AI.tldr: Introduces Entity-Anchored Video Scripting and Clue-Guided QA Generation to unify audio-visual understanding and enable long-range cross-modal reasoning in video QA; releases OmniVideo-100K (instruction-tuning) and OmniVideo-Test; shows strong gains and generalization across benchmarks and model sizes.
AI.motivation: Current AVQA pipelines rely on decoupled, clip-based processing that separates sounds from their sources and yields inconsistent descriptions across segments, limiting long-term reasoning and cross-modal integration.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) Entity-Anchored Video Scripting converts videos into structured scripts with summaries, a global entity list, and segment-level audio-visual descriptions to enforce cross-segment consistency and re-map AV associations; (2) Clue-Guided QA Generation prompts models to mine cross-segment, multimodal clues from the script and generate QA pairs based on high-value clues; builds OmniVideo-100K and a human-verified OmniVideo-Test; fine-tunes VITA-1.5, Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, and Qwen3-Omni-30B on OmniVideo-100K.
AI.result: Achieves up to 20.59% improvement on OmniVideo-Test; demonstrates strong generalization with up to 12.64% gains on Daily-Omni and JointAVBench benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves cross-modal alignment and long-range temporal reasoning in AVQA, aided by curated datasets and cross-segment entity priors; scalable across multiple backbone models. Potential limitations include dependence on prompt design, evaluation on additional diverse datasets, and the need for human-verified test data.

title: Federated Learning for Feature Generalization with Convex Constraints
comment: Accepted at the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025)
summary: Federated learning (FL) often struggles with generalization due to heterogeneous client data. Local models are prone to overfitting their local data distributions, and even transferable features can be distorted during aggregation. To address these challenges, we propose FedCONST, an approach that adaptively modulates update magnitudes based on the parameter strength of the global model. This prevents over-emphasizing well-learned parameters while reinforcing underdeveloped ones. Specifically, FedCONST employs linear convex constraints to ensure training stability and preserve locally learned generalization capabilities during aggregation. A Gradient Signal to Noise Ratio (GSNR) analysis further validates the effectiveness of FedCONST in enhancing feature transferability and robustness. As a result, FedCONST effectively aligns local and global objectives, mitigating overfitting and promoting stronger generalization across diverse FL environments, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: FedCONST adaptively modulates update magnitudes using global parameter strength within linear convex constraints to stabilize aggregation, reducing overfitting and enhancing generalization in federated learning.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity across clients that leads to overfitting of local models and distorted feature transfer during aggregation; need to preserve local generalization while achieving robust global transferability.
AI.method: Apply linear convex constraints to scale client updates by the global parameter strength, adaptively adjusting update magnitudes to avoid over-emphasizing well-learned parameters and reinforce underdeveloped ones; support via Gradient Signal to Noise Ratio (GSNR) analysis to justify robustness.
AI.result: Improved alignment of local and global objectives, reduced overfitting, stronger generalization across diverse FL settings; claims state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: FedCONST delivers stable, generalizable federated learning by constraining update magnitudes and leveraging GSNR insights to preserve local capabilities while achieving strong global transferability.

title: Gaze Heads: How VLMs Look at What They Describe
summary: How a vision-language model internally solves the task of describing an image is far from obvious. We find that the model develops a specific mechanism for this: a small set of attention heads in its language-model backbone, which we call gaze heads, whose attention tracks the image region the model is currently describing. We find them with a simple correlation score from a few forward passes, using comic strips as a controlled testbed where narrative order is laid out spatially. These gaze heads do not just track the image tokens being described: redirecting their attention to a chosen region forces the VLM to describe that region instead. A single attention-mask intervention on the top-100 gaze heads, fewer than 9% of all heads, steers the model's answer to any chosen comic panel at 83.1% accuracy, while the same intervention on random heads fails to redirect the answer, and intervening on all heads destroys generation. The same lever also extends to continuous control: switching the gaze target mid-generation makes the model wrap up its current panel description and move to the new one within a few tokens. Beyond comics, the same intervention redirects answers to chosen regions in natural COCO images. The mechanism further recurs across model sizes from 2B to 32B parameters and across other VLM architectures, although some frozen-encoder families show no comparable head set. More broadly, this shows that targeted edits identified through mechanistic analysis can serve as practical inference-time levers for steering multimodal model behavior, without any retraining. Our code, interactive demo, and datasets are available at https://gaze.baulab.info/

AI.tldr: A small, identifiable set of attention heads in vision-language models acts as 'gaze heads' that track the image region being described. Targeted attention edits can steer the model's description to a chosen region with high accuracy (83.1% for comic panels), without retraining, and this mechanism generalizes across natural images (COCO) and model sizes (2B–32B) though not in all architectures.
AI.motivation: To uncover how vision-language models internally solve the task of describing images, and to explore whether their behavior can be controlled at inference time via targeted, non-destructive edits.
AI.method: Identify gaze heads by computing a correlation score across a few forward passes on comic-strip data where narrative order aligns with spatial regions. Use an attention-mask intervention on the top-100 gaze heads (less than 9% of all heads) to steer generation to a chosen region; test both single-target redirection and mid-generation switches. Validate on natural images (COCO) and across model sizes (2B–32B) and various VLM architectures; assess whether frozen-encoder families show the phenomenon; provide code and demos.
AI.result: A small subset of heads (top ~100 gaze heads) robustly tracks the described region. Redirecting their attention to a region biases the model to describe that region; a single intervention achieves 83.1% accuracy for panel-level redirection, while random heads show no effect and masking all heads stops generation. The method supports continuous control by switching targets mid-generation. The effect generalizes to natural images (COCO) and persists across sizes (2B–32B) and multiple architectures, though some frozen-encoder families do not exhibit a comparable gaze-head set.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic analysis reveals actionable inference-time levers for steering multimodal models without retraining. Targeted, minimal interventions can control outputs, enhancing interpretability and controllability, with broad but not universal applicability across architectures.

title: EM-NeSy: Expectation Maximization for Neurosymbolic Learning
summary: Neurosymbolic (NeSy) models integrate neural networks and symbolic reasoning for robust and interpretable AI. State-of-the-art NeSy models require that the symbolic component is expressed in a differentiable way, often complicating the use of approximate inference. We propose EM-NeSy which casts probabilistic NeSy learning as an instance of the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. In the expectation step, we compute the posterior over the neurally predicted symbols conditioned on the label via probabilistic inference. In the maximization step, we update the neural parameters based on this posterior using gradient descent only through the neural component. This formulation unlocks the full potential of the EM algorithm for NeSy learning. It allows NeSy to extend naturally to approximate reasoning without any additional modifications or differentiability requirements of the symbolic component. Furthermore, it recovers the standard end-to-end gradient-based NeSy setting under exact inference. Our experimental results demonstrate the scalability and computational efficiency of EM-NeSy.

AI.tldr: EM-NeSy casts probabilistic NeSy learning as an EM procedure, enabling approximate reasoning with a differentiable neural component while keeping the symbolic part non-differentiable. E-step computes the posterior over predicted symbols given the label; M-step updates neural parameters via gradient descent through the neural component only. It recovers standard end-to-end NeSy under exact inference and shows scalability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional NeSy models require differentiable symbolic components, which hampers the use of approximate inference. EM-NeSy aims to decouple inference from learning, enabling probabilistic reasoning without differentiability constraints and leveraging the EM framework.
AI.method: Apply EM: E-step computes the posterior over neurally predicted symbols conditioned on the label via probabilistic inference; M-step updates neural parameters by gradient descent using this posterior, propagating only through the neural component; symbolic component need not be differentiable. Under exact inference, the approach reduces to standard end-to-end gradient-based NeSy.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate scalability and computational efficiency of EM-NeSy.
AI.conclusion: EM-NeSy enables EM-based probabilistic NeSy learning, allowing approximate reasoning without requiring differentiable symbolic components and recovering standard NeSy when inference is exact; it offers scalable and efficient learning for NeSy systems.

title: Recipe-Controlled Decoder Audit for Structural Knowledge-Graph Completion
summary: We present a recipe-controlled decoder audit (RCDA) for structural transductive knowledge-graph completion (KGC). The audit asks a simple reporting question: before attributing gains to an encoder or training recipe, what changes when the decoder is swapped under the same recipe? Using ComplEx and DistMult as the primary controlled pair, with targeted RotatE/TransE spot-checks, we evaluate seven benchmarks. On five standard KGs, ComplEx-vs-DistMult differences are modest but consistent under our recipe (+0.005 to +0.012 MRR), whereas CompGCN-style encoder effects vary more by dataset. On small KGs, decoder effects become the main diagnostic: Kinship shows a stable ComplEx advantage of +0.143 MRR (6 seeds), while UMLS favours ComplEx by +0.022 MRR in a clean 6-seed server rerun but reverses in an earlier provenance variant. We therefore treat small-KG decoder choice as recipe- and provenance-sensitive rather than as a fixed dataset winner. We further show that decoder choice interacts with encoder depth on WN18RR, and that under our recipe L=0 ComplEx on YAGO3-10 reaches 0.6971 +/- 0.0048 MRR at d=128. The result is a compact audit protocol: report matched decoder rows, log small-KG provenance, and sweep decoder x depth before making encoder-level claims.

AI.tldr: RCDA evaluates how decoder choices affect structural transductive KGC by swapping decoders under a fixed recipe; decoder effects are small on standard KGs but significant on small KGs, and the paper proposes a compact audit protocol to disentangle decoder, recipe, and provenance influences.
AI.motivation: To disentangle the contributions of encoders, decoders, training recipes, and provenance in knowledge-graph completion (KGC) and to provide a robust protocol for evaluating decoder impact across datasets of varying size.
AI.method: Compare primary decoders (ComplEx vs DistMult) with targeted RotatE/TransE checks across seven benchmarks; analyze standard KGs (where decoder differences are modest) and small KGs (where decoder effects dominate); examine encoder depth interactions (e.g., WN18RR) and a fixed recipe setting (L=0) on YAGO3-10; use multi-seed evaluations (e.g., 6 seeds) and report MRR differences.
AI.result: On five standard KGs, ComplEx vs DistMult differences are modest but consistent under the fixed recipe (+0.005 to +0.012 MRR); CompGCN-style encoders show dataset-dependent effects. On small KGs, decoder effects dominate: Kinship shows a stable ComplEx advantage of +0.143 MRR (6 seeds); UMLS shows +0.022 MRR in a clean 6-seed run but reverses in an earlier provenance variant. Decoder choice interacts with encoder depth on WN18RR; with L=0 on YAGO3-10, ComplEx reaches 0.6971 +/- 0.0048 MRR at d=128. The paper proposes a compact audit protocol: report matched decoder rows, log provenance, and sweep decoder x depth before encoder-level claims.
AI.conclusion: Decoder choice is recipe- and provenance-sensitive, especially on small KGs. Robust encoder-level claims require accounting for decoder and provenance; RCDA provides a pragmatic protocol to disentangle these factors and guide fair comparisons across datasets and configurations.

title: PepALD: Macrocyclic Peptide Generation via Autoregressive Latent Diffusion
summary: Macrocyclic peptides are promising therapeutic candidates for intracellular targets, but their design requires simultaneous control over non-natural monomer chemistry, ring topology, membrane permeability, and target binding. Existing SMILES- or HELM-string generative models either operate in long atom-level sequence spaces or treat monomers as symbolic tokens with limited chemical grounding. We introduce PepALD, an Autoregressive Latent Diffusion (ALD) foundation model for \textit{de novo} macrocyclic peptide generation. The model represents HELM monomers with structured chemical embeddings, generates each residue through context-conditioned diffusion in chemically informed latent space, predicts R-group-aware ring closures during autoregressive generation, and aligns the denoiser to affinity rewards using winner-protected diffusion-adapted preference optimization. In silico experiments demonstrate PepALD's generation quality and reward-optimization performance against representative peptide generation baselines.

AI.tldr: PepALD is an autoregressive latent diffusion model for de novo macrocyclic peptide generation that uses structured HELM monomer embeddings, diffusion in a chemically informed latent space, and R-group-aware ring closures with diffusion-based reward optimization.
AI.motivation: Designing macrocyclic peptides requires simultaneous control of non-natural monomer chemistry, ring topology, membrane permeability, and target binding. Existing SMILES- or HELM-string generators either operate over long atom-level sequences or treat monomers as symbolic tokens with limited chemical grounding, hindering realistic chemistry.
AI.method: Autoregressive Latent Diffusion (ALD) foundation model. Represent HELM monomers with structured chemical embeddings, generate each residue via context-conditioned diffusion in a chemically informed latent space, predict R-group-aware ring closures during autoregressive generation, and align the denoiser to affinity rewards using winner-protected diffusion-adapted preference optimization.
AI.result: In silico experiments demonstrate PepALD's generation quality and reward-optimization performance against representative peptide generation baselines.
AI.conclusion: PepALD offers a chemically grounded, controllable framework for de novo macrocyclic peptide design, with potential to improve topology, permeability, and binding; future work includes experimental validation and broader benchmarking.

title: Behavioral Audit of Machine Unlearning Has a Privacy Cost
summary: The removal of learned data from Machine Learning models through Machine Unlearning (MU) has been widely studied; however, there has yet to be an agreed-upon scheme for auditing MU. Existing work has shown that a dishonest model owner can falsify evidence to avoid executing MU, while curious auditors (and adversaries) can infer the privacy-sensitive properties of the model and its training data even with limited access. Yet auditing of MU under mutual distrust between the model owner and the auditor remains unexplored. We provide an information-theoretic proof for this scenario: for convex ML models, a generic audit scheme that relies solely on querying the model for \textit{behavioral} signals cannot identify insufficiently unlearned models without revealing membership information of the retained set. Therefore, auditing MU under the assumption of a dishonest model owner and an honest-but-curious auditor faces an inherent privacy-audit tradeoff. Our empirical results on convex models strongly supports this result, while further experiments demonstrate that this privacy-audit tension persists in non-convex models. Our results call for a more careful consideration of the privacy-audit tension under a realistic auditor threat model, and serve as a foundation for more scrutiny of designs of privacy-preserving audit schemes for the MU pipeline. We also release our code implementation at https://github.com/LiouTang/Behavioral-Unlearn-Audit.

AI.tldr: Auditing machine unlearning under mutual distrust is information-theoretically limited: for convex models, any audit relying only on behavioral signals cannot distinguish poorly unlearned models without leaking retained-member information; the privacy-audit tradeoff persists in non-convex models. Empirical results support theory; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of audit schemes under mutual distrust between model owner and auditor; reveal inherent privacy risks in auditing MU.
AI.method: Information-theoretic analysis proving impossibility for convex models; empirical experiments on convex and non-convex models; evaluation of auditing schemes based on behavioral signals; release of code.
AI.result: Impossibility result: cannot identify insufficiently unlearned models without revealing membership of retained data; empirical results confirm privacy-audit tension; non-convex models show similar tension.
AI.conclusion: Need privacy-preserving audit designs and careful threat modeling; this work provides foundation for scrutiny of MU audit schemes; code available.

title: Code Correctness Signals in LLM Hidden States: Pre-Generation Probing and Repair Geometry
comment: 12 pages, 8 tables. Code, data, and analysis scripts available at https://github.com/CarloDiCicco/ReasoningLab
summary: Large language models encode rich information in their hidden states. This work asks whether code correctness is legible in the hidden states of Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, before it generates and as it repairs a failed attempt, studied on 444 LiveCodeBench tasks. It reports two findings connected by a single confound-control tool: residualization. First, the correctness of the model's first-attempt code is linearly decodable from the prompt-final hidden state, with a leakage-free held-out AUC of 0.931 +/- 0.008 across 50 outer splits. After the linear effect of prompt length is removed from each hidden state dimension, the probe still reaches 0.911 +/- 0.010, well above a prompt-length baseline of 0.754 +/- 0.014. Second, on 236 cleaned cases where the model attempts to repair a failed first attempt, the hidden state shift from the failing attempt to its repair carries a statistically detectable contrastive direction, significant on both a magnitude and a split-half test against label-shuffled nulls. This direction does not survive a conditional residualization against repair-context covariates that differ between successful and failed repairs, marking it as a correlate of repair success driven by the repair context rather than an isolated repair-comprehension feature. The probe layer is selected by nested cross-validation, and the same residualization approach that upholds the pre-generation correctness result overturns the repair-direction interpretation. The contribution is as much methodological as empirical: a diagnostic honest enough to report a negative result alongside a positive one.

AI.tldr: Two main findings: (1) first-attempt code correctness is linearly decodable from the prompt-final hidden state with high AUC, robust to confound removal; (2) repair-direction signals in hidden states are not robust after conditioning on repair-context covariates, illustrating context-driven correlations. Methodological emphasis on residualization and nested CV; includes reporting a negative result with a positive one.
AI.motivation: Assess whether code correctness and repair information are encoded in hidden states and develop a robust diagnostic method (residualization) to separate genuine signals from confounds.
AI.method: Probe hidden states with linear classifiers across 444 LiveCodeBench tasks; use residualization to remove prompt-length effects; select probe layer via nested cross-validation; evaluate on held-out splits with AUC; compare first-attempt vs repair attempts; analyze hidden-state shifts during repair; test against label-sh shuffled nulls; apply conditional residualization with repair-context covariates.
AI.result: First-attempt correctness is linearly decodable from the final prompt-hidden state with held-out AUC ≈ 0.931 (50 outer splits); after removing the linear effect of prompt length, AUC remains ≈ 0.911, well above a prompt-length baseline ≈ 0.754. For repairs, the hidden-state shift from failing to repaired attempts shows a detectable direction in magnitude and split-half tests, but this direction is overturned after conditional residualization against repair-context covariates, indicating it reflects repair context rather than isolated repair-comprehension features. Probe layer chosen via nested CV; residualization turns a positive result into a more cautious interpretation, and negative results are reported.
AI.conclusion: The study provides both empirical findings and methodological contribution: residualization is essential to control confounds in probing hidden states; robust first-attempt correctness signals exist in the final prompt state, while purported repair-direction signals appear context-driven and not robust when covariates are accounted for. Emphasizes transparent reporting of negative results alongside positive ones.

title: The Risk Shadow of Principal Component Analysis: When 99.9999% Variance Preservation Causes Catastrophic Decision Errors
comment: 5 tables, 1 figure. all references fully checked manually
summary: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) preserves variance, not the information needed to detect rare catastrophic events. This paper proves the existence of a {\it Risk Shadow}: PCA can retain over 99.9999 percent of total variance while completely erasing all signal about rare, high-impact failures. When this happens, even the best possible classifier operating on the PCA representation reduces to a constant predictor. The root cause is a fundamental mismatch between variance maximization and tail risk awareness. To break the shadow, we introduce Expectile PCA (ExPCA) and Tail-Preserving PCA (TP-PCA), two methods that reweight the data covariance toward high-impact events. We prove theoretically that ExPCA strictly outperforms PCA in retaining rare-event information, and we validate our claims on synthetic data and a real-world credit card fraud detection benchmark. Our results call for a fundamental rethinking of variance-based dimensionality reduction in high-stakes decisions.

AI.tldr: ExPCA and TP-PCA mitigate the PCA 'risk shadow' by reweighting covariance to emphasize high-impact, tail events, providing theoretical and empirical advantages over standard PCA for rare-event detection.
AI.motivation: PCA maximizes overall variance and can erase tail information crucial for rare, high-impact events; this mismatch undermines decision-making in high-stakes domains.
AI.method: Introduce two tail-aware PCA variants—Expectile PCA (ExPCA) and Tail-Preserving PCA (TP-PCA)—that reweight the data covariance toward high-impact events; establish theoretical guarantees that ExPCA preserves more rare-event information than PCA; validate on synthetic data and a real-world credit card fraud benchmark.
AI.result: Theoretical results show ExPCA strictly outperforms PCA in retaining rare-event information; empirical validation on synthetic data and a credit-card fraud benchmark supports improved preservation of tail signals and detection performance.
AI.conclusion: Variance-based dimensionality reduction should be rethought in high-stakes settings; tail-preserving methods like ExPCA/TP-PCA are recommended to avoid the risk shadow and better retain rare-event information.

title: CARE: Controlling LLM-Generated Policies through Auditable Review of Evidence in Scientific Experimentation
summary: Granting LLMs direct control over costly, irreversible scientific experiments leads to unsafe exploration and unstable performance, but discarding LLM creativity entirely sacrifices significant optimization potential. We introduce CARE (Controlling LLM-Generated Policies through Auditable Review of Evidence in Scientific Experimentation), an auditable controller for high-throughput experimentation (HTE) optimization that keeps a non-LLM incumbent optimizer as the default action path while using LLMs to revise challenger ranking policies. Before each outcome is revealed, a public-evidence intervention gate compares the challenger with the incumbent. It authorizes the challenger's selection only when the evidence available before selection supports the change, with the decision recorded in the audit log. CARE outperforms all other evaluated methods on Minerva/Olympus and ChemLex benchmarks, with final-best improving from 80.0 to 88.5 on Minerva/Olympus and from 83.9 to 92.1 on ChemLex, relative to the public incumbent. Our experiments indicate that LLM self-evolution is more reliable when it expands the proposal space under an auditable controller, rather than directly choosing experiments.

AI.tldr: CARE is an auditable controller that allows LLMs to revise challenger policies in high-throughput experimentation while defaulting to a non-LLM incumbent. An evidence gate before outcomes decides whether to accept challengers, logging decisions for audit. It yields improved final-best performance on Minerva/Olympus and ChemLex and suggests LLM self-evolution is more reliable under auditable control than direct experiment selection.
AI.motivation: To reconcile LLM-powered optimization with the safety and irreversibility concerns of costly experiments by preserving a non-LLM incumbent as the default path and using LLMs only to adjust challenger rankings under auditable review.
AI.method: Introduce CARE: an auditable controller with a public-evidence intervention gate that compares challenger and incumbent before outcome revelation. The system authorizes the challenger's selection only when pre-outcome evidence supports the change, recording the decision in an audit log. LLMs revise challenger ranking policies while a non-LLM incumbent handles default actions, tested on Minerva/Olympus and ChemLex benchmarks.
AI.result: CARE outperforms other methods on the benchmarks, with final-best metrics improving from 80.0 to 88.5 on Minerva/Olympus and from 83.9 to 92.1 on ChemLex relative to the public incumbent.
AI.conclusion: An auditable controller enables beneficial LLM-guided optimization while maintaining safety; expanding the proposal space under auditable governance leads to more reliable LLM self-evolution than direct experimental selection.

title: Zero-shot generalization of transformer neural operators to larger domains
summary: Transformer-based neural operators have shown remarkable performance for approximating solution operators of partial differential equations on complex geometries. However, existing approaches implicitly assume a fixed domain size, which limits their ability to generalize at inference. In this work, we investigate domain extension, namely zero-shot inference on spatial domains that are significantly larger than those encountered during training. We argue that this setting fundamentally requires spatial locality and translation equivariance. We propose to implement this locality via a decomposable bias in the attention logits computation, enabling finely controllable locality while remaining fully decomposable into query-key inner products and directly compatible with optimized attention kernels. Combined with rotary positional embeddings, it enables expressive embeddings with controllable spatial support without altering the transformer architecture. We empirically show that our approach substantially improves zero-shot generalization to larger domains across two PDE benchmarks and a 3D industrial atmospheric flow application. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/cerea-daml/domain-extension.

AI.tldr: Proposes a decomposable local bias in transformer attention plus rotary embeddings to enable zero-shot extension to larger spatial domains for transformer-based neural operators solving PDEs, improving cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Current PDE neural operators using transformers assume fixed domain sizes, hindering generalization to larger geometries. Domain extension requires locality and translation equivariance to function across varying domains.
AI.method: Implement a decomposable bias added to attention logits to enforce spatial locality; this bias is separable into query-key inner products, keeping compatibility with optimized attention kernels. Combine with rotary positional embeddings to create expressive spatial embeddings with controllable locality, without changing transformer architecture.
AI.result: Empirically show substantial improvements in zero-shot generalization to larger domains on two PDE benchmarks and a 3D industrial atmospheric flow application; code/datasets available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Locality-enforcing bias plus rotary embeddings provide a simple, architecture-preserving way to achieve domain-size generalization for transformer-based neural operators in PDEs.

title: Realizing Native INT8 Compute for Diffusion Transformers on Consumer GPUs: A Fused INT8 GEMM Kernel for Ideogram 4.0
summary: Post-training INT8 (W8A8) quantization of diffusion transformers is widely deployed as a speed optimization, yet on consumer Ampere GPUs it is frequently slower than the FP8 and NF4 alternatives it is meant to beat. We trace this to a software artifact: the production "INT8" forward quantizes weights and activations only to immediately dequantize them back to bf16 and run a bf16 matrix multiply, never engaging the GPU's INT8 tensor cores, so the hardware's compute advantage is left entirely unrealized. We close this gap with a single fused Triton INT8 GEMM (int8xint8->int32 on Ampere tensor cores, with per-token x per-channel dequantization and bias folded into the epilogue, autotuned per GEMM shape) dropped into the Ideogram 4.0 diffusion transformer's linear layers in place of the dequantize-to-bf16 path. In the kernel, the int8xint8->int32 accumulation is bit-exact against torch._int_mm and the dequantized output matches the reference at cosine similarity 1.0 with no NaNs, running 2.8-4.2x faster than bf16 per GEMM. End to end it delivers a ~1.1x (~9-10%) speedup at 768px, and at 1024px it generates an image in 156.5 s on a single RTX 3090, faster than the single-card NF4 (164.5 s) and FP8 (172.9 s) baselines, at no measurable quality cost on these point estimates (PickScore/CLIPScore). INT8 thus goes from the slowest variant to the fastest, and 1024px becomes single-GPU feasible. The primary speed criterion (beat FP8, by ~9.5%) is comfortably met; the NF4 margin (~4.9%, single-run n=4) is within run-to-run variance we did not quantify and is best read as consistent with meeting the stretch target. We close with an honest deployment map: the win is specific to consumer Ampere, and on A100 and B200 the same kernel loses to those cards' fast native bf16/FP8 paths.

AI.tldr: A fused INT8 GEMM kernel for diffusion transformers on Ampere GPUs delivers real INT8 speedups, enabling 1024px single-GPU generation and beating FP8 by ~9–10%, with results hardware-specific to consumer Ampere.
AI.motivation: Fix the hardware-underutilized INT8 path in post-training quantization where INT8 forward passes degrade to bf16, preventing gains from tensor cores; enable an actual INT8 path that uses fast AMPERE INT8 matrix-multiply.
AI.method: Introduce a single fused Triton kernel performing int8xint8->int32 accumulation with per-token x per-channel dequantization and bias folded into the epilogue, autotuned per GEMM shape, inserted into Ideogram 4.0’s linear layers in place of dequantize-to-bf16. Kernel is bit-exact to torch._int_mm and outputs match the reference (cosine similarity 1.0, no NaNs).
AI.result: 2.8–4.2× faster per GEMM than bf16. End-to-end ~9–10% speedup at 768px. At 1024px on an RTX 3090, 156.5 s per image, faster than NF4 (164.5 s) and FP8 (172.9 s) with no measurable quality loss (PickScore/CLIPScore). INT8 becomes the fastest path and enables 1024px single-GPU feasibility; NF4 margin is within run-to-run variance (~4.9%).
AI.conclusion: The INT8 path on consumer Ampere unlocks real speedups for diffusion transformers, but performance is architecture-specific (not superior on A100/B200). This maps a deployment guideline: leverage INT8 on Ampere for 1024px generation, while preferring bf16/FP8 on A100/B200.

title: A Statistical and Machine Learning Framework for Operational Threshold Detection and Deployable Dispatch Controller Development in Hydrogen Multi-Energy Systems
summary: This study presents a statistical and machine learning framework for characterizing a hydrogen-based multi-energy system (H-MES) using one year of high-resolution operational data. Statistical analysis revealed a binary operation driven by renewable surplus, with solar irradiance explaining 45.7% of rank-based variance in hydrogen production, a large effect by conventional standards. Only high-irradiance periods triggered meaningful electrolyzer engagement, while electricity demand exerted a weaker inverse suppression effect ($ε^2 = 0.126$). Multiple regression confirmed electrolyzer power as the dominant linear predictor, with a synergistic solar-wind interaction. Notably, Random Forest analysis ranked wind output first in predictive importance despite its weak bivariate correlation (r = 0.167), revealing non-linear dynamics invisible to parametric methods. A sequence model exploited strong 24-hour autocorrelation (r = 0.845) for operational forecasting, while a reinforcement learning agent optimized hydrogen revenue dispatch. The core contribution is demonstrating that statistical and machine learning approaches are complementary for H-MES modeling and control.

AI.tldr: A data-driven framework combining statistical methods and machine learning to model and control a hydrogen-based multi-energy system (H-MES) using one year of high-resolution data; key findings include solar irradiance explaining 45.7% of rank-based variance in hydrogen production, high irradiance triggering electrolyzer operation, wind's predictive importance despite weak linear correlation, strong 24-hour autocorrelation for forecasting, and reinforcement learning for revenue-optimal dispatch; overall, the work argues for complementary use of statistics and ML in H-MES modeling and control.
AI.motivation: Characterize and optimize H-MES performance under renewable variability by leveraging both traditional statistics and modern ML to capture linear and nonlinear relationships, forecast short-term dynamics, and inform dispatch decisions.
AI.method: Statistical analysis (rank-based variance, regression) to identify main predictors; multiple regression with interaction terms; Random Forest to capture nonlinear relationships and variable importance; a sequence model to exploit 24-hour autocorrelation for operational forecasting; reinforcement learning agent to optimize hydrogen revenue dispatch; all applied to one year of high-resolution operational data.
AI.result: Solar irradiance explains 45.7% of rank-based variance in hydrogen production. Electrolyzer engagement occurs mainly during high irradiance periods. Electricity demand shows a weaker inverse effect (ε^2 = 0.126). Electrolyzer power is the dominant linear predictor with a solar–wind interaction. RF identifies wind output as highly predictive despite a low bivariate correlation (r = 0.167). A sequence model captures strong 24-hour autocorrelation (r = 0.845) for forecasting. A RL agent achieves revenue-oriented hydrogen dispatch optimization. Conclusion: statistics and ML are complementary for H-MES modeling and control.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a complementary framework where traditional statistical methods uncover linear and interaction effects, ML methods reveal nonlinear dynamics and important predictors that evade linear analysis, and sequential/RL approaches provide forecasting and dispatch optimization, collectively enhancing H-MES understanding and operation.

title: A Comparative Study of Deep Learning Architectures for Multi-Horizon Behavioural Forecasting for Mobile Health
summary: Wearable devices and smartphones generate rich behavioural time series that can support proactive health interventions, yet systematic comparisons of modern forecasting architectures for these data are lacking. In particular, it remains unclear how models generalise across populations, how different architectures respond to participant-level fine-tuning and how forecasting accuracy degrades across multi-day horizons. We benchmark six deep learning architectures, two zero-shot Foundation Models (FM) and statistical baselines on three public datasets encompassing over 800 participants, reporting per-feature metrics for step counts, screen time and sleep duration across 1-8 day horizons. We further conduct a per-feature personalisation study across all six architectures and assess FM transferability across dataset sizes and temporal granularities. Our key findings are: (i) no single architecture dominates, PatchTST leads among trained models while the three runners-up (TCN, MLP, Transformer) show no meaningful performance difference; (ii) the FM TimesFM matches or exceeds trained models zero-shot, especially in low-data regimes and (iii) participant-level fine-tuning reduces per-feature RMSE by 16-60\%, with sleep benefiting most and step counts least. These results provide practical guidance on architecture selection, FM applicability and personalisation strategies for mobile health forecasting. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to jointly evaluate modern deep learning, FMs and personalisation for multi-horizon behavioural forecasting from wearables.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark of modern forecasting architectures (six DL models, two Foundation Models, and baselines) for multi-horizon wearables data across 3 datasets; finds no universal best, with PatchTST strongest among trained models, TimesFM competitive zero-shot, and personalized fine-tuning substantially reducing RMSE.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of systematic comparisons among modern forecasting architectures for behavioural wearable time series, assess cross-population generalization, the impact of participant-level fine-tuning, and degradation across multi-day horizons.
AI.method: Benchmark six deep-learning architectures, two zero-shot Foundation Models (FM) and statistical baselines on three public datasets (800+ participants). Evaluate per-feature metrics for step counts, screen time, and sleep duration across 1–8 day horizons. Conduct per-feature personalization across all models and assess FM transferability across dataset sizes and temporal granularities.
AI.result: No single architecture dominates; PatchTST is best among trained models, with TCN, MLP, and Transformer close behind. TimesFM (FM) matches or exceeds trained models in zero-shot, especially in low-data regimes. Participant-level fine-tuning reduces per-feature RMSE by 16–60%, with sleep improving most and steps least.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidance on selecting architectures, applying foundation models, and using personalization for mobile-health forecasting; it is the first to jointly evaluate modern DL, FMs, and personalization for multi-horizon behavioural forecasting from wearables.

title: Expert-Driven Survival Machines: Improving Stratification and Interpretability in Multiple Clinical Cohorts
summary: Survival prediction plays a central role for healthcare providers and clinical researchers. Accurate risk stratification enables early intervention and improved patient management. Most existing deep survival models learn one common feature representation for all patients, which may hide important differences between patient subgroups. In contrast, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework allows different parts of the model to focus on different patient patterns, leading to more individualized representations. Therefore, in this work, we propose a mixture-of-experts enhanced adaptive deep clustering survival framework (AdaCSM) for modeling such heterogeneous survival patterns. We introduce a routing-based expert mechanism that enables conditional specialization within a parametric survival modeling framework. The proposed architecture allocates patients to specialized risk predictors dynamically while preserving the patient survival and subtype clustering objectives. We compare our method with state-of-the-art survival and deep clustering models on multiple real-world longitudinal clinical cohorts spanning diverse disease domains. The proposed method demonstrates improved predictive performance and leads to interpretable results in survival analysis.

AI.tldr: AdaCSM uses a Mixture-of-Experts with routing to capture patient subgroups for survival prediction, improving predictive accuracy and interpretability across cohorts.
AI.motivation: Survival models often learn a single shared representation, which can mask important heterogeneity across patient subgroups. A Mixture-of-Experts enables conditional specialization to better model diverse survival patterns.
AI.method: Introduce a routing-based expert mechanism within a parametric survival framework (AdaCSM) that dynamically assigns patients to specialized risk predictors while optimizing both survival prediction andSubtype clustering objectives.
AI.result: The method shows improved predictive performance compared to state-of-the-art survival and deep clustering models across multiple real-world longitudinal cohorts and yields interpretable survival patterns.
AI.conclusion: Mixture-of-Experts with adaptive deep clustering enhances heterogeneity modeling in survival analysis, enabling better risk stratification and interpretability across diverse disease domains.

title: Neither Parallel Nor Sequential: How DiffusionGemma Actually Commits Tokens
summary: Open diffusion language models are marketed as parallel, non-autoregressive decoders, yet the order in which a shipped checkpoint actually commits its tokens is almost never measured. We instrument DiffusionGemma 26B, a masked discrete-diffusion mixture-of-experts model built on Gemma 4, hooking its sampler's accept step to record which canvas positions commit, when, and at what confidence. Across a 686-prompt, six-regime probe suite we find that its decoding is neither parallel nor block-autoregressive: it follows a partial left-to-right commit bias whose apparent strength depends almost entirely on the granularity at which you look. Order is weak token by token and strengthens smoothly as the analysis is coarsened, so the model's "block size" turns out to be an artifact of the measuring ruler rather than the architecture. The model commits in large simultaneous batches, leaving much of the within-batch order genuinely undefined rather than merely unobserved. The behaviour is regime-dependent: structured JSON is committed in essentially arbitrary order, and a position's commit confidence tracks correctness on mathematical reasoning but carries no signal on factual recall. Commitment is aggressive, finishing in a short late burst well inside the step budget, while task accuracy matches the model's autoregressive Gemma-4 sibling. Beyond these findings, our central contribution is methodological: measuring decoding order honestly demands handling trailing-EOS padding, within-regime confounding, commit non-monotonicity, block-size sensitivity, and large commit-batch ties, each of which can otherwise manufacture a decoding-order result that is not really there.

AI.tldr: Decoding order in a masked diffusion LM is neither truly parallel nor strictly block-autoregressive; apparent order signals depend on measurement granularity and large, co-occurring commit batches.
AI.motivation: Open diffusion LMs are marketed as parallel decoders, yet real decoding order is rarely measured. The study seeks to empirically quantify how a shipped model commits tokens and to distinguish genuine order from measurement artifacts.
AI.method: Instrument DiffusionGemma 26B by hooking the sampler accept step to log commit positions, times, and confidence. Evaluate 686 prompts across six regimes; analyze commit order across multiple granularity levels; address trailing-EOS padding, within-regime confounding, non-monotonicity, block-size sensitivity, and commit-batch ties.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) decoding is neither parallel nor strictly block-autoregressive; a partial left-to-right commit bias exists, its strength depends on granularity; coarse analysis reveals stronger apparent order, so block size is an artifact of measurement. (2) Commits occur in large simultaneous batches; intra-batch token order is often undefined. (3) Regime dependence: structured JSON commits in essentially arbitrary order. (4) Commit confidence tracks correctness on mathematical reasoning but not factual recall. (5) Commitment is aggressive, finishing quickly within the step budget, with task accuracy matching the autoregressive Gemma-4. (6) Central contribution is methodological: measuring decoding order honestly requires careful handling of trailing-EOS padding, within-regime confounding, non-monotonicity, block-size sensitivity, and commit-batch ties to avoid spurious results.
AI.conclusion: The study’s main contribution is methodological, showing that decoding order must be measured with robust controls; naive claims about parallelism or block autoregression can be artifacts of measurement. The findings urge careful evaluation of decoding dynamics in diffusion LMs and suggest that order is regime- and granularity-dependent rather than architectural.

title: Graph Diffusion Residuals for Control-Function Instrumental Variables
comment: Submitted to Journal of Machine Learning Research (JMLR). 50 pages, 6 figures
summary: Control-function instrumental variable estimators need a first-stage residual, not merely a first-stage prediction. High-capacity first stages can interpolate treatment and leave too little residual information for the outcome equation. We study Adaptive Anisotropic Instrumental Heat Flow (A-IHF), a deterministic graph-diffusion residual extractor for flexible control functions. A-IHF treats treatment as a signal on a graph of first-stage features, uses pilot diffusion to detect large treatment jumps, attenuates conductance across those jumps, and computes the generated control with a sparse graph resolvent. Its observational selection rule uses only $(Z,X)$, combining graph generalized cross-validation, roughness, residualized-treatment relevance, and graph-admissibility filtering. The analysis decomposes error into structural leakage, residual attenuation, and residualized treatment variation, yielding finite-sample bounds, graph-admissibility rates under latent piecewise-smooth geometry, and finite-path selection calibration. Across 54 synthetic benchmark cells with tuned graph, kernel, tree, boosting, series, and neural control-function baselines, guarded observational A-IHF has the lowest average structural-response MSE; the A-IHF family beats the best non-A-IHF baseline in 32 cells. Performance is strongest when the graph captures piecewise-smooth first-stage structure.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Anisotropic Instrumental Heat Flow (A-IHF) is a deterministic graph-diffusion residual extractor for control-function IVs, designed to preserve informative residuals by modeling the first-stage as a graph and attenuating large treatment jumps. It yields finite-sample bounds and shows guarded A-IHF outperforms baselines on synthetic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: First-stage residuals are required for control-function IVs, but high-capacity first stages can interpolate treatment, leaving little residual information for the outcome equation. A robust residual extractor is needed to retain relevant variation for the outcome while controlling leakage.
AI.method: Treat treatment as a signal on a graph of first-stage features. Use pilot diffusion to detect large treatment jumps, attenuate conductance across jumps, and compute the generated control via a sparse graph resolvent. Observational selection uses (Z,X) with graph generalized cross-validation, roughness, residualized-treatment relevance, and graph-admissibility filtering.
AI.result: Decomposes error into structural leakage, residual attenuation, and residualized treatment variation; provides finite-sample bounds, graph-admissibility rates under latent piecewise-smooth geometry, and finite-path selection calibration. In 54 synthetic benchmark cells, guarded observational A-IHF achieves the lowest average structural-response MSE; the A-IHF family outperforms the best non-A-IHF baseline in 32 cells.
AI.conclusion: Performance is strongest when the graph captures piecewise-smooth first-stage structure; the A-IHF family offers robust, improved control-function performance in settings with structured first-stage signals, validated by extensive synthetic benchmarks.

title: Online Convex Optimization with Sublinear Noisy Probes
summary:
We study Online Convex Optimization (OCO) over a convex set $K\subseteq \mathbb R^d$, where in each round $t$ the learner selects $x_t\in K$ and then observes a convex loss $f_t:K\to[0,1]$, with the goal of minimizing regret to the best fixed decision in hindsight. We introduce a unified probing model that generalizes two recent lines of work: sublinear best-expert queries in the experts setting, and pairwise (comparison-based) feedback available every round in OCO. In our framework, the learner has a budget of $k\le T$ pairwise probes; on a probed round it may query two points and learn which one has smaller loss.
  Our main result shows that even a sublinear and noisy probe budget can provably improve worst-case regret in the full feedback OCO regime. With $k$ $δ$-noisy pairwise probes, we obtain: $ \text{Reg}_T \le O\left(\min\left\{\sqrt{dT\ln T},\; \frac{dT\ln T}{k|1-2δ|}\right\}\right) $, which is tight (up to logarithmic factors in $T$) across $T$, $k$ and $δ$. Specifically regarding the noise parameter $δ\in [0,1]$, the regret guarantee smoothly degrades as the oracle response approaches a coin flip, i.e., $δ$ is close to $\frac{1}{2}$. When applying the same techniques to a finite $K$ for the prediction with $d$ experts setting, the resulting rates are instead completely tight in all parameters, including $d$.
  Our analysis gives a streamlined treatment of pairwise probing in OCO by quantifying the benefit of probing via a variance reduction effect, combined with a second-order (variance-based) analysis of Continuous Exponential Weights.
