AI.tldr: Unified probing model for online convex optimization with k noisy pairwise probes yields improved regret bounds in full-feedback OCO; bounds transition between sqrt(dT log T) and dT log T /(k|1-2δ|), tight up to log factors; noise degrades gracefully as δ→1/2; results extend to finite K with d experts, achieving fully tight rates.
AI.motivation: Join two recent strands—sublinear best-expert queries and pairwise feedback in OCO—by quantifying how a limited budget of pairwise probes reduces variance and improves regret.
AI.method: Define a unified probing model with k pairwise probes per T rounds, each probe providing a δ-noisy comparison. Analyze using variance reduction in pairwise querying combined with a second-order (variance-based) analysis of Continuous Exponential Weights. Derive regret bounds for OCO with full feedback under probing; extend to finite expert setting (d experts, K finite).
AI.result: Reg_T ≤ O(min{ sqrt(d T ln T), (d T ln T)/(k |1-2δ|) }). Bound is tight up to log factors across T, k, δ. Noise δ near 1/2 degrades performance smoothly. For finite K and d experts, rates are completely tight in all parameters including d.
AI.conclusion: Probing—even with sublinear, noisy budgets—provably improves worst-case regret in OCO and can be tightly characterized. The framework yields a streamlined, variance-reduction-based analysis for Continuous Exponential Weights and clarifies the benefit of probing across settings.

title: Which Directions Matter? Sparse Design for Affine Robust Optimization
summary: Robust machine learning and optimization rely on the uncertainty model choice. We investigate which uncertainty directions a model must cover when defined by a finite dictionary and a budget constraint. Selecting a subset forms an atomic uncertainty set with a closed form support function, yielding tractable robust programs for affine objectives. We propose a data driven selection rule based on a coverage objective over evaluation directions, including gradients, adversarial perturbations, or shifts observed on held out data. We prove this objective is monotone and submodular, supporting a greedy method with a $(1-1/e)$ approximation guarantee and a matching hardness barrier. We also provide a certificate bounding the loss from the selected subset and a radius calibration rule with out of sample control.

AI.tldr: Select a finite dictionary of uncertainty directions under a budget to form an atomic uncertainty set with a closed-form support function, enabling tractable robust programs for affine objectives. A data-driven coverage-based selection rule over evaluation directions is shown to be monotone and submodular, enabling a greedy (1-1/e)-approximation with matching hardness. The work also provides a certificate for loss bounds and a radius calibration rule with out-of-sample control.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty modeling in robust ML and optimization depends on direction choices; with finite dictionaries and budgets, one must choose directions that cover essential perturbations while preserving tractability. A principled, data-driven selection rule is needed to ensure performance guarantees.
AI.method: Define a finite dictionary of uncertainty directions and an uncertainty budget to create an atomic uncertainty set with a closed-form support function. Propose a data-driven coverage objective over evaluation directions (e.g., gradients, adversarial perturbations, shifts on held-out data). Prove the objective is monotone and submodular, enabling a greedy algorithm with a (1-1/e) approximation and a matching hardness bound. Provide a certificate bounding the loss from the selected subset and a radius calibration rule with out-of-sample control.
AI.result: The coverage objective is proven monotone and submodular, yielding a greedy algorithm with a (1-1/e) approximation and a hardness barrier. A certificate bounds the loss from the selected subset and a radius calibration rule ensures out-of-sample control.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields tractable robust programs for affine objectives under a finite, data-driven uncertainty dictionary, with rigorous guarantees on selection quality, loss bounds, and calibration. It bridges uncertainty modeling with practical, provable selection rules and certificates.

title: Graph Structured Combinatorial Semi-Bandit with Nonlinear Reward Associations through Separable Signals
summary: The identification of optimal structures within vast arrays of interconnected data necessitates significant sampling- and computational effort. Learning and leveraging underlying signal dependencies can improve efficiency and predictive capabilities considerably, but the ubiquity of nonlinear statistical relations amplifies the complexity of such undertakings. In this paper, we develop novel generic and adaptive strategies equipped with routines for graph-based causal reward modeling, analytic reproducing kernel methods, and Taylor approximation of functional processes. We establish theoretical performance guarantees sublinear in time and linear in data volume over time. Our analyses cover robustness to a multitude of uncertainties arising from noise interference, gradual model convergence, and solution space mismatch. The framework's general appeal is substantiated by a minimalistic set of conditions or reliance on prior estimates, while various outlined modifications address specific or extended settings. To demonstrate practical effectiveness, we conduct numerical experiments using both benchmarked synthetic and real-world transportation datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces adaptable graph-based causal reward framework using reproducing kernel methods and Taylor approximations; proves sublinear time complexity with linear data growth; demonstrates robustness to noise, convergence, and model-mismatch; validated on synthetic and transportation datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the high computational and sampling burden of identifying optimal structures in large, interconnected systems with nonlinear dependencies by exploiting underlying signal relationships to improve efficiency and predictive performance.
AI.method: Propose generic, adaptive strategies with routines for graph-based causal reward modeling, analytic reproducing kernel methods, and Taylor approximations of functional processes; provide theoretical performance guarantees and robustness analyses; outline minimal conditions and adaptable extensions.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees: sublinear time complexity and linear scaling with data volume; robustness to noise, slow model convergence, and solution space mismatch; empirical validation on synthetic benchmarks and real-world transportation data demonstrating practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: A broadly applicable framework with minimal assumptions that effectively enables efficient, robust structure learning in large graphs; flexible to extensions and domain settings, with demonstrated practical impact on transportation datasets.

title: Flood and Harvest: The Provable Necessity of Trivia for Generating Valuable Mathematics via the Lens of Language Generation in the Limit
summary: AI systems coupled to proof assistants now generate formal mathematics at scale, and the gap between what a checker can verify and what a mathematician would value has become the binding constraint. We model the generation of valuable mathematics as nested language generation in the limit: a verifiable formal language $F$, accessed through a membership oracle (the proof checker), contains an unknown valuable language $H \in \mathcal{H}$ revealed only through an adversarial enumeration of a core $C \subseteq H$ of exact density $α$ (the literature). Every output is valuable ($\in H$), trivial ($\in F \setminus H$), or a hallucination ($\notin F$). We settle four questions. First, the verifier is not taste: the collections admitting generation with breadth are exactly those of the oracle-free model, characterized fiber-wise by Angluin's condition. Second, the verifier does buy sound coverage, covering all unseen valuable statements while asserting only valid ones: possible with it, impossible without it; it relocates unavoidable errors from false to trivial. Third, and centrally, a sharp dichotomy on the tight family: generators emitting finitely many trivia achieve optimal coverage $α/2$, while any infinite trivia allowance, even at vanishing rate, jumps the optimum to $1-α/2$ (both tight, for cores presented as the candidate intersection), and one generator attains both ends. The transition is in trivia count, not rate; the gap $1-α$ is the unrecorded mass. Fourth, both regimes instantiate in a compression model of mathematics. A perfect verifier cannot substitute for taste: the unbounded stream of correct-but-worthless statements is not an engineering accident but a provable necessity, since covering unrecorded valuable mathematics requires an infinite, but asymptotically negligible, stream of certified trivia.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for generating valuable formal mathematics via a verifiable language; it reveals a four-part landscape: verifier semantics, coverage capabilities, a dichotomy in generator behavior (finite vs infinite trivia), and a compression-based necessity of trivia streams for capturing unrecorded valuable math.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between what proof-checkers can verify and the value mathematicians seek in formal mathematics; understand limits of AI-assisted formalization and the role of verification in discovery.
AI.method: A theoretical model of nested language generation where a verifiable formal language F is accessed through a membership oracle (the checker). An unknown valuable language H ⊆ F is revealed through an adversarial enumeration of a core C ⊆ H of exact density α. Outputs are categorized as valuable (in H), trivial (in F\H), or hallucinations (not in F). The analysis uses Angluin-style characterization and a compression perspective on mathematical content.
AI.result: 1) Verifier is not taste: breadth-admitting collections coincide with the oracle-free model, characterized fiber-wise by Angluin's condition. 2) Verifier can achieve sound coverage: all unseen valuable statements can be covered while only valid ones are asserted; without verifiers this is impossible; errors can be relocated from false to trivial. 3) Dichotomy on tight families: generators with finitely many trivia achieve optimal coverage α/2; allowing infinite trivia raises optimum to 1−α/2 (tight for cores as candidate intersection), with a single generator attaining both ends; the transition depends on trivia count, not rate, with unrecorded mass 1−α. 4) The regimes align with a compression model of mathematics: a perfect verifier cannot replace taste; to cover unrecorded valuable mathematics requires an infinite but asymptotically vanishing stream of certified trivia.
AI.conclusion: A perfect verifier cannot substitute for mathematical taste. Capturing unrecorded valuable mathematics necessitates an ongoing, unbounded stream of certified trivia, even if its rate vanishes, highlighting a provable trade-off between verification and discovery.

title: Beyond task performance: Decoding bioacoustic embeddings with speech features
summary: Pretrained audio embeddings are standard in bioacoustics, yet little is known about which acoustic features these models encode, nor which are useful for a given task. This hinders transparency and limits extension to rare species or data-scarce domains. Here we reveal which speech-like features are encoded in bioacoustic representations. Using the 88~eGeMAPS features across six taxonomic groups, we apply linear and nonlinear regression probes to quantify which acoustic properties each model captures. Results confirm a ``no free lunch'' pattern: no single model captures the full feature space. A concatenated embedding achieves the highest performance, suggesting complementary acoustic space coverage across models. Loudness features are best encoded ($R^2 = 0.76$) while F0 is hardest to recover ($R^2 = 0.33$). By cross-referencing recoverability with per-species feature salience (NMI), we derive data-driven model selection guidance for bioacoustics.

AI.tldr: No single pretrained bioacoustic embedding captures all 88 speech-like features; concatenating multiple models yields best performance due to complementary feature coverage. Loudness is best encoded, F0 is the hardest to recover. The study provides data-driven guidance for model selection via feature recoverability and salience (NMI).
AI.motivation: To increase transparency of what acoustic features pretrained bioacoustic embeddings encode and to enable effective transfer to data-scarce domains and rare species.
AI.method: Use the 88 eGeMAPS features across six taxonomic groups. Apply linear and nonlinear regression probes to quantify which acoustic properties each model captures. Assess feature recoverability (R^2) and relate it to per-species feature salience via Normalized Mutual Information (NMI). Compare individual models and a concatenated embedding.
AI.result: No single model covers the full feature space (no free lunch). A concatenated embedding achieves the highest performance, indicating complementary coverage across models. Loudness features are best encoded (R^2 = 0.76) while F0 is the most challenging to recover (R^2 = 0.33). Cross-referencing recoverability with feature salience (NMI) yields data-driven guidance for selecting models by task and species.
AI.conclusion: The work provides practical guidance for model selection in bioacoustics, advocating model concatenation for maximum feature-space coverage and proposing data-driven criteria (recoverability and NMI salience) to tailor model choice to specific taxa and tasks.

title: When to Write and When to Suppress: Route-Specialized Dual Adapters for Memory-Assisted Knowledge Editing
summary: Knowledge editing systems must update selected facts while preserving nearby but irrelevant behavior. This paper studies this problem in a memory-assisted setting where an edit memory is retrieved at inference time and a parameter-efficient adapter corrects the model's object preference. We argue that the central design question is not only how to write an edit, but also when to suppress it. We introduce \method{}, a route-specialized dual-adapter editor. A relevance router first decides whether a prompt should receive an edit memory. Routed prompts use an edit adapter trained to prefer the new object over the original object; unrouted non-direct prompts use a separate locality adapter trained to preserve or restore the original-object preference. We evaluate \method{} on three 1,000-case protocols, \cf{}, \zsre{}, and \mquake{}, under the same memory protocol and two 7B/8B base models. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, \method{} obtains the best overall probability-preference accuracy on all three benchmarks: 0.8180 on \cf{}, 0.8946 on \zsre{}, and 0.9922 on \mquake{}. The same trend holds on Qwen3-8B. Router ablations show that the relevant memory boundary differs across datasets: a lexical neural router is safest on \cf{}, while BGE embedding routing is better on \zsre{} and \mquake{}. Component and module ablations show that the gain mainly comes from separating edit injection from off-route suppression rather than from simply increasing LoRA capacity.

AI.tldr: Introduces route-specialized dual-adapter editor (R-SDAE) for memory-assisted knowledge editing. A relevance router gates prompts to an edit path; an edit adapter enforces the new object, while a locality adapter preserves the original when not edited. Demonstrates state-of-the-art probability-preference accuracy across three benchmarks and two models, highlighting that separating edit injection from off-route suppression—not adapter size—in drives gains.
AI.motivation: Knowledge-editing systems must update facts while preserving nearby, irrelevant behavior. In memory-assisted settings, a key challenge is deciding when to apply an edit; erroneous suppression can hinder necessary updates and over-application can harm unrelated behavior. The paper investigates whether a routing-based mechanism to gate edits can improve fidelity and locality of edits.
AI.method: Propose R-SDAE: a route-specialized dual-adapter editor. A relevance router decides if a prompt should receive edit memory. Routed prompts use an edit adapter trained to prefer the new object over the original. Unrouted prompts (non-direct) use a locality adapter trained to preserve or restore the original-object preference. Evaluations on three 1,000-case protocols (C4, ZsRE, MQuake) using 7B/8B base models, with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen3-8B as targets. Ablations compare router types and attribute that gains come from separation of edit-injection and suppression rather than just increasing adapter capacity (LoRA).
AI.result: On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, R-SDAE achieves best overall probability-preference accuracy: 0.8180 on C4, 0.8946 on ZsRE, 0.9922 on MQuake. Similar improvements observed on Qwen3-8B. Router ablations reveal dataset-specific memory boundaries: lexical neural routing is safest for C4, while BGE embedding routing outperforms on ZsRE and MQuake. Component ablations indicate the principal gains arise from dividing edit injection and off-route suppression, not merely expanding LoRA capacity.
AI.conclusion: The central design insight is that when to suppress an edit is as important as how to inject it. By routing prompts to either an edit path or a preservation path, the method effectively localizes edits and reduces off-target changes. Memory-boundaries appear dataset-dependent, suggesting future work to tailor routing strategies per domain. Overall, architectural separation of edit-injection and suppression yields robust gains across models and benchmarks.

title: Compressed Computation is (probably) not Computation in Superposition
comment: Presented at the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at NeurIPS 2025
summary: We study whether the Compressed Computation (CC) toy model (Braun et al., 2025) is an instance of computation in superposition. The CC model appears to compute 100 ReLU functions with just 50 neurons, achieving a better loss than expected from only representing 50 ReLU functions. We show that the model mixes inputs via its noisy residual stream, corresponding to an unintended mixing matrix in the labels. Splitting the training objective into the ReLU term and the mixing term, we find that performance gains scale with the magnitude of the mixing matrix and vanish when the matrix is removed. The learned neuron directions concentrate in the subspace associated with the top 50 eigenvalues of the mixing matrix, suggesting that the mixing term governs the solution. Finally, a semi-non-negative matrix factorization (SNMF) baseline derived solely from the mixing matrix reproduces the qualitative loss profile and improves on prior baselines, though it does not match the trained model. These results suggest CC is not a suitable toy model of computation in superposition.

AI.tldr: CC does not realize computation in superposition; gains come from unintended label mixing via a noisy residual stream; removing mixing eliminates gains; an SNMF baseline capturing the mixing reproduces some loss behavior but cannot match the trained model, casting doubt on CC as a superposition toy model.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether the Compressed Computation (CC) toy model truly implements computation in superposition or if observed benefits are due to unintended mixing of labels through a residual stream.
AI.method: Decompose the objective into a ReLU term and a mixing term; analyze how performance scales with the magnitude of the mixing matrix; perform ablations that remove the mixing term; study the eigenstructure of the mixing matrix to see where learned directions lie; compare against a semi-non-negative matrix factorization (SNMF) baseline derived from the mixing matrix.
AI.result: Performance gains scale with the mixing-matrix magnitude and vanish when the mixing is removed. The learned neuron directions concentrate in the subspace spanned by the top 50 eigenvalues of the mixing matrix, indicating the mixing term governs the solution. An SNMF baseline derived from the mixing matrix reproduces the qualitative loss profile and outperforms prior baselines but does not match the trained model.
AI.conclusion: CC is not a suitable toy model of computation in superposition; the observed improvements are largely driven by an unintended mixing mechanism rather than genuine superposition-based computation.

title: Optimal Hidden-Target Learning for Online Inventory Optimization on General Convex Sets
summary:
Online inventory optimization (OIO) is online convex optimization with physical memory: inventory carryover makes the feasible action set depend on the past. A natural principle, used in stochastic inventory learning and recently in OIO under a single linear capacity constraint, is to maintain a hidden target chosen by an online learner and implement its projection onto the currently feasible order-up-to set. We prove that this simple principle is optimal for OIO on arbitrary bounded convex capacity sets. With online gradient descent as the base learner, the method improves the best known regret guarantee for OIO on general convex sets from inverse to inverse-square-root dependence on the common-demand probability, and we prove a matching lower bound. The same principle gives the first polylogarithmic regret guarantee for strongly convex losses and the first dynamic regret guarantee adapting to Euclidean path variation on general convex capacity sets.
  The analysis introduces a norm alignment principle: the right state variable is the distance from the hidden target to the feasible set, measured in the same norm as the projection. Under norm alignment, this distance evolves pathwise as a scalar queue, with target movement as arrival and common demand as service. This reduction to one-dimensional queue control resolves the state dependence and extends the guarantees to general convex capacity sets, beyond the reach of prior productwise approaches. Experiments on synthetic and real-world inventory data corroborate the theory.

AI.tldr: A general, optimal OIO algorithm using a hidden target projected onto the feasible set; introduces a norm-aligned queue interpretation to achieve optimal regret on general convex capacity sets, with polylog/polytime guarantees for strong convexity and dynamic settings.
AI.motivation: To design optimal online inventory optimization algorithms when the feasible action set changes with carryover, and to extend regret guarantees beyond productwise constraints to arbitrary bounded convex capacity sets.
AI.method: Maintain a hidden target and project onto the current feasible order-up-to set; base learner is online gradient descent; analyze via a norm-alignment principle where the distance from target to the set (in the projection norm) acts as a scalar queue with demand as service and target movement as arrivals.
AI.result: Proves optimality of the projection-on-target principle for general convex capacity sets; improves regret for OIO from inverse to inverse-square-root in common-demand probability and provides matching lower bounds; yields polylogarithmic regret for strongly convex losses and dynamic regret aligned with Euclidean path variation; extends guarantees beyond previous productwise approaches.
AI.conclusion: A unified, optimal framework for OIO on general convex capacity sets, grounded in norm-aligned distance dynamics, enabling strong theoretical guarantees and practical validation on synthetic and real data.

title: A Complexity Measure for Active Learning in Multi-group Mean Estimation
summary: We study a \emph{max-risk} objective for active learning in a multi-group mean estimation $d$-armed bandits: a learner adaptively allocates a budget of $T$ samples across $d$ groups to minimize the worst-case uncertainty index $\max_{k\in[d]}σ_k^2/n_k$, where $σ_k$ is the standard deviation of the distribution of arm $d$, and $n_k$ is the number of times arm $d$ is sampled. We develop a local minimax framework and prove the first general lower bound for this objective, valid for any finite-variance hypothesis class. The bound separates difficulty into three orthogonal factors: a \emph{budget} term, a \emph{heteroscedasticity} index measuring how unevenly the uncertainty is spread across arms, and a model-dependent complexity measure, the \emph{Variance Local Curvature} ($\mathrm{VLC}$), which captures how much information a local change of variance creates inside the hypothesis class. For smooth classes, the $\mathrm{VLC}$ is a reparametrization of a variance--Fisher information, with closed-form values for common families. Benchmarking against the strongest available upper bound shows near-optimality up to logarithmic factors in broad regimes, and pinpoints a systematic gap in highly heterogeneous instances. Our proof introduces two key ingredients: a loss-induced $\ell_1$ geometry on the decision space, and a representation-based instance generator that reduces hard-instance construction to an explicit random matrix calculation.

AI.tldr: Proposes a max-risk (worst-group-variance) objective for active learning in multi-group d-armed bandits, along with a local minimax lower bound and a three-factor difficulty decomposition (budget, heteroscedasticity, Variance Local Curvature). Introduces VLC as a model-dependent complexity; shows VLC links to variance-Fisher information for smooth classes; achieves near-optimal upper bounds up to log factors and identifies gaps in highly heterogeneous cases; proof hinges on loss-induced L1 geometry and a representation-based instance generator.
AI.motivation: Clarify fundamental limits of allocating sampling budget under a worst-case uncertainty objective across heterogeneous groups, to guide optimal adaptive sampling strategies in multi-group mean estimation.
AI.method: Develop a local minimax framework for the max-risk objective; derive a general lower bound valid for any finite-variance hypothesis class; define and analyze Variance Local Curvature (VLC); relate VLC to variance-Fisher information for smooth families; compare upper bounds to bound to assess optimality; introduce two proof ingredients: loss-induced L1 geometry on the decision space and a representation-based instance generator via random matrices.
AI.result: A lower bound that decomposes difficulty into budget, heteroscedasticity, and VLC; VLC reduces to variance-Fisher information for smooth classes with closed-form values; upper bounds are near-optimal up to logarithmic factors in broad regimes, with a systematic gap in highly heterogeneous instances; a near-complete characterization of the max-risk objective’s difficulty and guidance for algorithm design in such settings.
AI.conclusion: The VLC framework captures model-dependent complexity for worst-case uncertainty under budget constraints. The loss-induced geometry and representation-based instance generator provide general, reusable tools for proving bounds and constructing hard instances. The work lays groundwork for tighter bounds in heterogeneous regimes and extensions to broader objectives or non-smooth hypothesis classes.

title: Persona-Pruner: Sculpting Lightweight Models for Role-Playing
comment: 25 pages; ICML 2026; Code is available at https://github.com/jsu-kim/Persona-Pruner
summary: Language Models (LMs) have shown remarkable potential as role-playing chatbots, delivering consistent, stylized interactions when given a specification of a character or user persona. However, applying these capabilities to real-world applications (e.g., ecosystems with numerous NPCs interacting simultaneously) exposes a critical inefficiency due to the excessive computational cost. In this paper, we question the necessity of dedicating a full, generalist model to a single persona, hypothesizing that a specific character identity relies on only a fraction of the model's total capacity. We observe that naively pruning LMs often severely degrades the role-playing performance for a specific persona; it does not distinguish between redundant knowledge and essential character traits. We propose Persona-Pruner, a framework that sculpts a lightweight role-playing model by isolating persona-specific sub-networks from a single description. Our experiments consistently show that Persona-Pruner preserves role-playing performance substantially more effectively than existing state-of-the-art LLM pruning techniques, reducing the performance drop from the dense model by up to 93.8% over the strongest baseline on RoleBench in LLM-as-a-judge score, while still maintaining general LLM capabilities. Code is available at https://github.com/jsu-kim/Persona-Pruner.

AI.tldr: Persona-Pruner isolates persona-specific sub-networks from a single persona description to create a lightweight role-playing model. It preserves role-playing performance better than naive pruning, reducing performance drop by up to 93.8% compared to the strongest baseline on RoleBench, while maintaining general LLM capabilities; code available.
AI.motivation: Applications with many NPCs require running many personas efficiently; full generalist models are computationally wasteful for single-character tasks. The work posits that persona identity relies on only a fraction of model capacity and seeks to reduce compute without sacrificing role fidelity.
AI.method: Identify and prune persona-specific sub-networks guided by a single persona description to sculpt a lightweight role-playing model. The approach differentiates essential character traits from redundant knowledge, aiming to preserve role-playing ability under pruning.
AI.result: Empirically, Persona-Pruner preserves role-playing performance substantially better than existing state-of-the-art pruning methods; shows large reduction in performance drop (up to 93.8% on RoleBench in LLM-as-a-judge score) while keeping general LLM capabilities; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: A targeted sub-network pruning approach can achieve efficient, scalable role-playing models without sacrificing personality fidelity, addressing a key bottleneck in multi-agent LLM ecosystems.
